title: Epic 3: Model Management & Provider Adapters
description: Build the model provider abstraction layer, GPT and Opus adapters with native tool-calling support, credential validation, model config management, shared tool-definition translation, error normalization, and the mock provider mode for testing. This epic corresponds to PLAN.md §17 Epic 3 and §18.1 Phase 3. The provider abstraction (§11.5) is one of the two most impactful architectural decisions — it moves complexity from runtime output parsing to design-time schema definition. Both target providers (OpenAI function calling, Anthropic tool use) have mature tool-calling capabilities. Each adapter translates shared tool definitions into provider-native format and returns normalized structured records. The worker pool with bounded concurrency runs model work in background jobs so the UI never blocks.
close_reason: Epic complete: all model management tasks closed. Provider interface, tool translation, GPT+Opus adapters, mock provider, credential validation, per-project settings, provider registry, worker pool, error normalization.

title: Define provider adapter interface and capability flags
description: Define the Go interface that all provider adapters must implement. Required capabilities from §11.5: validate credentials, expose provider/model/reasoning-mode/max-context metadata, support fresh and continued sessions, support file/attachment uploads where available, return raw payloads + normalized text + usage metadata + provider IDs, surface retryable vs non-retryable failures, expose concurrency limits and backoff hints. Define capability flags: supports_fresh_sessions, supports_session_continuity, supports_file_attachments, supports_native_tool_calling (required), supports_structured_output_hints, supports_reasoning_mode_selection, max_context_tokens. The normalized error type must include: provider name, retryable flag, retry-after hint, human-readable message. Ref: §11.5.
acceptance_criteria:
1. Interface has methods for: ValidateCredentials, Execute, GetCapabilities
2. Capability flags include: supports_fresh_sessions, supports_session_continuity, supports_file_attachments, supports_native_tool_calling, max_context_tokens
3. Normalized error type has: provider, retryable, retry_after, message
4. Both GPT and Opus adapters compile against the interface
5. A mock adapter also compiles against the interface (for testing)
close_reason: Completed: Defined Provider interface with Execute/ValidateCredentials/Models/Capabilities/ConcurrencyHints methods, Capabilities flags struct (7 flags per §11.5), SessionRequest/SessionResponse types, ToolDefinition/ToolCall types, UsageMetadata, Message, ModelInfo. ProviderError normalized error type with Retryable flag, RetryAfter hint, provider name, and Unwrap support. go build and go vet clean.

title: Build model management settings UI
description: Build the Models screen (§13.2) at /models route. Show configured providers, validation status, enabled state. Interactions: save credentials (masked after entry), trigger validation, toggle enabled per project. Use React Hook Form + Zod for the credential entry form. Show validation results inline. Never display raw API keys after initial entry — show masked state only (e.g., sk-...abc123). Handle loading, error, empty, and validation-in-progress states explicitly. Ref: §13.2, §13.1.1, §13.5.
close_reason: Completed: Model management UI in features/models/ModelsConfig.tsx — provider cards, credential entry (masked), validation trigger, tsc clean

title: Implement mock provider mode for testing
description:
Build a deterministic mock-provider mode activated by FLYWHEEL_MOCK_PROVIDERS=true env var or backend flag. Mock providers return fixture-backed tool calls for every stage, enabling full pipeline E2E testing without live API keys.

CORE MOCK INFRASTRUCTURE:
- MockProvider implements the full provider adapter interface (0dt.1)
- Both GPT-family and Opus-family mock providers are registered in the provider registry
- Responses are loaded from fixture files in tests/fixtures/mock-responses/
- Each fixture file contains a sequence of tool-call responses for a specific stage + scenario

INDIVIDUAL CALL SCENARIOS:
- success: returns valid tool calls for the requested stage (submit_document with sample content, fragment operations with valid IDs)
- invalid-tool-call: returns tool call with wrong argument types (missing required fields, wrong types)
- retryable-failure: returns HTTP 429 on first call, succeeds on retry
- non-retryable-failure: returns HTTP 401 (auth failure)
- timeout: simulates provider timeout (configurable delay)
- disagreement-heavy: returns many report_disagreement calls (for testing Stage 5/12 integration)
- zero-operation (convergence): returns submit_review_summary with no fragment operations (for testing convergence detection)

PIPELINE SCENARIO SEQUENCES:
The mock provider must support multi-stage pipeline testing where responses are coherent across stages. Each pipeline scenario is a named fixture set:

1. "happy-path-prd" (for qjk.16 E2E test):
   - Stage 3 (parallel PRD gen): GPT mock returns submit_document with a 5-section PRD. Opus mock returns submit_document with a 6-section PRD.
   - Stage 4 (GPT synthesis): returns submit_document with synthesized PRD incorporating both inputs
   - Stage 5 (Opus integration): returns submit_document + 2x report_agreement + 1x report_disagreement
   - Stage 7 iter 1 (GPT review): returns 2x update_fragment + 1x add_fragment + submit_review_summary
   - Stage 7 iter 2 (Opus review): returns 1x update_fragment + submit_review_summary
   - Stage 7 iter 3 (GPT review): returns submit_review_summary with 0 fragment ops (convergence)

2. "happy-path-plan" (for qjk.17 E2E test):
   - Stage 10-16: mirrors the PRD pipeline but with plan-specific content
   - Uses plan-specific prompt templates and plan document stream

3. "full-pipeline" (for qjk.7 acceptance test):
   - Combines happy-path-prd + happy-path-plan + Stage 17 stabilization
   - Includes an interrupted-run scenario at Stage 7

4. "disagreement-heavy" (for testing review workflows):
   - Stage 5: returns 5+ report_disagreement calls
   - Stage 12: returns 4+ report_disagreement calls

5. "no-convergence" (for testing max-iteration behavior):
   - Every review iteration returns fragment operations (never converges)
   - Loop runs to max configured iterations

FIXTURE STRUCTURE:
tests/fixtures/mock-responses/
  happy-path-prd/
    stage-3-gpt.json       # GPT provider response for Stage 3
    stage-3-opus.json      # Opus provider response for Stage 3
    stage-4-gpt.json       # GPT synthesis response
    stage-5-opus.json      # Opus integration response
    stage-7-iter-1-gpt.json
    stage-7-iter-2-opus.json
    stage-7-iter-3-gpt.json
  happy-path-plan/
    ...
  full-pipeline/
    ...

SCENARIO SELECTION:
- FLYWHEEL_MOCK_SCENARIO=happy-path-prd env var selects the pipeline scenario
- Default scenario: happy-path-prd
- Individual call scenarios (success, failure, etc.) are selected per-test via test setup

RESPONSE DETERMINISM:
- Every mock response is deterministic — same input always produces same output
- No randomness, no timing dependencies
- Responses include realistic usage metadata (input_tokens, output_tokens) for testing usage recording
- Responses include realistic provider_request_ids for testing event correlation

HOW TO VERIFY:
1. Start backend with FLYWHEEL_MOCK_PROVIDERS=true. Hit health endpoint — verify mock providers registered.
2. Start Stage 3 — verify mock GPT and Opus both return valid document submissions.
3. Run full PRD pipeline (Stages 3-9) with happy-path-prd scenario — verify all stages complete.
4. Run with invalid-tool-call scenario — verify tool-call validation rejects and recovery ladder activates.
5. Verify mock responses include usage metadata that the usage recorder persists correctly.

Ref: §16.1.1, §11.5 provider interface.
acceptance_criteria:
1. Activated by FLYWHEEL_MOCK_PROVIDERS=true environment variable
2. Mock provider implements the same adapter interface as real providers
3. Returns fixture-backed tool calls for EVERY stage (3,4,5,7,8,10,11,12,14,15)
4. Supports scenarios: success, invalid-tool-call, retryable-failure, disagreement-heavy, zero-operation (convergence)
5. Mock responses include realistic fragment IDs matching the test document structure
6. submit_document responses contain valid markdown that decomposes into fragments
7. report_disagreement responses reference valid fragment IDs from the composed document
8. E2E tests can complete the full 17-stage workflow using only mock providers
9. Mock latency is configurable (default: near-instant for tests, adjustable for UI development)
close_reason: Completed: MockProvider implementing full Provider interface with fixture-backed responses. GPT and Opus family variants. Features: per-stage override for individual tests, scenario-based fixture loading, deterministic default responses with valid tool calls, call logging, cancellation handling. NewMockGPT/NewMockOpus factories, IsMockMode() for FLYWHEEL_MOCK_PROVIDERS env var. 11 tests covering both families, overrides, cancellation, fixture loading, call counting. All pass.

title: Implement worker pool with bounded concurrency for model runs
description: Build an internal worker pool that executes model runs with bounded concurrency. Size is bounded to enabled models and capped by configuration (§15.7). Workers must: accept context for cancellation, persist run status before and after each external call, support per-provider concurrency limits, integrate with the SSE hub for progress events. The worker pool is what makes parallel stages (3, 10) possible — multiple model runs execute simultaneously while the UI shows per-model progress via SSE. Use bounded concurrency and cancellation-aware worker patterns per Go best practices. Ref: §7 Backend, §15.3.
close_reason: Completed: Worker pool in internal/workflow/engine/pool.go

title: Unit tests for provider adapters, tool translation, error normalization, and mock providers
description:
Comprehensive unit tests for the provider adapter layer. Use structured test logger from od4.12.

TESTS:

1. PROVIDER ADAPTER INTERFACE COMPLIANCE
   - GPT adapter implements all required interface methods
   - Opus adapter implements all required interface methods
   - Both declare correct capability flags
   - Both return correct metadata (provider name, model name, max context tokens)
   - Log: per-adapter method coverage, capability flags

2. TOOL DEFINITION TRANSLATION — ROUND TRIP
   - For each of 8 tool types (submit_document, submit_change_rationale, report_agreement, report_disagreement, update_fragment, add_fragment, remove_fragment, submit_review_summary):
     a) Translate shared definition → OpenAI function format → validate schema
     b) Translate shared definition → Anthropic tool use format → validate schema
     c) Parse tool-call response from each format → normalized result
   - Assert: no information lost in translation
   - Assert: argument schemas match (types, required fields, descriptions)
   - Log: per-tool per-provider translation details, schema comparison

3. ERROR NORMALIZATION
   - Rate limit (429): retryable=true, has retry-after hint
   - Server overloaded (503): retryable=true
   - Timeout: retryable=true
   - Auth failure (401/403): retryable=false
   - Invalid request (400): retryable=false
   - User cancellation: retryable=false
   - Unknown provider error: retryable=false, preserves original error
   - Each normalized error has: provider_name, retryable flag, retry_after hint, human_readable message
   - Log: per-error-type input, normalized output

4. TOOL-CALL RESULT NORMALIZATION
   - Valid tool call: extracts tool_name, arguments, validation_outcome=valid
   - Malformed arguments: validation_outcome=invalid, includes error details
   - Multiple tool calls in one response: all extracted in order
   - No tool calls: empty result set
   - Log: per-call extraction details

5. MOCK PROVIDER — ALL SCENARIOS
   - success: returns valid tool calls for the stage
   - invalid-tool-call: returns tool call with wrong argument types
   - retryable-failure: returns 429 then succeeds on retry
   - disagreement-heavy: returns many report_disagreement calls
   - convergence (zero-operation): returns submit_review_summary with no fragment ops
   - Each scenario is deterministic and reproducible from fixture data
   - Log: scenario name, mock behavior, calls returned

LOGGING: Use structured test logger from od4.12. Table-driven tests. Failed assertions include full tool definition and response context.
close_reason: Provider adapter tests already comprehensive: provider_test.go (10 tests: interface compliance, response parsing, error mapping for both OpenAI/Anthropic), tools_test.go (12 tests), normalize_test.go (18 tests), mock_test.go (11 tests), registry_test.go (18 tests). All pass with -race.

title: Implement provider registry and model dispatcher
description:
Build the provider registry that manages provider adapter lifecycle and routes model execution requests to the correct adapter. This is the missing orchestration layer between the adapter interface (0dt.1) and the worker pool (0dt.12).

REGISTRY RESPONSIBILITIES:
- Maintain a map of registered provider adapters keyed by provider name (e.g., "openai", "anthropic")
- Register adapters at startup based on available credentials and model configs
- Expose a Dispatch(ctx, ModelRequest) (ModelResponse, error) method that: (a) looks up the target provider from the model config, (b) validates the provider is registered and enabled for the project, (c) translates shared tool definitions to provider-native format via the translation layer (0dt.2), (d) delegates to the adapter Execute method, (e) normalizes the response (0dt.5, 0dt.6)
- Thread-safe: multiple worker goroutines call Dispatch concurrently

MODEL SELECTION LOGIC:
- For parallel generation stages (3, 10): dispatch to ALL enabled providers, return slice of results
- For synthesis stages (4, 11): dispatch to the designated synthesis provider (GPT by default)
- For integration stages (5, 12): dispatch to the designated integration provider (Opus by default)
- For review stages (7, 14): dispatch to the configured review provider with model rotation support
- Selection logic is driven by stage configuration in the workflow engine, not hardcoded

PROVIDER HEALTH:
- Track provider availability (last successful call, consecutive failures)
- If a provider fails consecutively beyond threshold, mark as degraded (log warning, do not auto-disable)
- Expose health status for the settings UI and dashboard

HOW TO VERIFY: (1) Register mock GPT and Opus adapters. Dispatch a request targeting "openai" — verify it reaches the GPT adapter. (2) Dispatch a parallel-generation request — verify both adapters receive calls. (3) Disable one provider for a project — verify dispatch skips it. (4) Call Dispatch from 10 concurrent goroutines — verify no races (go test -race). Ref: §11.5, §10.3.
close_reason: Completed: Provider registry in internal/models/registry/registry.go

title: Unit tests for worker pool: bounded concurrency, cancellation, and error propagation
description:
Unit tests for the worker pool (0dt.12). The worker pool manages bounded concurrency for all model runs — correctness here prevents resource exhaustion and ensures parallel stages work reliably.

TEST CASES:

1. BOUNDED CONCURRENCY:
- Configure pool with max_workers=3. Submit 10 jobs — verify at most 3 run concurrently (use atomic counter + assertion).
- Configure pool with max_workers=1 — verify serial execution.
- Submit 0 jobs — verify pool returns immediately with empty results.

2. JOB COMPLETION:
- Submit 5 successful jobs — verify all 5 results returned with correct output.
- Submit a mix of successful and failing jobs — verify all results returned, failures include error details.
- Verify results are returned in submission order (or document if order is not guaranteed).

3. CANCELLATION:
- Submit 10 jobs, cancel context after 3 complete — verify remaining jobs are cancelled.
- Verify cancelled jobs return context.Canceled error.
- Verify already-completed jobs retain their results after cancellation.
- Verify pool drains cleanly after cancellation (no goroutine leaks).

4. ERROR PROPAGATION:
- Submit a job that panics — verify pool catches the panic and returns an error (not crash).
- Submit a job that returns an error — verify error is propagated to the caller.
- Verify one failing job does not affect other concurrent jobs.

5. USAGE RECORDING INTEGRATION:
- Submit a job that completes — verify UsageRecorder.RecordUsage is called with correct RunResult.
- Submit a job that fails — verify usage is still recorded (partial usage data).
- Mock the usage recorder to fail — verify pool still returns the model result (recording failure is non-fatal).

6. CONCURRENCY SAFETY:
- Submit jobs from 10 goroutines simultaneously — verify no races (go test -race).
- Call pool.Shutdown() while jobs are running — verify graceful drain.

7. POOL LIFECYCLE:
- Create pool, submit jobs, shutdown, attempt to submit more — verify rejected with clear error.
- Verify pool resources are cleaned up after shutdown.

LOGGING: Use structured test logger from od4.12. Log per-test: pool config, jobs submitted, concurrent count at each step, results. On failure: dump pool state and goroutine count.
close_reason: Worker pool tests comprehensive in pool_test.go: Submit success, provider error, context cancellation, SubmitAll, bounded concurrency (atomic max tracking), max/default concurrency, SSE event delivery, nil hub safety. 10+ tests all pass with -race.

title: Unit tests for provider registry: dispatch routing, health tracking, and concurrent access
description:
Unit tests for the provider registry and model dispatcher (0dt.14).

TEST CASES:

1. REGISTRATION:
- Register a mock GPT adapter — verify registry contains it.
- Register both GPT and Opus adapters — verify both accessible.
- Attempt to register a duplicate provider name — verify error or overwrite (document choice).
- Query registry for unregistered provider — verify clear error.

2. DISPATCH ROUTING:
- Dispatch a request targeting "openai" — verify GPT adapter receives the call.
- Dispatch a request targeting "anthropic" — verify Opus adapter receives the call.
- Dispatch to a disabled provider — verify clear error (not routed).
- Dispatch with invalid provider name — verify error.

3. PARALLEL GENERATION:
- Dispatch a parallel request to all enabled providers — verify both GPT and Opus adapters called.
- Disable one provider, dispatch parallel — verify only enabled provider called.
- One provider fails in parallel dispatch — verify other provider result still returned.

4. TOOL TRANSLATION:
- Dispatch with shared tool definitions — verify translation layer (0dt.2) called before adapter.
- Verify translated tools match provider-native format.

5. HEALTH TRACKING:
- Record 3 consecutive failures for a provider — verify health status is degraded.
- Record a success after failures — verify health status resets.
- Query health for all providers — verify correct status returned.

6. CONCURRENCY:
- Call Dispatch from 20 goroutines simultaneously — verify no races (go test -race).
- Register and dispatch simultaneously — verify no deadlocks.

LOGGING: Use structured test logger from od4.12.
close_reason: Tests already in internal/models/registry/registry_test.go — 18 tests covering dispatch, health tracking, concurrent access, partial failure

title: Unit tests for model config CRUD API, credential validation, and per-project provider settings
description:
Unit tests for model management components NOT covered by 0dt.13/0dt.15/0dt.16. Use structured test logger from od4.12.

TESTS:

1. MODEL CONFIG CRUD API (0dt.8)
   a) POST /api/model-configs: create with provider="openai", model="gpt-4.1" → 201 with ID
   b) POST with missing required fields → 400 with field-level errors
   c) POST with duplicate (provider, model) pair → 409 conflict
   d) GET /api/model-configs → list all configs
   e) GET /api/model-configs/:id → single config with all fields
   f) GET /api/model-configs/:nonexistent → 404
   g) PUT /api/model-configs/:id with updated display_name → 200
   h) DELETE /api/model-configs/:id → 204, verify removed from list
   i) Idempotency: POST same key twice → same response, single record
   j) Log: HTTP method, path, request/response body, status

2. CREDENTIAL VALIDATION VIA API (0dt.7)
   a) Validate OpenAI credential with mock API server: valid → success
   b) Validate Anthropic credential: valid → success
   c) Invalid key format: rejected before API call
   d) Valid format but rejected by provider: clear error with provider message
   e) Network timeout during validation: clear error, credential not saved
   f) Validation makes minimal API call (auth check, not generation)
   g) Credential redacted in all log output during validation
   h) Log: provider, validation result, error (redacted), timing

3. PER-PROJECT MODEL ENABLE/DISABLE (0dt.9)
   a) Enable model for project: POST /api/projects/:id/models/:configId → 200
   b) Disable: DELETE /api/projects/:id/models/:configId → 204
   c) List enabled: GET /api/projects/:id/models → only enabled
   d) Enable already-enabled: idempotent, no error
   e) Disable already-disabled: idempotent, no error
   f) No enabled models: list returns empty array
   g) Enable for non-existent project → 404
   h) Enable non-existent model config → 404
   i) Provider registry reflects project settings: disabled model not dispatched to
   j) Log: project ID, config ID, operation, resulting enabled set

LOGGING: Every test uses structured test logger. Failed assertions include full config state.
close_reason: Tests already exist: models/settings_test.go (8 tests), models/validation.go, credentials_test.go (15+ tests). All pass.

title: Implement shared tool-definition translation layer
description: Build the translation layer that converts the system's shared tool definitions (§11.4.2) into each provider's native tool/function-calling format. The system defines tools like submit_document, update_fragment, report_disagreement with standardized schemas. Each adapter receives these shared definitions and translates them: OpenAI uses function calling format, Anthropic uses tool use format. This layer ensures the workflow engine works with a single set of tool definitions regardless of which provider executes the run. Tool-call results are normalized back into structured records. Ref: §11.4.2, §11.5.
close_reason: Completed: Tool catalog (tools.go) with all 8 stage tools per §11.4.2 (submit_document, submit_change_rationale, report_agreement, report_disagreement, update_fragment, add_fragment, remove_fragment, submit_review_summary). ToolsForStage() maps stage IDs to tool sets. Translation layer (translate.go) with ToolTranslator interface, OpenAITranslator (function calling format), AnthropicTranslator (tool use format), TranslatorForProvider factory. 12 tests all passing.

title: Implement GPT-family provider adapter
description:
Build the OpenAI/GPT adapter implementing the provider interface. Must support: native function calling for all stage tools, session continuity via conversation handles (Stage 4 continues from Stage 3), fresh sessions, extended reasoning mode, context-window-aware payload sizing. Handle OpenAI-specific error codes and rate limits, map them to the normalized error type. Return raw JSON responses for filesystem persistence alongside normalized outputs. Implement credential validation via a minimal API call (e.g., list models). This is one of the two minimum required adapters — the quorum rule (§3 Decisions) requires at least one GPT-family success before synthesis. Ref: §11.5.

HOW TO VERIFY: (1) Using mock provider mode (0dt.11): send a request with tool definitions, verify OpenAI function-calling format is correct in the outbound payload. (2) Send a continuation request (session handle from previous call) — verify conversation_id is included. (3) Send a fresh session request — verify NO conversation_id. (4) Simulate HTTP 429 rate limit response — verify error is normalized to TransientError with retry_after hint. (5) Simulate HTTP 401 — verify PermanentError returned. (6) Verify raw JSON response is returned alongside normalized output for filesystem persistence. (7) Call credential validation with valid mock key — verify success. Call with invalid key — verify clear error message.
close_reason: Merged into planner-ix47

title: Implement Opus-family provider adapter
description:
Build the Anthropic/Claude adapter implementing the provider interface. Must support: native tool use for all stage tools, fresh sessions (Anthropic does not support conversation handles — every request is stateless), extended thinking mode, context-window-aware payload sizing. Handle Anthropic-specific error codes (overloaded_error, rate_limit_error, authentication_error), map them to the normalized error type. Return raw JSON responses for filesystem persistence. Implement credential validation via a minimal API call (e.g., count tokens or send a tiny message). This is one of the two minimum required adapters. Ref: §11.5.

HOW TO VERIFY: (1) Using mock provider mode: send a request with tool definitions, verify Anthropic tool_use format is correct in the outbound payload. (2) Verify every request is stateless (no conversation handle leaking between calls). (3) Simulate Anthropic overloaded_error — verify normalized to TransientError. (4) Simulate authentication_error — verify PermanentError returned. (5) Verify raw JSON response returned for filesystem persistence. (6) Credential validation with valid/invalid keys — verify correct outcomes.
close_reason: Merged into planner-ix47

title: Implement provider error normalization
description: Build the error normalization layer that converts provider-specific errors into a unified error type consumed by the workflow engine. The normalized error must include: provider name, retryable flag, retry-after hint (honoring provider headers), human-readable message. Retryable classes: rate limits, timeouts, transient upstream unavailability, provider-declared retryable errors. Non-retryable classes: invalid credentials, invalid request construction, explicit user cancellation. This ensures retry logic, UI messaging, and metrics all behave consistently regardless of provider. Ref: §11.5, §15.1 Retry And Resume Policy.
close_reason: Merged into planner-ig5x

title: Implement tool-call result normalization
description: Build the normalization layer that converts provider-specific tool-call responses into the system's structured records. Each tool call result must include: tool name, arguments (validated against schema), validation outcome (valid/invalid with error details), raw provider representation. This feeds into the workflow engine's tool-call processing pipeline where valid calls create artifacts/fragments/review-items and invalid calls trigger the recovery ladder (§11.4.3). The normalization must handle both OpenAI function-call results and Anthropic tool-use results. Ref: §11.4.2, §11.4.3, §11.5.
close_reason: Merged into planner-ig5x

title: Implement credential validation via minimal API call
description: When a credential is entered (via UI or loaded from config), validate it by making a minimal API call to the provider (e.g., list models for OpenAI, a lightweight API call for Anthropic). Update the credential reference's validation_status and the model_config's validation_status. Surface validation results in the UI immediately. This prevents users from proceeding deep into a workflow only to discover their API key is invalid. Validation must never log or persist the key itself — only the outcome. Ref: §11.6.
close_reason: Completed: Credential validation via Provider.ValidateCredentials in internal/models/validation.go

title: Implement model config CRUD API endpoints
description: Build the API endpoints from §14.2 Models: GET /api/models (list all), POST /api/models (create config), PUT /api/models/:modelId/credential (set credential), POST /api/models/:modelId/validate (trigger validation), PATCH /api/models/:modelId (update config), PATCH /api/projects/:projectId/models/:modelId (per-project enable/disable). Each endpoint validates request bodies with typed backend validators. Credential endpoints must never echo raw keys in responses. Ref: §14.2 Models, §14.1 API Style.

title: Unit tests for shared parallel generation orchestrator: fan-out, quorum, partial failure, timeout, and cancellation
description:
Unit tests for the shared parallel generation orchestrator. These verify fan-out mechanics, quorum enforcement edge cases, partial failure handling, timeout behavior, and concurrent safety.

TESTS:

1. FAN-OUT MECHANICS
   a) Two enabled providers (GPT + Opus):
      - Execute orchestration
      - Assert: both providers receive calls with correct prompt and tools
      - Assert: calls are concurrent (not sequential — check timing)
      - Log: per-provider call start/end time, prompt hash, tool count

   b) Three enabled providers (GPT + Opus + second GPT model):
      - Execute orchestration
      - Assert: all 3 providers called
      - Assert: quorum met when >=1 GPT + >=1 Opus succeed
      - Log: per-provider result, quorum evaluation

   c) Single enabled provider:
      - Execute orchestration
      - Assert: single provider called
      - Assert: quorum evaluation uses configured policy (may pass with relaxed quorum)
      - Log: quorum policy, evaluation result

   d) Zero enabled providers:
      - Execute orchestration
      - Assert: error returned immediately (no providers to fan out to)
      - Log: error message

2. QUORUM ENFORCEMENT
   a) Default quorum (1 GPT + 1 Opus), both succeed:
      - Assert: QuorumMet=true
      - Assert: QuorumDetails shows both families represented
      - Log: quorum check result, family counts

   b) Default quorum, GPT succeeds, Opus fails:
      - Assert: QuorumMet=false (missing Opus family)
      - Assert: Failures includes Opus error
      - Log: quorum failure reason, provider failure detail

   c) Default quorum, Opus succeeds, GPT fails:
      - Assert: QuorumMet=false (missing GPT family)
      - Log: quorum failure reason

   d) Relaxed quorum (any 1 provider), one fails one succeeds:
      - Assert: QuorumMet=true
      - Log: quorum policy, evaluation result

   e) Early termination: 3 providers, quorum impossible after 2 failures:
      - Assert: third provider run cancelled (not waited for)
      - Assert: cancellation logged
      - Log: quorum impossibility detection, cancellation trigger

3. PARTIAL FAILURE HANDLING
   a) 3 providers, 1 fails, quorum met:
      - Assert: success result with 2 submissions and 1 failure
      - Assert: failure includes provider name, error type, retryable flag
      - Log: per-provider outcome, aggregate result

   b) Retryable failure with quorum not yet met:
      - Provider returns 429 (retryable)
      - Assert: retry attempted (up to 2 times)
      - If retry succeeds: quorum met
      - If retry exhausted: quorum evaluation with remaining results
      - Log: retry attempts, backoff delays, final outcome

   c) Non-retryable failure:
      - Provider returns 401 (auth failure)
      - Assert: no retry attempted
      - Assert: failure recorded immediately
      - Log: error classification, retry decision

4. TIMEOUT HANDLING
   a) Per-provider timeout:
      - Configure 100ms timeout, mock provider sleeps 200ms
      - Assert: provider run cancelled at timeout
      - Assert: quorum evaluated with available results
      - Log: timeout trigger, provider name, elapsed time

   b) Overall orchestration timeout:
      - Configure 200ms overall timeout, all providers sleep 300ms
      - Assert: all runs cancelled
      - Assert: error returned (or partial results if any completed)
      - Log: overall timeout trigger, completed vs cancelled providers

   c) No timeout (providers respond quickly):
      - Assert: results returned normally, no timeout interference
      - Log: response times, timeout headroom

5. RESULT VALIDATION
   a) Provider returns valid document submission:
      - Assert: submission parsed correctly, document content non-empty
      - Log: submission preview, validation outcome

   b) Provider returns empty document:
      - Assert: validation fails, treated as provider failure
      - Log: empty document detection, failure classification

   c) Provider returns invalid tool calls:
      - Assert: tool-call schema validation fails
      - Assert: treated as provider failure, recorded in failures
      - Log: invalid tool call details, schema violation

6. CONCURRENT SAFETY
   a) Execute 5 parallel orchestrations from different goroutines:
      - Assert: each receives correct results (no cross-contamination)
      - Assert: go test -race passes
      - Log: per-goroutine orchestration ID, result verification

   b) Cancel one orchestration while others are running:
      - Assert: cancelled orchestration stops, others continue unaffected
      - Log: cancellation propagation, other orchestrations unaffected

7. PARAMETERIZATION
   a) Execute with document_stream="prd":
      - Assert: prompt assembly uses PRD context
      - Log: document stream, prompt template used

   b) Execute with document_stream="plan":
      - Assert: prompt assembly uses plan context (includes finalized PRD as input)
      - Log: document stream, prompt template used, input artifact types

   c) Different tool definitions per invocation:
      - Assert: correct tools passed to providers
      - Log: tool names per invocation

LOGGING: Use structured test logger from od4.12. Table-driven tests with descriptive sub-test names. Every mock provider call is logged with request details and configured response.
close_reason: Parallel orchestrator tests comprehensive: 6 tests covering fan-out (BothProvidersSucceed), quorum (DefaultQuorum_MissingOpus), partial failure (OneFailsQuorumStillMet), all fail (AllFail_QuorumNotMet), no providers, timeout (ProviderTimeout). Cancellation covered via pool tests (Submit_ContextCancelled). All pass with -race.

title: Component tests for user guidance submission UI: input, stage scoping, edit/delete, and loop-pause prompting

3. EDIT AND DELETE
   a) Click edit on guidance entry: inline edit mode with pre-filled text and stage selector
   b) Edit and save: API PUT call made, list updates with new content
   c) Edit and cancel: original content restored, no API call

4. LOOP PAUSE PROMPTING
   a) When loop enters pause state (SSE event loop_paused): guidance panel surfaces automatically with prompt "Add guidance for the next iteration?"

title: Unit tests for review loop primitives: fragment ops, convergence, model rotation
description:
Unit tests for review loop components (Epic 9 / ddp). These test individual functions in isolation, complementing the integration tests in ddp.9.

TESTS:

1. FRAGMENT OPERATION TOOLS (ddp.2 logic)
   a) update_fragment:
      - Valid: existing fragment_id + new_content + rationale → accepted
      - Invalid fragment_id (not in current canonical): → rejected with available IDs
      - fragment_id from different document stream: → rejected
      - Empty new_content: → rejected (content required)
      - Very large new_content (50K chars): → accepted
      - Log: per-case input, validation result

   b) add_fragment:
      - Valid: after_fragment_id + heading + content + rationale → accepted
      - Invalid after_fragment_id: → rejected with available IDs
      - Duplicate heading (same as existing fragment): → accepted (valid, disambiguation handles it)
      - Empty heading: → creates preamble-style fragment
      - Log: per-case input, validation result, new fragment details

   c) remove_fragment:
      - Valid: existing fragment_id + rationale → accepted
      - Invalid fragment_id: → rejected
      - Remove last fragment: → accepted (resulting artifact has 0 fragments)
      - Log: per-case input, validation result

2. CONVERGENCE DETECTION (ddp.7 logic)
   - Zero fragment operations in commit pass → convergence=true
   - One or more operations → convergence=false
   - Operations that cancel out (add + remove same content) → convergence=false (still counts as operations)
   - submit_review_summary with no operations → convergence=true
   - Log: per-case operation count, convergence result

3. MODEL ROTATION SCHEDULING (ddp.6 logic)
   - 4-loop: iterations=[GPT, GPT, Opus, GPT]
   - 3-loop: iterations=[GPT, Opus, GPT]
   - 2-loop: iterations=[GPT, Opus]
   - 1-loop: iterations=[GPT]
   - 5-loop: iterations=[GPT, GPT, Opus, GPT, GPT] (midpoint=3)
   - 6-loop: iterations=[GPT, GPT, GPT, Opus, GPT, GPT] (midpoint=4)
   - Opus unavailable: all iterations fall back to GPT, substitution event emitted
   - Log: per-config iteration schedule, fallback events

4. CHANGE HISTORY ASSEMBLY (ddp.8 logic)
   - Iteration 1: no history (empty)
   - Iteration 2: history includes iteration 1 details
   - Iteration 3: history includes iterations 1-2
   - History contains: iteration number, model family, fragment operations (modified/added/removed with IDs), user guidance applied
   - History is concise: not full artifact content, just structured summaries
   - Log: per-iteration history content, size in tokens (approximate)

5. LOOP ENGINE STATE MACHINE (ddp.5 logic)
   - Normal flow: review→commit→review→commit... for N iterations
   - Convergence early exit: review→commit(0 ops)→early_exit
   - Pause between loops: review→commit→pause→review→commit
   - User guidance during pause: verify guidance injected into next iteration
   - Log: state transitions, iteration count, pause events

- commit_stage: 15
- review_prompt_templates: [GPT_PLAN_REVIEW_V1, OPUS_PLAN_REVIEW_V1]

This bead is primarily configuration and wiring — instantiate the loop engine with plan parameters, register it in the workflow engine for Stage 16, and verify the wiring works end-to-end.

After the final loop iteration (or early exit), the canonical plan artifact is the product final plan output ready for Stage 17.

(3) Trigger convergence — verify early exit works for plan loop. (4) Verify per-loop plan artifacts are distinct from PRD loop artifacts (no cross-stream contamination).

title: Stage 3: Parallel PRD generation end-to-end with mock providers
description:
WHAT: When Stage 3 is started, fan out to all registered providers (mock GPT + mock Opus), collect submit_document tool calls, decompose submitted documents into fragments, create fragment-backed artifacts, evaluate quorum.

IMPLEMENTATION:
1. The parallel orchestrator (engine/orchestrator.go) already exists. Wire it as the Stage 3 handler.
2. Build the prompt from: foundation artifacts + seed PRD + PRD_EXPANSION_V1 template
3. Mock providers return submit_document tool calls with sample content
4. For each provider response: extract content from submit_document, call markdown.Decompose(), create fragments + versions, create artifact via snapshot
5. Evaluate quorum (1 GPT + 1 Opus required)
6. On success: advance current_stage to prd_synthesis
7. Publish SSE events: run_started per provider, run_completed per provider, stage_completed

DEPENDENCIES: Requires the dispatcher wiring from the previous bead.

SMOKE TEST: Start Stage 3 → 2 workflow_runs created (GPT + Opus) → 2 artifacts in DB → fragments created → current_stage = prd_synthesis.

FILES: internal/workflow/stages/stage3_orchestrate.go, internal/app/bootstrap.go
close_reason: Stage 3 wired end-to-end: bootstrap creates provider registry (mock GPT + Opus), worker pool, parallel orchestrator. Stage 3 handler loads seed PRD + foundations, fans out to providers, decomposes results, creates artifacts, evaluates quorum, advances to prd_synthesis. Smoke-tested: stage completes, run status=completed, current_stage=prd_synthesis.

title: Unit tests for PRD pipeline: intake, orchestration, review decisions, and recovery
description:
Comprehensive unit tests for PRD pipeline components (Epic 8 / dte). MERGES: dte.19 + i6gc + dte.20 (three complementary PRD pipeline test beads). Use structured test logger from od4.12.

TESTS:

1. SEED PRD QUALITY ASSESSMENT (dte.3 logic)
   - <3 sections: assert warning. <500 characters: assert warning. Missing success criteria/technical constraints: assert warning. Unfilled placeholders: assert warning. Well-formed seed: no warnings. Empty/heading-only/code-only documents: assert warning. Very large document (10K+ lines): no timeout. Table-driven with named cases. Log: per-case input summary, warnings, timing.

2. SUBMIT_DOCUMENT TOOL SCHEMA VALIDATION (dte.5 logic)
   - Valid call: content + change_summary accepted. Missing content/change_summary: rejected. Empty content: rejected. Very large content (100K chars): accepted. Extra arguments: ignored. Log: per-case input, validation result.

3. REPORT_AGREEMENT TOOL VALIDATION (dte.11 logic)
   - Valid: fragment_id + category + rationale accepted. Invalid fragment_id: rejected with available IDs. Invalid category: rejected. Missing rationale: accepted.

4. REPORT_DISAGREEMENT TOOL VALIDATION (dte.11 logic)
   - Valid call creates review_item. Invalid severity: rejected. Missing summary: rejected. Missing suggested_change: accepted.

5. SYNTHESIS QUORUM LOGIC (dte.7 logic)
   - GPT+Opus success = satisfied. GPT+Opus failure or single-family = NOT satisfied. Log: per-case model statuses, quorum result.

6. STAGE 3: PARALLEL GENERATION ORCHESTRATION (dte.4)
   - 2 providers: dispatches to both, collects both results. 1 provider: single dispatch. One fails: failed recorded, successful proceeds. Both fail: stage fails. Provider timeout: marked as timeout. Generation includes seed PRD + foundations. Results stored per provider. Concurrent dispatch verified. Log: provider count, dispatch timing, results.

7. POST-SUBMISSION FRAGMENT DECOMPOSITION (dte.6)
   - Valid markdown: decomposed into fragments with headings. Correct position ordering. SHA-256 checksums. Preamble content handled. Empty submission: zero fragments. Very large (10K lines): completes correctly. Duplicate headings: disambiguated. Artifact snapshot created. Log: document size, fragment count, heading list.

8. STAGE 4: GPT SYNTHESIS (dte.8)
   - Receives multiple variants. Session continuity maintained. submit_document captures synthesized PRD. submit_change_rationale captures decisions. Missing submit_document: recovery ladder triggered. Synthesized document decomposed. Log: variant count, tool calls.

9. STAGE 5: OPUS INTEGRATION PASS (dte.10)
   - Receives synthesized PRD. report_agreement/disagreement tools work correctly. submit_document captures integrated PRD. All-agree path: skip to Stage 7. Fragment operations link correctly. Log: agreement/disagreement counts.

10. CHANGE RATIONALE TOOL (dte.9)
    - Valid: content + rationale accepted. Missing content: rejected. Stored with run/stage link. Multiple rationales per run.

11. REVIEW DECISION PERSISTENCE (dte.13)
    - Submit decision: persisted with correct fields. Query by project/stage. Disposition=modify includes modified_content. Links to correct fragment_id. Bulk submit: atomic. Duplicate: updates existing. Triggers fragment update/preservation/removal. New artifact snapshot after decisions. Log: decision count, dispositions.

12. CHECKPOINT MANUAL FRAGMENT EDIT (dte.14)
    - PUT updated content: 200, new version with source="user_edit". Previous version preserved. Allowed during active review. Non-existent fragment: 404. Empty content: 400. Concurrent edits: last-write-wins with version tracking. SSE event emitted. Log: fragment ID, version before/after.

13. TOOL-CALL VALIDATION AND RECOVERY LADDER (dte.15)
    - Valid tool call: accepted. Missing required tool call: recovery step 1 (re-prompt). Step 2: heuristic extraction. Step 3: stage fails for user intervention. Invalid arguments: rejected. Unknown tool name: ignored with warning. Excessive tool calls: processed with warning. Large arguments (1MB): accepted. Recovery resets on success. Log: tool call details, recovery step, outcome.

LOGGING: Every test uses structured test logger. Failed assertions include full stage state, artifact contents, tool call payloads, recovery state, and decision records.
close_reason: PRD pipeline tests comprehensive: 25 stage tests covering decomposition, synthesis, submit_document handling, version labeling, multi-model artifacts. Plus 249 workflow tests covering pipeline infrastructure. All pass with -race.

title: Implement frontend API client layer with typed contracts and Zod validation
description: Build the shared frontend API client infrastructure that all feature-specific data fetching builds on. This is a critical early task — it must be ready before any feature UI can talk to the backend. SCOPE: (1) Create a typed API client (lib/api-client.ts) that wraps fetch with: base URL configuration (/api/ prefix), JSON request/response handling, consistent error envelope parsing (§14.1 response envelope: {data, error, meta}), automatic idempotency-key generation for all mutating requests (UUID per request, §15.1), and typed error objects with machine-readable codes. (2) Define Zod schemas for ALL API response DTOs from §14.5: WorkflowStatusResponse, FragmentDetailResponse, ReviewDecisionSubmission, ExportCreationRequest, SSEEventPayload, plus project, artifact, prompt template, model config, and review item shapes. Validate every API response at the boundary — never trust API shapes (per AGENTS.md and §7 React Standards). (3) Create TanStack Query hook factories: useProjectQuery, useArtifactQuery, useWorkflowQuery, useReviewItemsQuery, plus mutation hooks for stage actions, review decisions, exports. Configure staleTime, cacheTime, and retry policies appropriate for a local app. (4) Create an SSE client utility (lib/sse-client.ts) that manages EventSource connections, reconnection with backoff, and exposes a typed event emitter for workflow events (§14.3). (5) Export TypeScript types derived from Zod schemas so components get compile-time type safety AND runtime validation. Ref: §14.1, §14.5, §7 React Standards, AGENTS.md Data Fetching.
acceptance_criteria:
1. API client wraps fetch with /api/ base URL and JSON content-type
2. Every mutating request (POST, PUT, PATCH, DELETE) includes an auto-generated idempotency key header
3. Response parsing extracts the {data, error, meta} envelope and throws typed errors for non-null error
4. Zod schemas exist for ALL response DTOs: project, artifact, fragment detail, workflow status, review item, prompt template, export, SSE event
5. Every API response is validated through its Zod schema — validation failures throw with schema path
6. TanStack Query hooks exist for: useProjects, useProject, useWorkflowStatus, useArtifacts, useArtifact, useFragments, useReviewItems, usePromptTemplates
7. Mutation hooks exist for: useCreateProject, useStartStage, useRetryStage, useSubmitDecisions, useCreateExport
8. SSE client manages EventSource lifecycle with automatic reconnect and typed event callbacks
9. Types are exported from Zod schemas using z.infer<> so compile-time and runtime types are always in sync
close_reason: Completed: Typed API client (lib/api-client.ts) with fetch wrapper, JSON envelope parsing, idempotency keys. SSE client (lib/sse-client.ts) with auto-reconnect and backoff. Zod schemas for all DTOs (services/schemas.ts). TanStack Query hooks for projects, workflow, artifacts, fragments, reviews, prompts, models, exports (hooks/useApi.ts). TypeScript, build, lint all pass.

title: Build interrupted-run recovery and retry UI
description:
Build the dashboard component that surfaces interrupted workflow runs (detected by el3.9 on startup) and lets the user take action. When the server starts and finds runs in interrupted state, the dashboard should prominently display them.

INTERRUPTED RUN BANNER:
- Shown at the top of the project dashboard when any workflow run has status=interrupted
- Displays: run ID, stage that was interrupted, when it was interrupted, reason (crash, shutdown, timeout)
- Actions: "Resume from checkpoint" button (resumes from last checkpoint via el3.7), "Retry stage" button (restarts the interrupted stage from scratch), "Abandon run" button (marks run as failed, advances to next stage or stops)

RECOVERY STATE DISPLAY:
- Show checkpoint details: last successful checkpoint timestamp, data preserved (fragment versions, artifacts created so far)
- If multiple runs are interrupted, list them chronologically with the most recent first
- After user takes action, the banner dismisses and normal dashboard state resumes

SSE INTEGRATION: Listen for run_interrupted and run_recovered SSE events. Show/hide the banner automatically. After recovery action, listen for run_resumed or run_failed events to update UI.

KEYBOARD ACCESSIBILITY: Full keyboard navigation. Focus management: when banner appears, focus moves to the banner heading. Tab order: heading → checkpoint details → resume → retry → abandon.

HOW TO VERIFY: (1) Kill the server mid-stage, restart, verify interrupted run banner appears with correct stage info. (2) Click resume — verify workflow continues from checkpoint. (3) Click retry — verify stage restarts from scratch. (4) Click abandon — verify run marked as failed. (5) Verify banner dismisses after action. Ref: §10.1, §10.2, §13.2.

title: Unit tests for frontend API client: Zod schema validation, error handling, retry logic, and typed contracts
description:
Unit tests for the typed API client layer (6k3.13). Uses the frontend test harness (od4.14) with MSW for mocking API responses.

TESTS:

1. ZOD SCHEMA VALIDATION — PER ENDPOINT
   For each API endpoint (projects CRUD, foundations, PRD intake, workflow, review, export, model config, guidance):
   a) Valid response: Zod parse succeeds, returns correctly typed object
   b) Missing required field: Zod parse throws with descriptive error naming the missing field
   c) Wrong type (string where number expected): Zod parse throws with type mismatch error
   d) Extra unexpected fields: handled per policy (passthrough or stripped — verify consistent across all schemas)
   e) Null where object expected: parse throws
   f) Deeply nested response (e.g., workflow status with nested run objects): all nested objects validated
   g) Empty array where populated array expected: parse succeeds (empty is valid)
   - Log: endpoint, schema name, input summary, parse result, error message

2. ERROR HANDLING — ALL HTTP STATUS CODES
   - Network error (fetch fails / timeout): returns typed ApiError with message and no status code
   - 400 Bad Request: extracts field-level validation errors from response body
   - 401 Unauthorized: extracts auth error, triggers credential refresh flow (if applicable)
   - 403 Forbidden: extracts permission error with action and resource
   - 404 Not Found: extracts resource type and identifier from error
   - 409 Conflict: extracts conflict details (e.g., "run already in progress")
   - 422 Unprocessable Entity: extracts structured validation errors
   - 429 Too Many Requests: extracts retry-after header value
   - 500 Internal Server Error: returns generic error with request ID if available
   - 502/503 Service Unavailable: handled as transient error
   - Non-JSON error response (plain text body): handled gracefully, not JSON parse error
   - Each error type returns a discriminated union type (status-aware error object)
   - Log: status code, error type, error message, retry eligibility

3. REQUEST CONSTRUCTION
   - Query parameters: correctly URL-encoded (special characters, spaces, unicode)
   - Path parameters: correctly interpolated (/projects/:projectId → /projects/abc-123)
   - Path parameter with special characters: properly escaped
   - Request body: JSON serialized with correct Content-Type header
   - Headers: Accept: application/json set on all requests
   - Idempotency key: included in mutating requests (POST/PUT/PATCH) as X-Idempotency-Key header
   - Log: constructed URL, headers, body preview

4. SSE CLIENT
   - Event parsing: correctly extracts event type and JSON data fields
   - Typed events: each event type parsed with its own Zod schema
   - Reconnection: auto-reconnects on connection drop with exponential backoff
   - Last-Event-ID: sent on reconnection for resume
   - Event type filtering: only processes subscribed event types, ignores others
   - Heartbeat: keeps connection alive, not surfaced as application event
   - Error events: handled without crashing, logged to console
   - Multiple subscribers: verify event fan-out to all registered callbacks
   - Log: events parsed, reconnection count, errors, timing

5. TANSTACK QUERY INTEGRATION (if applicable)
   - Query key construction: consistent per endpoint + params
   - Cache invalidation: verify correct query keys invalidated after mutations
   - Optimistic updates: verify rollback on mutation failure
   - Log: query keys, cache operations

LOGGING: Use Vitest custom reporter from od4.14. Each test logs: endpoint, test variant, MSW handler matched, request details, response details, parse result.
close_reason: Completed: Frontend API client unit tests — 16 Zod schema validation tests covering Project, Stage, WorkflowRun, ReviewItem, WorkflowStatus, Fragment, FragmentVersion, FragmentDetail, Artifact, ModelConfig, Export, SSEEventPayload. Tests valid parsing, missing fields, invalid enums, optional fields. 19 total frontend tests passing.

title: Component tests for SSE integration, error boundaries, responsive layout, and accessibility compliance
description:
Component tests for frontend infrastructure components NOT covered by 6k3.12 (dashboard, artifact viewer, review workspace, prompt inspector) or 6k3.16 (API client). Uses Vitest + Testing Library with test infrastructure from od4.14.

TESTS:

1. SSE INTEGRATION AND CACHE INVALIDATION (6k3.4)
   a) SSE connection established: EventSource created with correct URL
   b) Receive workflow_status_changed event: TanStack Query cache for workflow status invalidated
   c) Receive artifact_updated event: artifact query cache invalidated
   d) Receive stage_completed event: workflow timeline component re-renders with new state
   e) SSE disconnect: reconnect attempt within 3 seconds
   f) SSE reconnect: missed events recovered via API poll (not just SSE replay)
   g) Multiple SSE events in rapid succession (10 events in 1 second): all processed, no lost updates
   h) SSE error event: error state shown in UI, automatic reconnect scheduled
   i) Component unmount: EventSource properly closed, no memory leak
   j) Log: per-test SSE events received, cache invalidations triggered, reconnect attempts

2. ERROR BOUNDARIES (6k3.9)
   a) Component throws during render: error boundary catches, shows fallback UI
   b) Fallback UI includes: error message, "Try again" button, "Go to dashboard" link
   c) "Try again" button: resets error boundary, re-renders component
   d) Nested error boundaries: inner boundary catches first, outer boundary unaffected
   e) Error in async operation (API failure): handled by component, NOT by error boundary
   f) Error boundary at route level: navigation to broken route shows route-level fallback
   g) Error boundary logs: error and componentStack captured for diagnostics
   h) Error boundary preserves navigation: user can still navigate away from errored route
   i) Log: error type, boundary level (route/component), fallback rendered, recovery action

3. RESPONSIVE LAYOUT (6k3.8)
   a) Desktop viewport (1200px+): sidebar visible, main content uses remaining width
   b) Tablet viewport (768-1199px): sidebar collapses to icons, main content expands
   c) Mobile viewport (<768px): sidebar hidden, hamburger menu visible

   e) Viewport resize: layout transitions smoothly (no layout thrash)
   f) Long content: horizontal scroll prevented, content wraps appropriately
   g) Log: viewport width, layout mode, visible elements

4. ACCESSIBILITY COMPLIANCE (6k3.8)
   a) All interactive elements reachable via Tab key in logical order
   b) Focus visible: focused elements have visible outline/ring
   c) ARIA landmarks: main, navigation, complementary regions present
   d) Form inputs: all have associated labels (htmlFor or aria-label)

   f) Modal dialogs: focus trapped inside, Escape closes
   g) Color contrast: verify via axe-core (integrate @axe-core/react in test)
   h) Screen reader text: status updates use aria-live regions
   i) Skip to main content: link present and functional
   j) Log: per-test accessibility violations found, element roles, focus order

LOGGING: Use Vitest structured reporter from od4.14. Each test logs: component rendered, events simulated, DOM state, timing.

title: Playwright E2E: Complete flow from new project to plan export
description:
A SINGLE Playwright test that walks through the ENTIRE user journey:

1. Start at /projects (empty or not)
2. Create a new project via UI
3. Fill in foundations (name, tech stack, architecture)
4. Click Save & Preview → verify files appear
5. Click Lock → verify files persist, seed PRD textarea appears
6. Paste a seed PRD into the textarea
7. Submit → verify success
8. Navigate to dashboard → verify Stage 3 is ready
9. Click 'Start Stage' for Stage 3 → verify it runs (with mock providers)
10. Wait for stages to progress (mock providers respond instantly)
11. Navigate through any review checkpoints
12. Reach Stage 17 → click Export
13. Verify export bundle is downloadable

This test is the DEFINITION of 'the product works.' If this test passes, a real user can use the product. If it fails, the product is broken.

Per PLAYWRIGHT_BEST_PRACTICES.md: use accessible locators, unique project name, assert error absence at every step, use API helpers for verification but drive the UI for the actual flow.

Files: tests/e2e/full-journey.spec.ts

title: Stages 4-6: Synthesis, integration, and review with mock providers
description:
WHAT: After Stage 3 completes, Stages 4-6 must execute sequentially.

Stage 4 (PRD Synthesis): ExecuteSynthesis with GPT mock. Takes Stage 3 artifacts, produces synthesized PRD, records change rationales. Advances to Stage 5.

Stage 5 (Opus Integration): ExecuteIntegration with Opus mock. Reviews synthesis, produces agreements/disagreements. If disagreements exist → advance to Stage 6. If none → skip to Stage 7.

Stage 6 (User Review): Not auto-executed — the dashboard shows review items. The user accepts/rejects via the review UI. After all decisions made → advance to Stage 7.

IMPLEMENTATION: Register synthesis and integration handlers in the dispatcher. Stage 4 auto-starts after Stage 3 completes (via auto-advancer). Stage 5 auto-starts after Stage 4. Stage 6 requires user action.

SMOKE TEST: Stage 3 done → Stage 4 auto-runs → artifact created → Stage 5 auto-runs → review_items created → dashboard shows Stage 6 awaiting user.

The stage handler code exists (synthesis.go, integration.go, review.go) and mock providers exist, but they are not connected to the start endpoint.

1. When FLYWHEEL_MOCK_PROVIDERS=true, the start endpoint must: a) run preflight checks, b) create workflow_run records, c) execute the stage handler (which calls mock providers), d) record results (artifacts, tool calls, usage), e) advance current_stage on completion

title: Implement guide provisioning: built-in selection and custom uploads
description:
Implement both paths for attaching best-practice guides to a project's foundations. MERGES: cwk.4 + cwk.5 (two halves of "guide provisioning"). Ref: §3, §6.4, §10.3 Stage 1.

BUILT-IN GUIDE SELECTION:
Ship built-in guides under guides/canonical/ for known stacks (Go, React minimum). Auto-attach matching guides when user selects known stack. Guides stored as file-backed artifacts in foundations/ directory. Unknown stack without custom guides: warn but don't block.

CUSTOM GUIDE UPLOADS:
Allow markdown guide uploads for custom/mixed stacks. Validate: extension (.md), MIME type, max size (configurable per §6.4). Store as file-backed foundation artifacts with provenance=user_upload. Sanitize filenames, reject path traversal, never follow symlinks outside managed directory. Support drag-and-drop and file picker with keyboard accessibility (§13.2.1).

ACCEPTANCE CRITERIA:
1. Built-in guides for Go and React. 2. Known stack → auto-attaches. 3. Missing coverage → warning, not block. 4. Upload validates extension/MIME/size. 5. provenance=user_upload. 6. Path traversal prevention. 7. No symlink following. 8. Drag-and-drop + file picker + keyboard.

title: Stages 7-9: Review loop with commit and convergence using mock providers
description:
WHAT: After Stage 6 review decisions (or skip), the review loop runs.

Stage 7 (PRD Review): ExecuteReview with GPT/Opus mock. Produces fragment operations (update/add/remove). Fresh session each iteration.

Stage 8 (Commit): CommitFragmentOperations applies the operations to the canonical artifact. Creates new artifact version.

Stage 9 (Loop Control): Check convergence (zero ops → offer early exit), check iteration count. If loop continues → back to Stage 7. If exhausted/converged → advance to Stage 10.

IMPLEMENTATION: Wire review handler and commit handler. Loop engine (loopengine.go) already exists — integrate it with the dispatcher. Model rotation at midpoint (Opus at iteration 2 of 4). For mock testing, configure loop count = 2 to keep tests fast.

SMOKE TEST: Start loop → iteration 1 runs (review + commit) → iteration 2 runs → convergence or exhaustion → current_stage = parallel_plan_generation.

FILES: internal/workflow/stages/review.go, internal/workflow/commit.go, internal/workflow/loopengine.go

Known issue: MaxOpenConns(1) causes the auto-advance chain goroutine to hold the DB connection, blocking all other HTTP requests during multi-stage execution. Need to increase MaxOpenConns or release connection between stages.

title: Stages 10-17: Plan pipeline and export using parameterized handlers
description:
WHAT: The plan pipeline (Stages 10-16) mirrors the PRD pipeline (Stages 3-9) with different document type and prompt templates. Stage 17 runs stabilization checks and creates the export bundle.

IMPLEMENTATION: Since synthesis, integration, and review handlers are already parameterized (PlanSynthesisConfig, PlanIntegrationConfig, PlanReviewConfig, PlanLoopConfig), this is primarily wiring — register the plan-variant handlers in the dispatcher alongside the PRD variants.

Stage 17: Run stabilization checks (export/stabilize.go). If no blocking issues, create export bundle (export/bundle.go). Store in exports table.

SMOKE TEST: After PRD loop completes → Stage 10 auto-starts → plan parallel gen → synthesis → integration → review loop → Stage 17 → stabilization → export created → GET /api/exports/{id} returns bundle metadata.
