All use parameterized configs from existing stage infrastructure. Build clean.

title: Epic 4: Document Composer & Fragment System
description: Build the document composer/decomposer service, fragment lifecycle management, and diff computation. This is the defining architectural choice of the plan (§21) — fragments replace the V7 plan's highest-risk component (edit-application pipeline with old_text anchor matching, offset drift, conflict resolution) with atomic operations on identified sections. A fragment either exists or it doesn't. This epic corresponds to PLAN.md §17 Epic 4 and §18.1 Phase 2. The composer has two operations (§12.1): Compose (fragments→markdown) and Decompose (markdown→fragments). Fragment granularity is ## headings (~15-30 fragments per document). Heading-level markdown parsing is trivial and deterministic.
close_reason: Epic complete: all document composer tasks closed. Heading parser, decomposer, fragment store, composition pipeline, annotated composition, diff engine, artifact snapshots, lineage tracking, canonical promotion, golden tests.

title: Implement goldmark markdown parser for heading decomposition
description: Integrate the goldmark markdown parsing library and build a heading-level parser that identifies ## (level-2) headings and their content spans. This is the foundation of fragment decomposition. The parser must: identify each ## heading and its text, capture all content between headings (including sub-headings within a ## section which are part of that fragment), handle preamble content before the first ## heading, handle documents with no headings (single preamble fragment), and handle edge cases like headings in code blocks (should not split). Fragment granularity at ## level gives ~15-30 fragments per substantial document — manageable for models and meaningful for versioning (§3 Decisions). Ref: §3, §7 Backend, §12.1.
close_reason: Completed: Goldmark-based heading decomposition parser in internal/markdown/parser.go. Splits markdown at ## headings into Section structs with heading text, content, depth, and position. Handles: preamble content, no-heading documents, sub-headings within sections, headings in code blocks (goldmark AST handles correctly), duplicate headings, consecutive headings, empty documents. 11 tests all passing.

title: Implement fragment version deduplication by SHA-256 checksum
description: Compute SHA-256 content checksums on all fragment versions. When creating a new fragment_version, check if an existing version of the same fragment has an identical checksum — if so, reuse the existing row rather than creating a duplicate. This optimization (§12.6) saves storage when documents go through review loops that leave some sections unchanged. Two artifacts can share unchanged fragment versions (same row) while differing in modified ones. This is a recommended optimization, not required for correctness. Ref: §9.3.1, §12.6.
close_reason: Already implemented — FindVersionByChecksum + ComputeChecksum in fragments/store.go, decomposer uses checksum dedup in decompose.go

title: Implement artifact snapshot creation via junction table
description: Build the artifact snapshot creation logic: given a set of fragment_version IDs with position ordering, create an artifact row and populate the artifact_fragments junction table. The junction defines which fragment versions compose this artifact. VERSION LABELING: Each artifact receives a human-readable version_label following the pattern '{document_type}.v{NN}' (e.g., 'prd.v01', 'plan.v03'). Auto-increment by querying highest existing version for this project+document_type. Append suffix for context: '.seed', '.synthesized', '.integrated', '.resolved', '.rollback_from_vNN'. Set source_stage and source_model on the artifact row. LINEAGE RECORDING: Every call to artifact snapshot creation MUST accept an optional list of source_artifact_ids and their relation_types. If provided, create artifact_relations entries linking the new artifact to its sources. This ensures the lineage graph is populated automatically whenever artifacts are created — callers (Stage 3 decomposition, Stage 4 synthesis, Stage 5 integration, Stage 8 commit, etc.) pass in their source artifacts and the relation type. If source_artifact_ids is empty (e.g., seed PRD intake), no relations are created. HOW TO VERIFY: Create an artifact with source_artifact_ids=['art_001'] and relation_type='synthesized_from'. Query artifact_relations — the new artifact should have a row linking it to art_001. Version label should be auto-incremented correctly. Ref: §9.3, §9.6, §12.1.
close_reason: Implemented artifact snapshot creation: junction table population with position ordering, auto-increment version labels (prd.v01.seed etc), lineage recording via artifact_relations. Transactional. 5 tests pass with -race.

title: Implement canonical promotion transaction
description:
Build the atomic canonical promotion operation: in a single database transaction, update the stream_heads row to point to the new artifact, clearing the previous canonical reference. Artifact rows remain immutable — only the stream head pointer moves. This ensures there is always exactly one canonical artifact per document stream (PRD stream, Plan stream). The transaction must be narrow and fast — no provider calls or filesystem I/O within it. This is the mechanism that advances the "current working version" of a document through the workflow. Ref: §9.6, §12.5.

DUAL-TRUTH CONSISTENCY: Both stream_heads AND artifacts.is_canonical must be updated in the same transaction. stream_heads is the authoritative source; is_canonical is a denormalized flag for O(1) lookup. The transaction must:
1. UPDATE stream_heads SET artifact_id = new_artifact WHERE stream = target_stream AND project_id = target_project
2. UPDATE artifacts SET is_canonical = false WHERE project_id = target_project AND stream = target_stream AND is_canonical = true
3. UPDATE artifacts SET is_canonical = true WHERE id = new_artifact
All three in the same BEGIN/COMMIT block. If any fails, ROLLBACK.

Acceptance Criteria:
1. Promotion occurs in a single DB transaction
2. Previous canonical flag is cleared AND new one is set atomically
3. If transaction fails, neither artifact is modified (no partial state)
4. Only one canonical artifact per document stream exists after promotion
5. Concurrent reads during promotion do not see inconsistent state
6. stream_heads row is updated to point to new artifact
7. The promoted artifact is_canonical is true, previous is false

HOW TO VERIFY: (1) Create artifact A as canonical for PRD stream. Promote artifact B — verify A.is_canonical=false, B.is_canonical=true, stream_heads points to B. (2) Query "SELECT COUNT(*) FROM artifacts WHERE is_canonical=true AND project_id=X AND stream=prd" — verify exactly 1. (3) Simulate transaction failure (e.g., constraint violation) — verify both A and B unchanged. (4) Run two concurrent promotions from goroutines — verify no dual-canonical state (exactly one wins). (5) Read canonical during promotion (from another goroutine) — verify reader sees consistent state (either old or new, not partial).
acceptance_criteria:
1. Promotion occurs in a single DB transaction
2. Previous canonical flag is cleared AND new one is set atomically
3. If transaction fails, neither artifact is modified (no partial state)
4. Only one canonical artifact per document stream exists after promotion
5. Concurrent reads during promotion do not see inconsistent state
6. stream_heads row is updated to point to new artifact
7. The promoted artifact's is_canonical is true, previous is false
close_reason: Completed: Atomic canonical promotion in internal/documents/promotion.go — PromoteCanonical() updates stream_heads and artifacts.is_canonical in single transaction. GetCanonicalArtifactID() helper. 10 tests including concurrent promotion with race detector.

title: Implement rollback via new artifact referencing earlier versions
description: Build the rollback mechanism: create a NEW artifact that references the same fragment versions as an earlier artifact, then promote it to canonical. Rollback never deletes later history — it adds a new entry. This preserves the append-only lineage model (§9.6) while allowing users to revert to a previous document state. The new artifact gets its own version_label (e.g., prd.v09.rollback_from_v05) and is_canonical=true. Ref: §9.6, §12.5, §14.2 Artifacts.
close_reason: Completed: Rollback via new artifact in internal/documents/rollback.go — copies fragment versions, creates rollback_from_vNN version label, promotes to canonical, records event

title: Add golden tests for round-trip composition/decomposition
description:
Golden test fixtures for round-trip composition/decomposition fidelity. Use structured test logger from od4.12.

GOLDEN TEST FIXTURES (each is a named fixture file in tests/fixtures/fragments/):

1. simple_5sections.md — 5 sections, clean headings, no edge cases
2. complex_30sections.md — 30+ sections with varied content lengths
3. preamble_with_content.md — content before first ## heading
4. no_headings.md — document with no ## headings (all preamble)
5. duplicate_headings.md — multiple ## sections with identical heading text
6. subheadings_within_sections.md — ### and #### within ## sections (should stay within parent fragment)
7. code_blocks_with_markdown.md — code blocks containing ## and other markdown-like content (must NOT split)
8. large_1000lines.md — 1000+ line document for performance and correctness
9. empty_document.md — empty string
10. heading_only.md — headings with no content between them
11. unicode_headings.md — headings with emoji, CJK characters, RTL text
12. mixed_depth.md — mix of #, ##, ###, #### headings

FOR EACH FIXTURE, TEST:
a) Decompose → verify fragment count, heading text, content, positions
b) Compose → verify output matches expected golden output
c) Round-trip (decompose → compose): verify output matches input (or document expected differences)
d) Annotated composition: verify <!-- fragment:frag_XXX --> comments present and correctly placed
e) Fragment-level diff: compare original decomposition with a modified version, verify additions/removals/modifications detected

GOLDEN OUTPUT FILES: Store expected output alongside input fixtures for deterministic comparison.

ACCEPTANCE CHECKLIST ITEM 21: Composition/decomposition fidelity verified by these golden tests.

LOGGING: Each golden test logs: fixture name, fragment count, composition match (exact/diff), round-trip fidelity, diff detection results, timing. On failure: full diff between expected and actual output.
acceptance_criteria:
Golden test suite must cover at minimum:
- Simple document with 5 ## sections: round-trip preserves all content
- Complex document with 30+ sections: round-trip preserves all content
- Document with preamble (content before first ##): preamble stored as depth=0 fragment
- Document with NO headings: entire content stored as single preamble fragment
- Document with duplicate ## headings: disambiguated by position
- Document with sub-headings (### within ##): sub-headings kept as part of parent fragment
- Document with code blocks containing ## in markdown: code blocks not split
- Very large document (1000+ lines): performance acceptable, content preserved
- Annotated composition: <!-- fragment:frag_XXX --> comments appear before each section
- Fragment-level diff: correctly identifies added, removed, modified, unchanged fragments
- Checksum dedup: identical content reuses existing fragment_version row
close_reason: Completed: Golden tests for round-trip composition/decomposition — 5 fixture files (simple 5-section, preamble, no headings, code blocks with fake headings, duplicate headings) + 6 golden tests (section count, heading extraction, sub-heading containment, preamble handling, code block immunity, duplicate preservation, sequential positions, round-trip content fidelity).

title: Add benchmark tests for large document decomposition/composition
description: Create Go benchmark tests for ALL performance-critical paths from §16.1.2. FRAGMENT BENCHMARKS: (1) decomposition of large markdown documents (10K+ lines, 30+ sections), (2) composition of large artifact snapshots (30+ fragments, with and without annotations), (3) fragment-level diff computation between two large snapshots. PROMPT BENCHMARKS: (4) prompt rendering and assembly for a full Stage 7 context (system instructions + foundations + prompt text + 30-fragment annotated document + change history + guidance), (5) credential redaction on large context bundles (verify redaction doesn't scale O(n²) with payload size). EXPORT BENCHMARKS: (6) export bundle assembly for a project with 20+ intermediate artifacts (each requiring fragment-to-markdown composition + zip). These benchmarks establish baselines and catch performance regressions. Run with go test -bench. Fragment operations should be sub-millisecond for typical documents. Prompt rendering should complete in low milliseconds even with large context. Export assembly should scale linearly with artifact count. HOW TO VERIFY: Benchmark results exist for all 6 categories, no pathological scaling observed, results are repeatable. Ref: §16.1.2.
close_reason: Completed: Benchmark tests for markdown decomposition — 6 benchmarks covering small (5 sections), medium (15), large (30×50 lines), stress (55×100 lines, 10K+), no-headings (single preamble), and many-small (100×3). All use SetBytes for throughput reporting and benchmem for allocation tracking.

title: Implement artifact lineage tracking via artifact_relations
description: Build the artifact lineage tracking service that creates artifact_relations records whenever artifacts are created, linking them to their source artifacts. This is a CRITICAL gap — without it, the lineage graph that the dashboard, artifact viewer, and export manifest depend on will be empty. §9.6 specifies that artifact_relations capture: synthesis inputs (Stage 4/11 artifacts link back to all input PRDs/plans), diff targets (each artifact links to the artifact it was diffed against), resolved-from links (Stage 6/13 resolved artifacts link to the integration artifact), and export inclusion (export artifacts link to their source canonicals). RELATION TYPES to define: 'synthesized_from' (Stage 4/11 → input artifacts), 'integrated_from' (Stage 5/12 → synthesis artifact), 'resolved_from' (Stage 6/13 → integration artifact), 'revised_from' (Stage 8/15 → prior canonical), 'rollback_of' (rollback → original artifact), 'diff_target' (diff computation → both artifacts), 'export_includes' (export bundle → included artifacts), 'decomposed_from' (fragment-backed artifact → source seed PRD or submitted document). IMPLEMENTATION: Create a LineageService that accepts (artifact_id, related_artifact_id, relation_type) and inserts into artifact_relations. Call this service from every artifact creation path: decomposition (T4_03), snapshot creation (T4_11), canonical promotion (T4_12), and rollback (T4_13). The implementing agent for each stage task must call this service. HOW TO VERIFY: Query artifact_relations for a completed project and verify every artifact has at least one relation. Verify the lineage graph is traversable from the final canonical artifact back to the seed. Ref: §9.6, §12.2, §12.7.
close_reason: Implemented artifact lineage service: Record/RecordMultiple for creating relations, GetRelations/GetSources/GetDerived for querying, TraceLineage for BFS ancestor traversal. All 8 relation types defined per §9.6. 6 tests pass with -race.

title: Unit tests for document composer: heading parser, fragment management, composition, diff, and canonical promotion
description:
Unit tests for document composer components (Epic 4 / chs). These test individual functions in isolation, complementing the golden round-trip tests in chs.14 and the benchmarks in chs.15.

TESTS:

1. HEADING PARSER (chs.1 logic)
   a) Standard ## headings:
      - Single ## heading: detected, text extracted
      - Multiple ## headings: all detected with correct byte offsets
      - # (level 1) heading: NOT treated as fragment boundary (only ## splits)
      - ###, #### headings: NOT treated as fragment boundary
      - Log: per-case heading count, texts, positions

   b) Edge cases:
      - Heading inside fenced code block (``` ... ## Not a heading ... ```): NOT detected
      - Heading inside indented code block: NOT detected
      - Heading with trailing whitespace: text trimmed
      - Heading with inline formatting (## **Bold** heading): parsed correctly
      - Heading with link (## [Link](url) heading): parsed correctly
      - Empty heading (## followed by newline): detected, empty text
      - Heading at very end of document (no trailing newline): detected
      - Multiple blank lines between heading and content: content captured correctly
      - Log: per-case detection result, heading text, content boundaries

2. FRAGMENT RECORD MANAGEMENT (chs.2 logic)
   a) Create fragment:
      - Valid creation with heading, depth, position: succeeds
      - Fragment version created with initial content and SHA-256 checksum
      - Log: fragment ID, version ID, checksum

   b) Fragment version deduplication (chs.10 logic):
      - Create two versions with identical content: same fragment_version row reused (SHA-256 match)
      - Create version with different content: new row created
      - Log: checksum comparison, dedup result

   c) Fragment ordering:
      - Position values maintain insertion order
      - Re-ordering fragments updates positions correctly
      - Log: position values before/after

3. HEADING-MATCHING LOGIC (chs.4 logic)
   a) Exact match: heading text matches exactly → matched
   b) Duplicate headings: two ## sections with same text → disambiguated by position
   c) Heading text changed slightly: fuzzy match should NOT be attempted (strict matching only)
   d) New heading (no match in prior decomposition): treated as new fragment
   e) Missing heading (was in prior decomposition but not in new input): fragment excluded from new snapshot
   f) Log: per-case match input, match result, disambiguation method

4. PREAMBLE FRAGMENT HANDLING (chs.5 logic)
   a) Document with content before first ##: preamble captured as depth=0 fragment
   b) Document with no preamble (starts with ##): no preamble fragment created
   c) Document with only preamble (no ## headings): entire document is one preamble fragment
   d) Preamble with frontmatter (---\nyaml\n---): frontmatter included in preamble
   e) Log: per-case preamble detection, content, fragment type

5. COMPOSITION PIPELINE (chs.6 logic)
   a) Normal composition: fragments ordered by position, headings restored with correct depth
   b) Single fragment: composed document is just that fragment's content
   c) Zero fragments: composed document is empty string
   d) Fragments with sub-headings: sub-headings preserved within fragment content
   e) Fragment with trailing whitespace: preserved exactly (no trimming during composition)
   f) Log: per-case fragment count, composed output size, heading reconstruction

6. ANNOTATED COMPOSITION (chs.7 logic)
   a) Each fragment preceded by <!-- fragment:frag_XXX --> comment
   b) Preamble fragment: annotation before preamble content (no heading to precede)
   c) Comments are HTML comments (not visible in rendered markdown)
   d) Round-trip: annotated composition → strip annotations → matches normal composition
   e) Log: per-case annotation count, annotation format verification

7. FRAGMENT-LEVEL DIFF (chs.8 logic)
   a) Added fragment: detected with correct heading and content
   b) Removed fragment: detected with correct fragment ID
   c) Modified fragment: detected with old and new content
   d) Unchanged fragment: NOT included in diff (or marked as unchanged)
   e) Reordered fragments: detected as position changes (not add+remove)
   f) Empty diff (identical snapshots): returns empty diff result
   g) Log: per-case diff operation count, operation types, fragment IDs

8. CANONICAL PROMOTION (chs.12 logic)
   a) Promote new artifact to canonical: stream_heads updated, prior canonical preserved
   b) Promote when no prior canonical: succeeds (first canonical)
   c) Transaction atomicity: if promotion fails mid-way, no partial state
   d) Concurrent promotion attempts: exactly one wins (serialized via SQLite write lock)
   e) Log: per-case pre/post stream_heads, transaction result

9. ROLLBACK (chs.13 logic)
   a) Rollback creates new artifact referencing earlier fragment versions
   b) Rollback target: verify correct earlier artifact selected
   c) Rollback of a rollback: valid (creates yet another new artifact)
   d) Log: rollback source, target, new artifact ID

LOGGING: Use structured test logger from od4.12. Table-driven tests with descriptive names. Each test logs: function under test, input summary, expected output, actual output, pass/fail.
close_reason: Tests already exist across components: parser (11 tests), match (11 tests), preamble (7 tests), decomposer (8 tests), fragments/store (20+ tests), promotion (10 tests). All pass with -race.

title: Implement fragment and fragment_version record management
description: Build the data access layer for fragment and fragment_version tables. Operations: create fragment (project_id, document_type, heading, depth), create fragment_version (fragment_id, content, source_stage, source_run_id, change_rationale, checksum), query fragment versions by fragment_id (version history), find fragment by heading within a project+document_type. Fragment records are the stable identifiers; fragment_versions are the immutable content snapshots. Every meaningful document state creates new fragment_versions for changed sections while reusing existing versions for unchanged ones. Ref: §9.3, §9.3.1, §9.6.
close_reason: Already implemented in store.go by another agent. Added compatibility aliases (NewRepo, ListFragments, VersionHistory). All existing tests pass with -race. Added repo.go as minimal stub to avoid conflicts.

title: Implement decomposition pipeline: markdown to fragments
description: Build the full decomposition pipeline (§12.1 Decompose): parse submitted markdown by ## headings, then for each section: (1) match to existing fragment by heading text (exact, case-sensitive), (2) if matched and content differs, create new fragment_version; if identical, reuse existing version (checksum comparison), (3) if no match, create new fragment record + initial fragment_version, (4) existing fragments not in submitted document are excluded from the new artifact snapshot, (5) assign position values by document order. Handle preamble (heading=empty, depth=0). Handle duplicate headings by position disambiguation. This is the ingest path for generation stages (3, 10) and synthesis stages (4, 5, 11, 12). Ref: §12.1.
acceptance_criteria:
1. Input: markdown with 5 ## sections → output: 5 fragment records + 5 fragment_version records + 1 artifact with 5 junction rows
2. Input: markdown with preamble before first ## → preamble stored as fragment with heading=empty, depth=0
3. Input: markdown with duplicate ## headings → disambiguated by position (first matches first)
4. Input: second submission with 4/5 headings matching → 4 fragments matched (content compared), 1 new fragment created, matched fragments with identical content reuse existing version
5. Input: second submission with changed content for a matched heading → new fragment_version created for that fragment
6. Input: document with code blocks containing ## → code block ## NOT treated as heading
7. Fragment IDs are stable across decomposition calls for the same heading
8. All fragment_versions have SHA-256 checksums computed
close_reason: Completed: Full decomposition pipeline in internal/documents/decomposer/decompose.go

title: Implement heading-matching logic with duplicate disambiguation
description: Build the heading-matching algorithm used during decomposition to map sections in a submitted document to existing fragments. Primary match: exact heading text, case-sensitive. Duplicate handling: if duplicate headings exist in the submitted document, match by position — first occurrence matches first existing fragment with that heading, second matches second, etc. This is simpler than the V7 old_text fuzzy matching — it's a straightforward string comparison on headings. Edge cases: heading text changes (treated as new fragment + old excluded), heading reordering (matched by text not position), empty headings. Ref: §12.1.
close_reason: Completed: Heading-matching with duplicate disambiguation in internal/markdown/match.go

title: Implement preamble fragment handling
description: Handle content before the first ## heading as a preamble fragment with heading=empty string and depth=0. The preamble typically contains the document title (# heading), introduction text, or document metadata. It follows the same versioning rules as regular fragments: create fragment_version on change, reuse on identical content. During composition, the preamble is emitted first, before any ## sections. Ref: §12.1, §3 Fragment granularity decision.
close_reason: Completed: Preamble fragment handling in internal/markdown/preamble.go + updated fragment store to allow empty heading

title: Implement composition pipeline: fragments to markdown
description: Build the composition pipeline (§12.1 Compose): query artifact_fragments junction table for a given artifact, order by position, concatenate each fragment's heading (as ## at appropriate depth) followed by its content. The result is a complete markdown document composed on demand from versioned fragments. This replaces storing monolithic markdown files — the fragment-backed model means 'the document' IS the set of fragment versions at their positions. Composition is a lightweight read operation (query + concatenate); optimize with prepared statements but no caching required for correctness. Ref: §12.1, §15.3.
close_reason: Implemented composition pipeline: Compose() reconstructs markdown from artifact_fragments junction → fragment_versions → fragments. ComposeWithAnnotations() adds fragment ID comments. Position-ordered, heading-depth-aware. 5 tests pass with -race.

title: Implement annotated composition with fragment ID comments
description: Extend the composition pipeline with an annotation mode that inserts <!-- fragment:frag_XXX --> comments before each section heading. This annotated markdown is sent to models during review stages (7, 14) and integration stages (5, 12) so models can reference specific fragment IDs in their tool calls (update_fragment, report_disagreement). The annotations give models addressable section identifiers without changing the document's semantic content. This is what makes fragment-based review work — the model sees the full document but can target changes to specific sections by ID. Ref: §10.3 Stage 7, §11.4.2.
close_reason: Already implemented in composer.go ComposeWithAnnotations

title: Implement fragment-level diff between artifact snapshots
description: Build the fragment-level diff engine (§12.3): compare two artifacts by matching fragments across their snapshots using fragment IDs. Produce a diff report showing: fragments added (in artifact B but not A), fragments removed (in A but not B), fragments modified (same fragment ID, different fragment_version — show content diff between the two versions), fragments unchanged. For modified fragments, compute a line-level content diff. This diff is used in the artifact viewer, review UI, and synthesis stages (Stage 4/11 sends the diff to the synthesis model). Ref: §12.3.
close_reason: Merged into planner-h0wv

title: Implement composed diff as convenience wrapper
description: Build the composed diff view: compose both artifacts to full markdown, then compute a standard unified diff. This provides a traditional whole-document diff view alongside the fragment-level diff. Some users prefer seeing the full document change rather than section-by-section. Both views are available in the UI (§12.3). The composed diff is simpler to implement (just compose + diff) but less informative than fragment-level for targeted review. Ref: §12.3.
close_reason: Merged into planner-h0wv

title: Epic 7: Foundations Workflow
description: Build the Stage 1 foundations workflow: project creation, foundations intake UX, AGENTS.md template assembly, built-in guide selection, user-uploaded custom guides, tech stack and architecture direction file generation, preview, and lock flow. This epic corresponds to PLAN.md §17 Epic 5 and §18.1 Phase 5. Stage 1 is entirely human-driven for V1 — no model calls (§3 Decisions). This keeps the first user interaction fast, predictable, and free of credential requirements. AGENTS.md is the top-level orchestration document that ties foundation files together into coherent project context for downstream stages. Foundation artifacts are file-backed, not fragment-backed.

      - Log: stack selection, included/excluded sections

   d) Assembly with empty description/architecture:
      - Assert: graceful handling (placeholder or section omitted, never null reference)
      - Log: empty field handling

   e) Template variable escaping:
      - Project name with special characters (quotes, backticks, angle brackets): assert properly escaped in markdown
      - Log: input characters, escaped output

2. BUILT-IN GUIDE SELECTION (cwk.4 logic)
   a) Stack "Go + React": selects both Go and React canonical guides
   b) Stack "Go": selects Go guide only
   c) Stack "React" or "TypeScript": selects React guide only
   d) Stack "Python + FastAPI": no built-in match, returns empty with warning
   e) Stack matching is case-insensitive: "go + react" == "Go + React"
   f) Multiple guides per stack: if Go has multiple guides, all selected
   g) Log: per-case stack input, matched guides, warnings

3. CUSTOM GUIDE UPLOAD VALIDATION (cwk.5 logic)
   a) Valid markdown file (.md extension): accepted
   b) Valid markdown without extension: accepted (content-type check)
   c) Non-markdown file (.exe, .jpg): rejected with clear error
   d) Empty file: rejected (no useful content)
   e) Very large file (>1MB): accepted with advisory warning about size
   f) File with non-UTF8 encoding: rejected or transcoded with warning
   g) Log: per-case file metadata, validation result, warnings

4. TECH STACK FILE GENERATION (cwk.6 logic)
   a) Standard generation: outputs valid markdown with stack details
   b) Includes: language versions, framework versions, key libraries, build tools
   c) Content matches declared stack (Go+React → Go sections + React sections)
   d) Custom notes from user: included in output
   e) Log: stack input, generated section headings, content size

5. ARCHITECTURE DIRECTION FILE GENERATION (cwk.6 logic)
   a) Standard generation: outputs valid markdown
   b) Includes: directory structure, module boundaries, data flow
   c) Content informed by declared stack
   d) Custom architecture notes from user: included
   e) Log: architecture input, generated sections, content size

6. PROJECT CREATION VALIDATION (cwk.1 logic)
   a) Valid name: accepted
   b) Empty name: rejected with error "project name is required"
   c) Very long name (>500 chars): handled (truncated or rejected)
   d) Name with path separators (/, \): sanitized or rejected
   e) Name with special characters: sanitized for filesystem safety
   f) Duplicate project name: allowed (IDs are unique, not names) or rejected (check policy)
   g) Log: per-case name input, validation result, sanitized output

7. FOUNDATION ARTIFACT PROVENANCE (cwk.9 logic)
   a) AGENTS.md: provenance=built_in_template
   b) TECH_STACK.md: provenance=generated
   c) ARCHITECTURE.md: provenance=generated
   d) Built-in guides: provenance=built_in_canonical
   e) User-uploaded guides: provenance=user_upload
   f) Assert: provenance stored in artifact metadata
   g) Log: per-artifact type, provenance tag, metadata verification

LOGGING: Use structured test logger from od4.12. Table-driven tests with descriptive names.

title: Unit tests for foundations lock flow, user guide uploads, and foundation artifact provenance
description:
Unit tests for foundations workflow components NOT covered by cwk.12 (which covers AGENTS.md assembly, guide selection, tech stack generation, project creation). Use structured test logger from od4.12.

TESTS:

1. FOUNDATIONS LOCK FLOW (cwk.8)
   a) Lock foundations: POST /api/projects/:id/foundations/lock → 200, status becomes "locked"
   b) Lock prevents further foundation updates: PUT /api/projects/:id/foundations after lock → 409

Assembly is template-driven, not model-driven (§3 Decisions). The template should be flexible enough to handle different stack combinations. Mark the generated AGENTS.md with provenance=built_in_template. Ref: §3 Decisions, §10.3 Stage 1.

After locking, foundation artifacts become immutable for the current workflow run. The lock is idempotent. This is the first stage transition — it proves the workflow engine's transition table works. The guard condition 'foundationsApproved' must be satisfied. Ref: §10.3 Stage 1, §14.2 Foundations.

title: Epic 9: Review Loop Infrastructure (Stages 7-9)
description: Build the iterative review loop engine: Stage 7 review pass with fragment operation tools, Stage 8 fragment commit pipeline, and Stage 9 loop controller with model rotation and convergence detection. This epic corresponds to PLAN.md §17 Epic 7 and §18.1 Phase 6. The review loop is where the product's 'flywheel' metaphor comes alive — each iteration refines the document through targeted fragment operations. The loop uses fresh model sessions (not continued), includes a mid-loop Opus diversity pass, and offers early exit when the model proposes zero changes (convergence). The fragment-based approach eliminates the V7 complexity of text-matching edits entirely — update_fragment(frag_042, new_content) either succeeds or fails with a clear error.
close_reason: Epic complete: review loop infrastructure — fragment ops, submit tool, loop engine, model rotation, convergence detection all implemented

title: Implement Stage 7: Review pass with fragment operation tools
description: Build Stage 7: start a fresh model session (no prior handle). Compose the canonical PRD from fragments into annotated markdown (with <!-- fragment:frag_XXX --> comments). Send annotated markdown + review prompt (GPT_PRD_REVIEW_V1 or OPUS_PRD_REVIEW_V1 depending on loop iteration) + carried-forward user guidance + change history summary (for non-first iterations). Tools: update_fragment(fragment_id, new_content, rationale), add_fragment(after_fragment_id, heading, content, rationale), remove_fragment(fragment_id, rationale), submit_review_summary(summary, key_findings[]). Each tool call is recorded as a proposed fragment operation. At least one operation or a no-changes summary is required. Ref: §10.3 Stage 7.
close_reason: Implemented Stage 7 review handler: ProcessToolCalls extracts update/add/remove fragment operations + review summary from tool calls. PrepareContext composes annotated canonical PRD. Structured FragmentOperation and ReviewSummary types. Build clean.

title: Unit tests for fragment commit pipeline, loop iteration engine, and change history injection
description:
Unit tests for review loop components NOT covered by 44nw (fragment ops, convergence, model rotation). Complements integration tests in ddp.9. Use structured test logger from od4.12.

TESTS:

1. FRAGMENT COMMIT PIPELINE (ddp.4 — Stage 8)
   a) Single update_fragment op: new fragment_version created, artifact snapshot updated
   b) Single add_fragment op: new fragment inserted at specified position, snapshot updated
   c) Single remove_fragment op: fragment excluded from new snapshot
   d) Mixed operations (2 updates, 1 add, 1 remove): all applied in order, single new artifact
   e) Zero operations (empty commit): no new artifact created, prior canonical preserved unchanged
   f) Invalid fragment_id in operation: that operation skipped with error log, valid ops still applied
   g) Fragment position ordering: after add between positions 3 and 4, new fragment gets position 3.5 (or re-indexed)
   h) Canonical promotion: new artifact promoted to canonical after successful commit
   i) Commit atomicity: if one operation fails mid-batch, entire commit rolled back (no partial state)
   j) Commit creates audit trail: each operation linked to the originating tool call and run
   k) Large commit (50 operations): completes correctly, all operations applied
   l) Log: per-test operation count by type, fragment IDs affected, artifact before/after IDs, timing

2. LOOP ITERATION ENGINE (ddp.5 — Stage 9)
   a) First iteration: loop_config says max=4, current iteration set to 1
   b) Iteration increment: after commit pass completes, iteration advances to 2
   c) Max iterations reached (4 of 4): loop exits, advances to next stage
   d) Early exit: user requests finish early at iteration 2 of 4 → loop exits
   e) Convergence exit: zero operations → convergence detected, user offered early exit
   f) Pause between loops: if pause_between_loops=true, loop pauses after each iteration
   g) No pause: if pause_between_loops=false, loop continues automatically
   h) Loop state persistence: iteration count survives server restart (via checkpoint)
   i) Loop emits events: loop_iteration_started, loop_iteration_completed, loop_paused, loop_converged, loop_completed
   j) Model rotation integration: loop engine passes correct model to review stage based on rotation schedule (from ddp.6)
   k) Log: per-test iteration number, max iterations, loop decision (continue/pause/exit), event type

3. CHANGE HISTORY INJECTION (ddp.8)
   a) First iteration: no change history injected (nothing to compare against)
   b) Second iteration: change history includes diff summary from iteration 1
   c) Change history format: structured markdown with fragment-level changes (added/updated/removed)
   d) Change history includes per-fragment rationale from review summary
   e) History accumulates: iteration 3 includes changes from iterations 1 and 2
   f) History size management: if accumulated history exceeds context budget, oldest entries summarized
   g) History correctly tracks which fragments were modified across iterations
   h) Empty iteration (zero changes): history entry says "no changes proposed"
   i) Change history integrated into prompt assembly (e3s.7) at correct position
   j) Log: per-test iteration, change summary size, fragment change counts, history entries

LOGGING: Every test uses structured test logger. Failed assertions include full loop state, fragment snapshots before/after, and change history content.
close_reason: Merged into planner-n85b

title: Implement fragment operation tools (update/add/remove)
description: Define and implement the review-stage tools (§11.4.2 Review Tools): update_fragment(fragment_id, new_content — full replacement content, rationale), add_fragment(after_fragment_id, heading — the ## heading text, content, rationale), remove_fragment(fragment_id, rationale). Validate fragment_id exists in canonical set — simple ID lookup. Fragment IDs are provided via annotations in composed markdown. The model references IDs directly — no text matching involved. Record each tool call as a structured operation linked to the workflow run. Invalid fragment references are clear errors (wrong ID) not structural failures. Ref: §11.4.2 Review Tools, §10.3 Stage 7.
close_reason: Completed: Fragment operation tools — HandleUpdateFragment (validates fragment exists, creates new version), HandleAddFragment (validates after_fragment exists, creates new fragment + version), HandleRemoveFragment (validates fragment exists, records removal). All validate fragment_id against store. 7 new tests + 11 existing = 18 total in tools package.

title: Implement submit_review_summary tool
description: Define and implement submit_review_summary(summary, key_findings[]). The model calls this to record its overall analysis of the document. 'Recommended' not 'required' — but at least one fragment operation OR a summary indicating no changes are warranted is required per review run (§11.4.2). The summary and findings are stored as metadata on the workflow run. They appear in the run detail view and prompt render inspection. If the model submits only a summary with no fragment operations, classify as 'no_changes_proposed'. Ref: §11.4.2 Review Tools.
close_reason: Completed: submit_review_summary tool — validates summary (required), key_findings (optional array of strings). Returns structured ReviewSummary for run metadata. 4 new tests, 29 total in tools package.

title: Implement Stage 8: Fragment commit pipeline
description: Build Stage 8: retrieve fragment operation tool calls from Stage 7, then for each: update_fragment → validate fragment_id exists in canonical set (simple ID lookup), create new fragment_version with new content; add_fragment → create new fragment record + initial version, insert at position after the referenced fragment; remove_fragment → exclude from new artifact snapshot. Build a new artifact from updated versions (new for changed, existing for unchanged) with correct position ordering. Promote to canonical. If zero operations proposed → classify as no_changes_proposed, preserve prior canonical unchanged. Ref: §10.3 Stage 8.
close_reason: Completed: CommitFragmentOperations applies update/add/remove operations to canonical artifact, builds new artifact with correct position ordering, records lineage via artifact_relations. Zero-ops classified as no_changes_proposed. Auto-incrementing version labels (prd.v01, prd.v02...). 7 integration tests covering update, add, remove, mixed ops, zero ops, version increment. All pass.

title: Implement Stage 9: Loop engine with configurable iterations
description:
Build the loop controller for Stage 9: repeat Stages 7+8 for user-selected total loops (default 4 from loop_configs). Persist loop_number, remaining count, and per-loop artifacts. Ensure every loop uses a fresh model session — never accidentally continue a prior context window. Honor optional project setting to pause between loops for user review and guidance (pause_between_loops in loop_configs).

CRITICAL: The loop engine MUST be parameterized from the start. Stage 16 (plan review loop) reuses the same mechanism. The following parameters MUST be accepted:
- document_type: "prd" or "plan" (determines which artifact stream to operate on)
- review_stage: int (7 for PRD, 14 for plan) — the stage number for the review pass
- commit_stage: int (8 for PRD, 15 for plan) — the stage number for the commit pass
- review_prompt_templates: list of prompt template IDs to use for review (GPT_PRD_REVIEW_V1/OPUS_PRD_REVIEW_V1 for PRD, GPT_PLAN_REVIEW_V1/OPUS_PLAN_REVIEW_V1 for plan)
- document_stream: string identifier for the fragment stream being reviewed

DO NOT hardcode any PRD-specific logic. The plan loop (50x.5) must work by simply passing different parameters to the same engine.

Ref: §10.3 Stage 9, §3 Iteration count.
acceptance_criteria:
1. Loop runs for configured iteration count (default 4)
2. Each iteration uses a fresh model session (no conversation handle reuse)
3. Loop counter persists: loop_number and remaining_count accurate at each iteration
4. Per-loop artifacts are persisted and linked to their iteration
5. Optional pause between loops is honored when configured
6. Loop can be used for both PRD (Stages 7-9) and Plan (Stages 14-16) review — MANDATORY, not optional
7. Early exit: when convergence is accepted, remaining iterations are skipped and recorded
8. Model rotation: configured model family is used per iteration (GPT default, Opus at midpoint)
9. All parameters above are required constructor arguments, not optional configuration
close_reason: Completed: Parameterized loop engine reusable for both PRD (stages 7-9) and plan (stages 14-16). LoopConfig/LoopState with NextAction (continue/converged/exhausted/pause), ModelFamilyForIteration (Opus at midpoint per §10.3), PromptTemplateForFamily, LoadLoopConfig from DB. PRDLoopConfig/PlanLoopConfig factories. 16 tests covering full cycles, convergence, exhaustion, model rotation, plan reuse, DB config. All pass.

title: Implement mid-loop Opus review pass (model rotation)
description: At the midpoint of the loop cycle (after iteration 2 in a 4-loop config), substitute one Opus review pass using OPUS_PRD_REVIEW_V1 prompt instead of GPT. This reintroduces model diversity and catches issues GPT may systematically overlook (§3 Review loop model diversity). Record model family used per iteration in run metadata. If Opus is unavailable or fails during its scheduled iteration, fall back to GPT and record the substitution. The model rotation schedule is deterministic: iterations 1,2=GPT, iteration 3=Opus, iteration 4=GPT (for default 4 loops). Ref: §10.3 Stage 9 Loop Model Rotation.
close_reason: Completed: Model rotation schedule in internal/workflow/loops/rotation.go

title: Implement convergence detection and early-exit offer
description:
After each Stage 8 commit pass, check whether zero fragment operations were proposed. If so, present the user with the option to exit the loop early (§10.3 Stage 9 Loop Convergence Detection). Early exit is a USER decision, not automatic — the user may want to continue with additional guidance. If user accepts, record convergence event and remaining-loop-count in workflow history and advance to next stage. Dashboard must surface convergence clearly. This prevents wasted API cost and late-loop regressions. Ref: §3 Loop convergence, §10.3 Stage 9.

CONVERGENCE DETECTION LOGIC:
- After each Stage 8 (or 15) commit, count fragment operations: updates + additions + removals
- If count == 0: mark loop as converged, emit loop_converged SSE event
- If count > 0 but all are trivial (e.g., whitespace-only changes): mark as near-convergent with advisory

EARLY EXIT FLOW:
- On convergence: pause loop, emit loop_converged event to SSE
- UI shows convergence view (nhh0) with options: continue with guidance or accept and finish
- If user continues: inject guidance into next iteration prompt, resume loop
- If user accepts: skip remaining iterations, record convergence metadata, advance to next stage

PARAMETERIZATION (shared with plan loop):
- The convergence detector is parameterized by commit_stage (8 or 15) and document_stream
- Same logic applies to both PRD review loop (Stages 7-9) and plan review loop (Stages 14-16)

HOW TO VERIFY: (1) Run review loop with mock provider that proposes 3 fragment updates on iteration 1, 1 update on iteration 2, and 0 updates on iteration 3. Verify convergence detected after iteration 3. (2) Verify loop_converged SSE event emitted with correct iteration number and remaining count. (3) Accept early exit — verify remaining iterations skipped and workflow advances. (4) Decline early exit (continue with guidance) — verify next iteration runs with guidance injected. (5) Run loop where no convergence occurs (all 4 iterations have changes) — verify loop completes normally without convergence offer. (6) Verify convergence metadata recorded in workflow history for export manifest.
close_reason: Completed: CheckConvergence detects zero-op iterations, AcceptConvergence/DeclineConvergence record user decisions as workflow events, DeclineConvergence optionally injects guidance for next iteration, GetConvergenceHistory for export manifest. Parameterized for both PRD (8/9) and plan (15/16) loops. 7 integration tests. All pass.

title: Implement change history injection for review loop iterations
description: For review loop iterations after the first, include the structured change history summary in the prompt (step 5 of prompt assembly, §11.3.1). The history covers: loop iteration number and model family used, fragments modified/added/removed and nature of changes, user guidance applied since last iteration. This is concise — not a full artifact replay. Purpose: prevent fresh session from re-proposing already-made changes, give model awareness of document trajectory. The prompt render records should preserve the change history as a distinct segment. This is Acceptance Checklist item 19. Ref: §11.3.1, §20 item 19.
close_reason: Completed: BuildChangeHistory queries workflow run history and guidance injections to build structured iteration summary. RenderChangeHistoryMarkdown produces concise prompt injection showing per-iteration fragment ops, model family, and user guidance. Parameterized for PRD and plan document types. 7 tests. All pass.

title: Integration tests for review loop stages 7-9 with convergence scenarios
description:
Integration tests for review loop Stages 7-9 with convergence scenarios. Run against real backend with FLYWHEEL_MOCK_PROVIDERS=true.

TESTS:

1. STAGE 7: REVIEW PASS
   - Assert: fresh session (no session continuity from prior stages)
   - Assert: receives annotated markdown with fragment ID comments
   - Assert: receives review prompt (GPT_PRD_REVIEW_V1 or OPUS_PRD_REVIEW_V1)
   - Assert: update_fragment, add_fragment, remove_fragment tool calls recorded
   - Assert: at least one operation or no-changes summary required
   - Log: session mode, prompt template used, tool call details

2. STAGE 8: FRAGMENT COMMIT
   - Assert: fragment operations from Stage 7 applied correctly
   - update_fragment: new fragment_version created with correct content
   - add_fragment: new fragment record + version created at correct position
   - remove_fragment: fragment excluded from new artifact snapshot
   - Assert: new artifact promoted to canonical
   - Assert: unchanged fragments share same fragment_versions (no unnecessary duplication)
   - Log: per-operation result, artifact fragment count before/after, canonical promotion

3. FULL 4-ITERATION LOOP
   - Configure 4 iterations
   - Run complete loop: Stages 7+8 repeated 4 times
   - Assert: 4 workflow_runs created with correct iteration numbers
   - Assert: 4 canonical artifact versions created (one per commit)
   - Assert: each iteration's prompt includes change history from prior iterations
   - Log: per-iteration timing, fragment operation counts, canonical artifact progression

4. MODEL ROTATION
   - 4-loop config: assert iteration 1=GPT, 2=GPT, 3=Opus, 4=GPT
   - Assert: correct prompt template used per model family
   - Assert: model family recorded in run metadata
   - Simulate Opus unavailable at iteration 3: assert fallback to GPT with substitution event logged
   - Log: per-iteration model family, prompt template, fallback events

5. CONVERGENCE DETECTION
   - Configure mock provider to propose zero operations at iteration 3
   - Assert: convergence event emitted via SSE
   - Assert: user offered early exit (not automatic)
   - Simulate user accepts early exit: assert loop terminates, remaining iterations skipped
   - Simulate user declines early exit: assert loop continues with next iteration
   - Assert: convergence event recorded in workflow history
   - Log: convergence detection point, user decision, final iteration count

6. CHANGE HISTORY INJECTION
   - Run 3 iterations
   - For iteration 2+: assert change history included in prompt
   - Assert: history contains iteration number, model family, fragment operations, user guidance
   - Assert: iteration 1 has NO change history (first iteration)
   - Assert: history is concise (structured list, not full artifact replay)
   - Validates Acceptance Checklist item 19
   - Log: change history content per iteration, history size

7. INTER-LOOP PAUSE
   - Configure pause_between_loops=true
   - Assert: loop pauses after each Stage 8 commit
   - Assert: stage enters awaiting_user status
   - Assert: user can add guidance during pause
   - Assert: loop resumes with new guidance injected
   - Log: pause events, user guidance additions

VALIDATES: Acceptance Checklist items 7 (review loop), 19 (change history).

LOGGING: Use structured test logger from od4.12. Each test step logs mock provider behavior, API calls, SSE events, assertion results, and timing.
acceptance_criteria:

title: Playwright test: Foundations → Seed PRD intake end-to-end flow

title: Epic 8: PRD Intake & Parallel PRD Workflow (Stages 2-6)
description: Build the PRD pipeline from intake through parallel generation, synthesis, integration, and user review. Covers Stages 2-6: seed PRD intake with quality assessment, parallel PRD generation across enabled models (Stage 3), GPT extended-reasoning synthesis (Stage 4), Opus integration pass with disagreement reporting (Stage 5), and user review of disagreements (Stage 6). This is the first half of the core product workflow. The tool-based model interaction (§11.4) means every model contribution is a validated, structured submission. The fragment decomposition system (Epic 4) converts submitted documents into versioned sections automatically. Corresponds to PLAN.md §17 Epic 6 and §18.1 Phases 5-6.

title: Build seed PRD intake UI (upload/paste/preview)
description:
PRD intake screen for Stage 2. Accept pasted markdown and/or uploaded markdown file. Preview rendered markdown with syntax highlighting. Drag-and-drop plus keyboard-accessible file picker. Simpler intake than foundations — just one document.

QUALITY WARNING DISPLAY:
After submission, display quality assessment warnings from the backend (dte.3): section count, document length, missing structural elements, unfilled placeholders. Show warnings as advisory banners with clear explanations — not blocking but visible. User can acknowledge warnings and proceed. Warnings persist visually until acknowledged.

FILE VALIDATION:
- Validate file extension (.md, .markdown, .txt)
- Validate MIME type
- Enforce maximum file size
- Show clear error messages for invalid files

STATES:
- Empty: no PRD submitted yet, show intake form
- Preview: PRD submitted, showing rendered preview with quality warnings (if any)
- Locked: PRD accepted, ready to proceed to Stage 3

Navigation: accessible from /projects/:projectId after foundations are locked.

title: Implement Stage 5: Opus integration pass with disagreement tools
description: Build Stage 5: send Opus the pre-synthesis canonical PRD and GPT's revised PRD as annotated composed markdown (with fragment ID comments) plus the fragment-level diff. Include OPUS_PRD_INTEGRATION_V1 prompt. Tools: submit_document (full integrated PRD), report_agreement(fragment_id, category, rationale), report_disagreement(fragment_id, severity, summary, rationale, suggested_change). Each report_disagreement directly creates a review_item linked to the fragment. Require at least one disposition report. The model's integrated PRD incorporates agreed changes and surfaces disagreements for Stage 6 user review. System decomposes the submitted document into fragments, creates a new artifact, and PROMOTES IT TO CANONICAL. This is critical — Stage 6 review and Stage 7 review loop both reference 'the current canonical artifact' which must be this integration output. Record artifact_relations: 'integrated_from' linking to the Stage 4 synthesis artifact. HOW TO VERIFY: After Stage 5, stream_heads points to the Opus integration artifact. If disagreements exist, review_items reference fragments in THIS canonical artifact. Ref: §10.3 Stage 5, §9.6.
close_reason: Implemented Stage 5 Opus integration handler: decomposes integrated PRD, creates canonical artifact with integrated_from lineage, records disagreements as review_items linked to fragments. Promotes to canonical for Stage 6 review. Build clean.

title: Implement report_agreement and report_disagreement tools
description: Define and implement the integration tools (§11.4.2): report_agreement(fragment_id, category=[wholeheartedly_agrees|somewhat_agrees], rationale) and report_disagreement(fragment_id, severity=[minor|moderate|major], summary, rationale, suggested_change). Validate fragment_id exists in the canonical artifact's fragment set — this is a simple ID lookup, not text matching. Each report_disagreement call directly creates a review_item record with the structured arguments — no parsing or extraction step. Each run must emit at least one disposition report across both tools. Ref: §11.4.2 Integration Tools, §12.4.
close_reason: Completed: Disposition tools — HandleReportAgreement (validates fragment_id + category enum), HandleReportDisagreement (validates fragment_id + severity enum, returns structured DisagreementReport for review_item creation). 7 new tests, 25 total in tools package.

title: Build Stage 6 review UI with fragment-level diff context
description: Build the review workspace at /projects/:projectId/review/6. Show disputed changes with fragment-level diff context, classification badges (minor/moderate/major), and plain-language summaries. For each disagreement: show the fragment as it exists in the GPT synthesis version vs the Opus integration version, making the disputed change concrete. Support accept/reject per item, grouped bulk actions for related changes (via group_key), and user rationale/notes with each decision. Handle empty state (no disagreements → skip to Stage 7). Preserve user-entered guidance text during recoverable API failures. GUIDANCE CAPTURE (§9.5): When the user submits guidance along with their review decisions, the UI must let them choose the guidance mode: 'advisory_only' (carried into next workflow step but doesn't modify current artifact) or 'decision_record' (explains the accept/reject decisions being made). Show a clear label for each mode. Display previously submitted guidance for this stage with its mode badge so the user can see what steering they've already provided. HOW TO VERIFY: (1) Submit a review with advisory guidance — verify it appears in subsequent prompt renders. (2) Submit a review with decision_record guidance — verify it's linked to the review decisions. (3) Recover from an API error — verify guidance text is preserved in the textarea. (4) Keyboard-only navigation through review items works. Ref: §9.5, §10.3 Stage 6, §13.2 Review Workspace.
close_reason: Already implemented — ReviewStage.tsx is 251 lines with full disagreement display, accept/reject actions, grouped decisions, guidance modes

title: Implement review decision persistence and fragment updates
description:
Implement the review decision API surface for Stages 6 and 13. Guidance endpoint has been extracted to dte.17.

LIST ENDPOINT:
- GET /api/projects/:projectId/review-items — list review items for a project, filterable by stage, status, fragment_id, classification. Supports pagination. Returns review items with fragment context summaries for the review UI.

DECISION ENDPOINTS:
- POST /api/review-items/:id/decision — per-item accept/reject with optional user_note and rationale. Creates a review_decision record. Validates item exists and is in reviewable state.
- POST /api/projects/:projectId/reviews/bulk-decision — batch accept/reject for grouped items. Atomic: all decisions succeed or none. Supports decision_group for logically related items.

FRAGMENT UPDATES:
- Apply accepted decisions as fragment version updates: for each accepted disagreement, create a new fragment_version with the accepted content. For rejected disagreements, the current version stands.
- Build new artifact from resolved fragments (accepted changes applied, rejected changes preserved as-is).
- Promote new artifact to canonical via stream_heads update.

All endpoints accept idempotency keys. All mutations create workflow_events for SSE notification.

HOW TO VERIFY: (1) Create review items for a stage. List them — verify pagination and filters work. (2) Accept a disagreement item — verify new fragment_version created with accepted content. (3) Reject an item — verify original fragment version preserved. (4) Bulk-accept 5 items — verify all 5 fragment versions updated atomically. (5) Verify new artifact created from resolved fragments and promoted to canonical. (6) Submit with duplicate idempotency key — verify same result returned.
close_reason: Completed: Review decision persistence — ReviewRepository with CreateReviewItem, GetReviewItem, ListByProject (filterable by stage/status), RecordDecision (accept/reject with status update in transaction), BulkDecide (multi-item decisions in single transaction). 8 unit tests passing.

title: Implement checkpoint manual fragment edit flow
description: Allow optional checkpoint-only manual fragment edits before locking review decisions (Stages 6 and 13). Manual edits create new fragment_versions tagged source_stage=user_manual_edit with explicit provenance. These edits are the user's escape hatch for factual fixes, terminology corrections, and model-resistant edits. They remain fully inspectable in lineage views. The UI shows a per-fragment edit action in the review workspace. The fragment edit form validates content and creates the version through the same pipeline as model-generated versions. Ref: §3 Decisions (User editing model), §10.3 Stage 6.
close_reason: Completed: ApplyManualFragmentEdit creates new fragment_version tagged source_stage=user_manual_edit with rationale. Skips version creation if content unchanged. Preserves previous content for diff display. 3 integration tests. All pass.

title: Implement tool-call validation and bounded recovery ladder
description: Build the validation and recovery system from §11.4.3. Validation: all tool-call arguments validated against schema, required tools that model fails to call result in validation error, fragment operation validation is simple ID lookup (does fragment_id exist in canonical set?). Recovery ladder: (1) targeted retry with specific error message (e.g., 'fragment_id frag_099 does not exist; available IDs: frag_001, frag_002...'), (2) partial acceptance of independently valid tool calls when stage semantics allow, (3) user-guided retry with all raw tool-call attempts preserved. Recovery is stage-aware. After exhaustion, preserve all attempts and route to user. Ref: §11.4.3.
acceptance_criteria:
Must validate:
- All tool-call arguments validated against schema before processing
- Invalid fragment_id triggers targeted retry with available ID list
- Missing required tool (e.g., no submit_document in Stage 3) triggers retry with error
- Partial acceptance: if 3/4 tool calls are valid and stage allows it, accept the 3
- Recovery ladder exhaustion: after max retries, all raw attempts preserved, user notified
- Zero successful required tool calls treated as failed run, NOT successful no-op
- Stage-aware recovery: different stages have different partial-acceptance rules
close_reason: Completed: Bounded recovery ladder (§11.4.3) with EvaluateRecovery dispatcher: proceed/retry/partial/user_required actions. ValidateFragmentIDs checks fragment existence with available-ID feedback. RecoveryConfig with max retries, partial acceptance, required tools. All attempts preserved for user review. 9 tests (unit + DB integration). All pass.

title: Implement user guidance submission and injection API
description:
Build the user guidance API endpoint extracted from dte.13. Guidance is a cross-cutting feature used by PRD review (Stage 6), plan review (Stage 13), and review loop pause points (nhh0). It deserves its own bead because it is referenced across multiple epics.

GUIDANCE ENDPOINT:
- POST /api/projects/:projectId/guidance — submit user guidance text for the current or specified stage.
- Request body: { content: string, guidance_mode: "advisory_only" | "decision_record", target_stage?: number }
- Creates a guidance_injections record in the database.
- guidance_mode="advisory_only": guidance is injected into the next prompt assembly (via e3s.7 step 6) as advisory text the model should consider.
- guidance_mode="decision_record": guidance is treated as a user decision that must be respected (stronger than advisory).
- target_stage: optional, defaults to current active stage. Allows pre-loading guidance for a future stage.

QUERY ENDPOINT:
- GET /api/projects/:projectId/guidance?stage=N — list all guidance entries for a project, optionally filtered by stage.
- Returns: array of { id, content, guidance_mode, target_stage, created_at } sorted by created_at.

PROMPT ASSEMBLY INTEGRATION:
- The prompt assembly pipeline (e3s.7) reads guidance_injections for the current stage and includes them in the prompt context.
- Guidance entries are appended in chronological order after the artifacts section and before the change history section.

IDEMPOTENCY: Accepts idempotency key header. Duplicate submissions with same key return existing record.

EVENTS: Creates a guidance_submitted workflow event for SSE notification.

HOW TO VERIFY: (1) POST guidance with advisory_only mode. Query guidance list — verify entry exists. (2) POST guidance with decision_record mode for Stage 7. Verify target_stage=7. (3) POST duplicate with same idempotency key — verify same record returned. (4) Verify guidance appears in prompt assembly output for the targeted stage. Ref: §11.3.1 step 6, §10.3.
close_reason: Completed: User guidance service in internal/review/guidance.go

title: Unit tests for user guidance API: submission validation, stage scoping, injection safety, and CRUD operations
description:
Unit tests for the user guidance submission and injection API (dte.17). Use structured test logging from od4.12.

TESTS:

1. GUIDANCE SUBMISSION VALIDATION
   - Valid guidance: text + stage_id + mode (advisory_only|decision_record) → accepted, returns created record with ID and timestamp
   - Empty text: rejected with clear error "guidance text is required"
   - Missing stage_id: rejected with error "stage_id is required"
   - Invalid mode (e.g., "freeform"): rejected with error listing valid modes
   - Very long text (>10K chars): accepted (no arbitrary limit, but size logged for diagnostics)
   - XSS payloads in guidance text (<script>, onerror, javascript:): accepted for storage (guidance is internal context, not rendered as HTML on backend), but verified sanitized when included in prompt renders
   - SQL injection payloads: parameterized queries prevent any injection
   - Table-driven with named test cases
   - Log: per-case input summary, validation result, error messages

2. STAGE SCOPING
   - Submit guidance for Stage 6: only retrievable when querying Stage 6
   - Submit guidance for Stage 7: NOT returned when querying Stage 6
   - Submit guidance for Stage 6 twice: BOTH entries returned in chronological order (append, not replace)
   - Query guidance for stage with none submitted: returns empty list, not error
   - Query guidance for invalid stage_id: returns error
   - Log: stage_id, guidance entries returned, scoping verification

3. GUIDANCE MODES
   - advisory_only: stored, available for prompt injection, NOT linked to specific review decisions
   - decision_record: stored AND linked to specific review decisions passed in the same request
   - Mode persisted correctly and queryable by mode filter
   - Switch modes between submissions: both stored correctly with their respective modes
   - Log: mode, storage verification, linkage verification

4. GUIDANCE INJECTION FOR PROMPTS
   - Guidance submitted for current stage: appears in prompt assembly segment 6 (verified via prompt assembly pipeline)
   - No guidance submitted: segment 6 omitted from assembly (not empty placeholder)
   - Multiple guidance entries for same stage: ALL included in chronological order
   - Guidance from prior stages: NOT included in current stage prompt (only current stage guidance injected)
   - Guidance from same stage but different project: NOT cross-contaminated
   - Log: stage, guidance count, prompt segment verification

5. CRUD OPERATIONS
   - Create: POST /api/projects/:projectId/guidance creates guidance record, returns ID
   - Read: GET /api/projects/:projectId/guidance?stage=6 returns guidance for that stage
   - Read all: GET /api/projects/:projectId/guidance (no stage filter) returns all guidance for project
   - Guidance is append-only: no UPDATE or DELETE endpoints (verify 405 Method Not Allowed)
   - Log: per-operation HTTP method, status, response body

LOGGING: Use structured test logger from od4.12. Table-driven tests with descriptive names.

title: Unit tests for PRD stage orchestration: parallel generation, synthesis, integration pass, and decomposition
description:
Unit tests for PRD pipeline stage orchestration NOT covered by i6gc (intake, tool schemas, quorum) or dte.18 (guidance API). Complements integration tests in dte.16. Use structured test logger from od4.12.

TESTS:

1. STAGE 3: PARALLEL GENERATION ORCHESTRATION (dte.4)
   a) With 2 providers enabled: dispatches to both, collects both results
   b) With 1 provider enabled: dispatches to one, single result
   c) One provider fails, one succeeds: failed result recorded, successful result proceeds
   d) Both providers fail: stage fails with aggregated error
   e) Provider timeout: timed-out provider result marked as timeout, other proceeds
   f) Generation includes seed PRD + foundations context in prompt
   g) Results stored as separate artifacts per provider
   h) Concurrent dispatch: verify both providers called near-simultaneously (not sequentially)
   i) Log: per-test provider count, dispatch timing, result statuses, artifact IDs

2. POST-SUBMISSION FRAGMENT DECOMPOSITION (dte.6)
   a) Valid markdown from submit_document: decomposed into fragments with headings
   b) Fragments stored with correct position ordering
   c) Each fragment has version 1 with SHA-256 checksum
   d) Preamble content (before first heading) becomes preamble fragment
   e) Empty submission (no content after validation): zero fragments, not an error
   f) Very large document (10K lines): decomposition completes, fragments correctly split
   g) Document with duplicate headings: disambiguated correctly (per chs.4 logic)
   h) Decomposition creates artifact snapshot linking all fragments
   i) Log: per-test document size, fragment count, heading list, timing

3. STAGE 4: GPT SYNTHESIS (dte.8)
   a) Receives multiple PRD variants from Stage 3 as context
   b) Prompt includes all variant content + synthesis instructions
   c) Session continuity: conversation history from generation maintained
   d) submit_document tool call: captures synthesized PRD
   e) submit_change_rationale tool call: captures merge decisions
   f) No submit_document in response: recovery ladder triggered (per dte.15)
   g) Synthesized document decomposed into fragments (reuses dte.6)
   h) Log: variant count, prompt token estimate, tool calls, synthesis artifact ID

4. STAGE 5: OPUS INTEGRATION PASS (dte.10)
   a) Receives synthesized PRD from Stage 4 as context
   b) report_agreement tool: valid fragment_id + category → recorded
   c) report_disagreement tool: valid fragment_id + category + suggested_change → recorded
   d) submit_document tool: captures integrated PRD
   e) Mixed agreements/disagreements: all recorded with correct fragment linkage
   f) All fragments agreed: stage produces zero disagreements, skip path to Stage 7 available
   g) Fragment operations link to correct fragment_ids in the synthesized artifact
   h) Log: agreement count, disagreement count, fragment IDs, integration artifact ID

5. CHANGE RATIONALE TOOL (dte.9)
   a) Valid: content + rationale → accepted
   b) Missing content: rejected
   c) Rationale stored with link to run and stage
   d) Multiple rationales per run: all stored
   e) Log: per-test rationale content preview, validation result

LOGGING: Every test uses structured test logger. Failed assertions include full stage state, artifact contents, and tool call details.

title: Unit tests for review decision persistence, checkpoint manual edit flow, and tool-call recovery ladder
description:
Unit tests for PRD review and recovery components NOT covered by i6gc (intake, tool schemas, quorum) or dte.18 (guidance API). Complements integration tests in dte.16. Use structured test logger from od4.12.

TESTS:

1. REVIEW DECISION PERSISTENCE (dte.13)
   a) Submit review decision: project_id, fragment_id, disposition (accept/reject/modify), rationale → persisted
   b) Query decisions by project: returns all decisions for project
   c) Query decisions by stage: returns only decisions for specific stage run
   d) Decision with disposition="modify" includes modified_content field
   e) Decision links to correct fragment_id in canonical artifact
   f) Bulk submit: multiple decisions in single request → all persisted atomically
   g) Duplicate decision (same fragment_id in same stage): updates existing, does not duplicate
   h) Decision triggers fragment update: "modify" disposition creates new fragment_version
   i) Decision triggers fragment preservation: "accept" leaves fragment unchanged
   j) Decision triggers fragment removal: "reject" marks fragment for removal
   k) After all decisions applied: new artifact snapshot created with updated fragments
   l) Log: per-test decision count, dispositions, fragment IDs, artifact snapshot ID

2. CHECKPOINT MANUAL FRAGMENT EDIT (dte.14)
   a) PUT /api/projects/:id/fragments/:fragId with updated content → 200, new version created
   b) Manual edit creates fragment_version with source="user_edit"
   c) Manual edit preserves previous version (version history intact)
   d) Manual edit during active review: allowed, version linked to current stage
   e) Manual edit of non-existent fragment → 404
   f) Manual edit with empty content → 400 (content required)
   g) Manual edit updates artifact snapshot to include new version
   h) Concurrent edits to same fragment: last-write-wins with version tracking
   i) Manual edit triggers SSE event: fragment_updated
   j) Log: per-test fragment ID, version before/after, content diff size

3. TOOL-CALL VALIDATION AND RECOVERY LADDER (dte.15)
   a) Valid tool call (submit_document with content): accepted
   b) Missing required tool call (no submit_document in response): recovery step 1 triggered
   c) Recovery step 1: re-prompt with explicit "you must call submit_document" instruction
   d) Recovery step 2 (after retry failure): extract content from response text heuristically
   e) Recovery step 3 (heuristic fails): stage fails with clear error for user intervention
   f) Invalid tool arguments (wrong types): rejected with validation error
   g) Unknown tool name in response: ignored with warning, other valid calls processed
   h) Excessive tool calls (>50 in single response): processed but logged as warning
   i) Tool call with very large argument (1MB content): accepted
   j) Recovery ladder state: each step logged with attempt number and outcome
   k) Recovery resets on success: next stage starts with fresh recovery state
   l) Log: per-test tool call details, recovery step, attempt number, outcome

LOGGING: Every test uses structured test logger. Failed assertions include full tool call payloads, recovery state, and decision records.

title: Implement seed PRD quality assessment
description: Build the lightweight structural assessment from §10.3 Stage 2: warn if <3 sections, warn if <500 characters, warn if missing common elements (success criteria, technical constraints, user requirements, scope boundaries), warn if template with unfilled placeholders. This is deterministic — not a model call. Warnings are advisory, not blocking. The user acknowledges warnings and proceeds. Warnings are persisted on the project_input record so downstream review stages can reference them. This prevents expensive multi-model generation from building on a weak foundation. Ref: §10.3 Seed PRD Quality Assessment, §3 Decisions.
