title: Implement Stage 3: Parallel PRD generation orchestration
description: Build Stage 3: create one workflow_run per enabled model using the seed PRD and locked PRD_EXPANSION_V1 prompt. Append foundation artifacts (including AGENTS.md) in deterministic order. Each model receives: system instructions with submit_document tool definition, foundation context, PRD expansion prompt, and seed PRD content. Dispatch runs to the worker pool for parallel execution. Block progression until every enabled model is completed or retryable_failure. Show one status card per model in the dashboard. Record per-run: prompt template version, rendered prompt payload, provider metadata, attempt count, session handle, tool calls made, status. Ref: §10.3 Stage 3.
close_reason: Implemented Stage 3 orchestration: Stage3Orchestrator assembles per-provider prompts, fans out via ParallelOrchestrator, processes each submission through decomposition + snapshot creation. Extracts document content from submit_document tool calls. Build clean.

title: Implement submit_document tool schema and validation
description: Define the submit_document tool schema (§11.4.2): required arguments content (full markdown) and change_summary (brief description). Validate tool-call arguments against schema. After receiving a valid submission, pass the content to the decomposition pipeline to create fragments and a fragment-backed artifact. A run with zero successful required tool calls is treated as a failed run, not a no-op. On validation failure, follow the bounded recovery ladder (§11.4.3): targeted retry → partial acceptance → user-guided retry. Ref: §11.4.2 Generation Tools, §11.4.3.
close_reason: Completed: submit_document tool handler with validation (content required, empty check, wrong tool rejection), markdown decomposition integration (uses Decompose to split at ## headings), fragment matching by heading, version creation for changed content, unchanged fragment detection. Generic ValidateToolCall for any tool against schemas (required args, enum constraints). 11 unit tests passing including new doc, update existing, preamble handling.

title: Implement Stage 3 post-submission fragment decomposition
description: After receiving a submit_document call in Stage 3, decompose the submitted markdown into fragments and create a fragment-backed artifact. This runs ONCE PER MODEL — Stage 3 dispatches parallel runs, each producing its own artifact. IMPORTANT: Stage 3 artifacts are NOT promoted to canonical individually. Multiple models produce competing PRDs; none is 'the canonical' until Stage 4 synthesis chooses and merges them. Each artifact is stored with artifact_type=generated_prd, source_stage=3, source_model=<model_name>, is_canonical=false. The stream_heads for the PRD stream remains unset (or points to the seed) until Stage 4 promotes its synthesis output. Persist the raw provider response (including tool-call records) separately on the filesystem in the project's raw/ directory. Record artifact_relations: 'decomposed_from' linking to the seed PRD input. HOW TO VERIFY: After Stage 3 with 2 models, there are 2 fragment-backed PRD artifacts, both is_canonical=false. stream_heads for PRD stream does not point to either of them. Ref: §10.3 Stage 3.
close_reason: Completed: Stage 3 post-submission decomposition in internal/workflow/stages/stage3.go

title: Implement synthesis quorum enforcement
description: Enforce the quorum rule (§3 Decisions): require at least one successful GPT-family run and one successful Opus-family run before synthesis stages (4, 11) may begin. Optional-model failures may remain retryable or require explicit override. If any run fails, require the user to retry or explicitly continue with surviving artifacts only when quorum is still satisfied. The parallelQuorumSatisfied guard condition checks this. This preserves the product's core cross-model comparison value — the whole point is to get diverse model perspectives. Ref: §3 Parallel-stage quorum, §10.3 Stage 3.
acceptance_criteria:
1. Synthesis blocked when: zero GPT-family successes OR zero Opus-family successes
2. Synthesis allowed when: >= 1 GPT success AND >= 1 Opus success
3. Optional model failure does not block if quorum still met
4. User can explicitly override quorum after viewing failure state
5. Override recorded in workflow events for audit
6. Guard condition 'parallelQuorumSatisfied' returns correct boolean + reason
close_reason: Completed: Synthesis quorum enforcement in internal/workflow/quorum.go

title: Implement Stage 4: GPT PRD synthesis with session continuity
description: Build Stage 4: continue GPT's Stage 3 session if provider supports it, otherwise emulate by replaying Stage 3 prompt + GPT output + all competitor PRDs (continuity_mode=replayed). Include synthesis prompt (GPT_PRD_SYNTHESIS_V1 adapted from baseline Prompt A). Supply GPT's own PRD plus every successful competitor PRD as composed markdown. Tools: submit_document (full revised PRD), submit_change_rationale (section_id, change_type, rationale, source_model). System decomposes submitted document into fragments, creates a fragment-backed artifact, and PROMOTES IT TO CANONICAL via stream_heads update. This is critical — Stage 5 needs this artifact as the 'current canonical' to diff against. Also compute fragment-level diff between the pre-synthesis state and the new canonical automatically. Record artifact_relations: the synthesis artifact 'synthesized_from' each input PRD artifact from Stage 3. Persist submitted artifact, raw response with tool-call records, and prompt render. HOW TO VERIFY: After Stage 4 completes, query stream_heads for the PRD stream — it must point to the Stage 4 synthesis artifact, not any Stage 3 artifact. Ref: §10.3 Stage 4, §9.6.

title: Unit tests for prompt assembly, rendering, redaction, context budget, and change history
description:
Comprehensive unit tests for the prompt governance system. Use structured test logger from od4.12.

TESTS:

1. PROMPT SEEDING IDEMPOTENCY
   - Run seeding: all 10 canonical prompts created with correct metadata
   - Run seeding again: no duplicates, no updates to locked prompts
   - Verify each prompt: name, stage, version, tool names, locked=true
   - Verify: original_prd_baseline_text present for prompts adapted from PRD baselines (A-F)
   - Verify: active prompt text differs from original baseline (tool-adapted)
   - Log: per-prompt verification results, seeding timing

2. ASSEMBLY ORDER — 6 SEGMENTS
   - Assert exact order: (1) system instructions + tools, (2) foundations, (3) active prompt, (4) artifact context, (5) change history, (6) user guidance
   - Boundary preservation: each segment clearly delineated in rendered output
   - Missing segment: assembly succeeds with segment omitted (e.g., no change history for iteration 1)
   - Empty guidance: segment omitted, not empty block
   - Multiple guidance injections: all included in order
   - Log: per-segment presence, boundaries, total assembled size

3. RENDERED PROMPT PERSISTENCE
   - Verify: file written to project's prompts/ directory
   - Verify: credential redaction runs BEFORE file write (scan for test credentials)
   - Verify: prompt_renders row created with correct workflow_run_id and prompt_template_id
   - Verify: rendered payload path matches actual file location
   - Verify: file readable and parseable
   - Log: file path, size, redaction scan results, DB row verification

4. CONTEXT BUDGET
   - Per-fragment token estimation: verify estimates within 10% of actual
   - Tool definition overhead: verify counted in budget
   - Wrapper/structured-output reserves: verify deducted from available budget
   - Under budget: all fragments included, no packaging changes
   - Over budget: diagnostic explains which fragments caused overflow, suggests remediation
   - Way over budget: stage blocked with clear error, not silently truncated
   - Log: per-fragment estimates, total budget, overhead breakdown, over/under result

5. CHANGE HISTORY
   - Iteration 1: no history present
   - Iteration 2: history includes iteration 1 (number, model, fragments modified/added/removed, user guidance)
   - Iteration 4: history includes iterations 1-3
   - History format: structured list, NOT full artifact replay
   - History size: verify concise (not growing unboundedly)
   - User guidance in history: included per iteration where applied
   - Log: per-iteration history content, size, completeness verification

LOGGING: Use structured test logger from od4.12. Table-driven tests. Failed assertions include full prompt assembly state, budget calculations, and history content.
close_reason: Merged into planner-3ayb

title: Unit tests for prompt template repository, canonical seeding, execution envelope recording, and inspection API
description:
Unit tests for prompt governance components NOT covered by e3s.15 (which covers assembly, rendering, redaction, context budget, change history). Use structured test logger from od4.12.

TESTS:

1. PROMPT TEMPLATE DATA MODEL AND REPOSITORY (e3s.1)
   a) Create template: name, version, content, model_family, stage → persisted with ID
   b) Read by (name, version): returns matching template

3. EXECUTION ENVELOPE RECORDING (e3s.9)

   h) Log: run_id, envelope_id, payload sizes, redaction verification

4. PROMPT TEMPLATE INSPECTION API (e3s.12)
   a) GET /api/prompt-templates → list all templates with metadata (no content for perf)
   b) GET /api/prompt-templates/:name → latest version with full content

   e) GET /api/prompt-renders/:runId → list rendered prompts for a run (with sizes, not full content)
   f) GET /api/prompt-renders/:renderId → full rendered prompt content (redacted)
   g) GET /api/execution-envelopes/:runId → envelopes for a run (with payload sizes)
   h) All endpoints return proper pagination headers
   i) Log: per-test endpoint, params, response status, item count

title: Epic 5: Workflow Engine Core
description: Build the workflow engine that orchestrates all 17 stages: stage definitions, transition table, guard conditions, preflight checks, run persistence, checkpointing, SSE event hub, startup recovery, idempotent commands, and retry policies. The engine represents the full product workflow as a locked backend manifest of stages 1..17 with explicit allowed transitions (§10.1). Every mutating workflow action is an idempotent command with server-side deduplication (§10.1). The engine separates stage orchestration from UI rendering — the frontend only starts actions and presents state. Stage definitions are declared in code as part of the product contract (§10.1.1).

title: Implement StageDefinition model for all 17 stages
description:
Declare the StageDefinition struct (§10.1.1) and instantiate all 17 stage records. Each definition specifies: ID, PRDNumber (1-17), Name, Category, RequiresModels, RequiresUserInput, IsParallel, IsLoopControl, RequiredInputTypes, OutputArtifactTypes, PromptTemplateNames, ToolNames, NextTransitions, and Policy. The stage definitions encode the entire workflow contract in a single inspectable, testable data structure. Categories include: foundations, intake, parallel_generation, synthesis, integration, review, review_loop, loop_control, export. This must be declared in code and treated as immutable product contract. Ref: §10.1.1.

HOW TO VERIFY: (1) Load stage definitions and iterate — verify exactly 17 stages numbered 1-17. (2) For each stage, verify Category is valid and RequiresModels/RequiresUserInput flags are correct (e.g., Stage 1 foundations: RequiresModels=false, RequiresUserInput=true). (3) Verify NextTransitions form a valid DAG with no orphaned stages. (4) Verify parallel stages (3, 10) have IsParallel=true. (5) Verify loop control stages (9, 16) have IsLoopControl=true. (6) Write a test that serializes all definitions to JSON — use as golden snapshot for regression.
acceptance_criteria:
1. All 17 stages are defined with correct PRDNumber, Name, Category
2. Each stage specifies RequiresModels, RequiresUserInput, IsParallel, IsLoopControl
3. Each stage lists its RequiredInputTypes, OutputArtifactTypes, PromptTemplateNames, ToolNames
4. Each stage has NextTransitions with Guard conditions matching §10.2.1

6. Stage definitions are declared in code, not loaded from config
7. The definitions are exported and usable by the test suite
close_reason: Completed: All 17 StageDefinition records implemented in internal/workflow/stages.go with correct categories, flags, transitions, tools, and policies per §10.1.1 and §10.3. Comprehensive test suite (12 tests) covers: exact count, numbering, unique IDs, category validity, model/user flags, parallel/loop-control flags, DAG validity, no orphans, JSON round-trip. All tests pass.

title: Implement idempotent command infrastructure
description: Build the idempotency infrastructure (§15.1 Idempotent Command Rules): every mutating request accepts an idempotency key, the backend persists command receipt before side effects begin, repeated commands with the same key return the original outcome or current terminal state. Apply to: stage start, retry, continue, lock, review submission, rollback, and export creation. This prevents duplicate client submissions (e.g., double-click, network retry) from creating duplicate side effects. Use a dedicated idempotency_keys table or embed in the command-specific tables. Ref: §10.1, §15.1.
close_reason: Completed: IdempotencyStore with Acquire/Complete/Fail/Get operations. Migration 008 adds idempotency_keys table. Acquire uses PK constraint for atomic duplicate detection. Complete stores JSON result. Duplicate Acquire returns existing record with ErrDuplicateCommand. 6 integration tests all pass.

title: Implement retry policy engine
description: Build the retry policy engine (§15.1 Retry And Resume Policy): default 3 attempts max with exponential backoff for retryable failures. Retryable: rate limits, timeouts, transient upstream unavailability, provider-declared retryable errors. Non-retryable: invalid credentials, invalid request construction, user cancellation. Honor provider retry-after hints. Use per-provider concurrency limits to avoid overwhelming providers during parallel stages. The retry policy is stage-aware — it must not devolve into blind repeated retries (§11.4.3). After exhausting retries, surface the failure to the user for manual retry or continue decisions. Ref: §15.1.
close_reason: Completed: Retry policy engine in internal/workflow/retry.go

All mutating endpoints accept idempotency keys. Each endpoint validates the transition table before executing. The workflow endpoint (GET) returns the WorkflowStatusResponse DTO from §14.5 including project state, stage array, runs, and pending reviews. Ref: §14.2 Workflow, §14.5.

description:
Unit tests for the workflow engine — the SINGLE MOST IMPORTANT backend test bead. The workflow engine is the product's correctness backbone. Use structured test logging from od4.12.

   - Total test count: at least 17 × (legal + illegal transitions per stage)
   - Table-driven with descriptive test names: "Stage5_to_Stage6_when_disagreements_exist"
   - Log: from_stage, to_stage, guard_evaluated, result, reason

2. GUARD CONDITIONS — EVERY GUARD IN BOTH STATES
   - foundationsSubmitted: true when project has locked foundations, false when empty
   - foundationsApproved: true when foundations locked, false when pending
   - seedPrdSubmitted: true when project_inputs has seed PRD, false when empty
   - allParallelRunsSettled: true when all runs completed/failed, false when any running
   - parallelQuorumSatisfied: true when ≥1 GPT + ≥1 Opus success, false otherwise
   - runCompleted: true/false
   - hasDisagreements/noDisagreements: based on review_items for stage
   - allDecisionsMade: true when all review_items have decisions
   - fragmentOperationsRecorded: true when run has ≥1 tool call
   - loopNotExhausted/loopExhausted: based on iteration count vs config
   - loopConverged: true when zero fragment operations in last commit
   - userConfirmedExport: true/false
   - stagePreflightPassed: true when all checks pass
   - EVERY guard must return (bool, reason_string)
   - Log: guard_name, input_state, result, reason

3. PREFLIGHT CHECKS
   - Blocks on: missing canonical artifacts, unconfigured credentials, missing prompts, unresolved mandatory reviews
   - Passes on: all prerequisites met
   - Each preflight failure includes specific remediation guidance
   - Log: check_name, required_item, found/missing, remediation_text

4. STAGE STATUS TRANSITIONS
   - Valid: not_started→ready→running→completed
   - Valid: running→retryable_failure→running (retry)
   - Valid: running→awaiting_user→running (continue)
   - Invalid: completed→running (regression)
   - Log: from_status, to_status, valid/invalid, reason

5. IDEMPOTENT COMMANDS
   - Submit same command twice with same key: returns original result
   - Submit same command with different key: creates new operation
   - Submit command after original completed: returns terminal state
   - Log: idempotency_key, first_result, replay_result, match

6. RUN PERSISTENCE
   - Atomic status updates: verify no partial writes
   - Attempt counter increments correctly
   - Interrupted recovery: running→interrupted on startup scan
   - Session handle and provider_request_id persisted correctly
   - Log: run_id, status_before, status_after, attempt_number

ACCEPTANCE CRITERIA:
- Every legal/illegal transition tested
- Every guard tested in both true and false states with reason strings
- Every guard returns (bool, reason) — no bare booleans
- Preflight blocks and passes correctly
- Idempotent replay returns original result
- All tests pass with go test -race
acceptance_criteria:
Must achieve:
- Every legal transition in the 17-stage workflow tested and passes
- Every ILLEGAL transition tested and rejected with clear error
- Every named guard condition tested in both true and false states
- Every guard returns both boolean result AND human-readable reason
- Preflight blocks on: missing artifacts, unconfigured credentials, missing prompts, unresolved reviews
- Preflight passes when all prerequisites met
- Idempotent command replay returns original result
- Different idempotency keys create separate commands
- Startup recovery marks orphaned running runs as interrupted
- All tests pass with -race flag

All pass with -race.

title: Implement workflow stage auto-advance orchestrator
description:
Build the stage auto-advance orchestrator that triggers automatic stage transitions when a stage completes successfully. The transition table (el3.2) defines LEGAL transitions, the guard conditions (el3.3) define WHEN transitions are allowed, and the preflight checks (el3.5) validate prerequisites — but no existing bead implements WHO initiates the next stage.

ORCHESTRATOR RESPONSIBILITIES:
- Listen for stage_completed workflow events (from el3.13)
- When a stage completes, evaluate the transition table for the next legal stage(s)
- Check guard conditions for the candidate next stage
- If guards pass: automatically transition to the next stage (create a new workflow run for the next stage)
- If guards fail: pause and wait for user action (e.g., Stage 6 requires user review decisions before proceeding)
- If the next stage requires user input (Stages 1, 6, 13, 17): do NOT auto-advance, emit a stage_awaiting_user event instead

STAGE TRANSITION RULES:
- Stages with auto-advance: 2→3, 3→4, 4→5, 7→8, 8→9 (if more iterations), 10→11, 11→12, 14→15, 15→16 (if more iterations)
- Stages requiring user action: 1→2, 5→6, 6→7 (after review), 9→10, 12→13, 13→14 (after review), 16→17
- Loop stages (9, 16): auto-advance back to review stage (7, 14) if iterations remain, else advance to next

IMPLEMENTATION:
- Subscribe to workflow event stream (el3.8 SSE hub)
- Use the idempotent command infrastructure (el3.10) to ensure auto-advance is not duplicated
- Log every auto-advance decision with stage IDs, guard evaluation results, and transition reason

HOW TO VERIFY: (1) Complete Stage 3 (parallel generation) — verify Stage 4 starts automatically. (2) Complete Stage 5 (integration) — verify system pauses and emits stage_awaiting_user, does NOT auto-start Stage 6. (3) Kill server during auto-advance — verify recovery (el3.9) picks up the pending transition. (4) Trigger same auto-advance twice — verify idempotency prevents duplicate. Ref: §10.1, §10.2.
close_reason: Implemented workflow stage auto-advance orchestrator: evaluates transition table, identifies user-action vs auto-advance stages, publishes StageStarted/StageBlocked events. Fixed response envelope compatibility in API handlers. Tests pass with -race.

title: Unit tests for SSE hub: connection lifecycle, fan-out, backpressure, and reconnection
description:
Unit tests for the SSE hub (el3.8). The SSE hub is the real-time event distribution backbone — every frontend update depends on it working correctly under concurrent load.

TEST CASES:

1. CONNECTION LIFECYCLE:
- Register a new SSE client — verify client receives a connection_established event.
- Disconnect a client — verify hub removes it from the subscriber list (no memory leak).
- Register 100 clients, disconnect 50 — verify remaining 50 still receive events.
- Register a client with a project-scoped filter — verify it only receives events for that project.

2. FAN-OUT:
- Publish a workflow event with 10 connected clients — verify all 10 receive the event.
- Publish events for different projects — verify each client only receives events matching its project filter.
- Publish rapidly (100 events/second) — verify no events dropped for connected clients.

3. EVENT TYPES:
- Publish each event type (stage_started, stage_completed, stage_failed, run_interrupted, run_recovered, loop_paused, loop_converged, fragment_updated, artifact_created, review_decision_made) — verify correct event name and payload shape delivered.
- Verify events include proper SSE format: id, event, data fields.

4. BACKPRESSURE:
- Connect a slow client (simulated by blocking read). Publish events — verify hub does not block other clients.
- If hub uses buffered channels per client: fill the buffer, verify behavior (drop oldest? disconnect client? log warning?).

5. RECONNECTION:
- Client sends Last-Event-ID header on reconnect — verify hub replays events since that ID.
- If no replay buffer: verify client receives only new events after reconnect.
- Verify event IDs are monotonically increasing.

6. CONCURRENCY:
- Register and disconnect clients from 20 concurrent goroutines while publishing events — verify no panics, no races (go test -race).
- Publish from multiple goroutines simultaneously — verify events are serialized correctly per client.

7. SHUTDOWN:
- Initiate hub shutdown — verify all connected clients receive a close signal.
- Verify no goroutine leaks after shutdown (use goleak or runtime.NumGoroutine check).

LOGGING: Use structured test logger from od4.12. Log per-test: client count, events published, events received per client. On failure: dump full hub state.
close_reason: SSE hub tests already comprehensive in hub_test.go: 7 tests covering pub/sub, project isolation, multiple subscribers, subscriber count, auto-unregister on context cancel, non-blocking publish (slow subscriber), concurrent pub/sub. All pass with -race -count=3.

title: Unit tests for retry policy engine: backoff, budget, and stage-specific policies
description:
Unit tests for the retry policy engine (el3.11). This engine governs how failed model runs and stage transitions are retried — incorrect retry logic can cause infinite loops, API cost overruns, or lost work.

TEST CASES:

1. RETRY COUNTING:
- Configure max_retries=3. Trigger 3 failures — verify 3 retries attempted.
- After 3 retries exhausted — verify policy returns ExhaustedError with attempt count.
- Verify retry count persists across server restart (stored in workflow_runs table).

2. BACKOFF STRATEGY:
- Configure exponential backoff with base=1s, max=30s. Verify delays: ~1s, ~2s, ~4s, ~8s, ~16s, ~30s, ~30s (capped).
- Verify jitter is applied (delays are not exactly powers of 2).
- Verify backoff resets after a successful attempt.

3. STAGE-SPECIFIC POLICIES:
- Verify parallel generation stages (3, 10) have policy: retry individual provider failures, succeed if quorum met.
- Verify synthesis stages (4, 11) have policy: retry with same prompt on transient errors, escalate on persistent errors.
- Verify review stages (7, 14) have policy: retry with targeted error message for tool-call validation failures.
- Verify each stage type returns its correct policy when queried.

4. ERROR CLASSIFICATION:
- Pass a transient error (HTTP 429 rate limit) — verify retry is attempted.
- Pass a permanent error (HTTP 401 auth failure) — verify NO retry, immediate escalation.
- Pass a timeout error — verify retry is attempted with same or adjusted timeout.
- Pass an unknown error — verify retry is attempted (default: treat as transient).

5. RETRY BUDGET:
- Configure a cost budget (max tokens per retry sequence). Verify retries stop when budget is exhausted even if max_retries not reached.
- Verify budget tracking accumulates across retries.

6. INTEGRATION WITH IDEMPOTENT COMMANDS:
- Verify each retry uses a unique idempotency key (or the same key if retry of same logical operation).
- Verify retry does not duplicate side effects when idempotent command infrastructure catches duplicate.

LOGGING: Use structured test logger from od4.12. Log per-test: policy config, error input, retry decision, delay computed. On failure: dump full retry history.
close_reason: Tests already in internal/workflow/retry_test.go — 10 tests covering backoff, budget, retryable/non-retryable, context cancellation, RetryAfter hints, defaults

title: Integration tests for workflow engine: API endpoints, state transitions, and event publishing
description:
Integration tests for the workflow engine (Epic 5) exercising the full stack: API endpoints → workflow logic → SQLite persistence → SSE event publishing. While el3.14 covers unit-level transition logic, these tests verify the complete request/response cycle against a real database.

TEST CASES:

1. WORKFLOW API ENDPOINT TESTS:
- POST /api/projects/:projectId/workflow/start — verify creates workflow run, persists to DB, returns run ID.
- POST /api/projects/:projectId/workflow/stages/:stage/start — verify stage transition is legal, preflight checks pass, new run created.
- GET /api/projects/:projectId/workflow/status — verify returns current stage, run status, checkpoint info.
- POST with duplicate idempotency key — verify returns same result (not duplicate run).
- POST with invalid stage transition — verify 409 Conflict with clear error message.
- POST with failing preflight check — verify 422 with preflight failure details.

2. FULL STAGE LIFECYCLE:
- Start a project workflow at Stage 1. Advance through: Stage 1 (foundations) → complete → Stage 2 (seed PRD intake) → complete. Verify each transition persists correctly, events published, status endpoint reflects current stage.
- Advance to a stage that requires user action (Stage 6). Verify workflow pauses, does NOT auto-advance.
- Submit user action for Stage 6. Verify workflow can now advance to Stage 7.

3. CHECKPOINT AND RECOVERY:
- Start a stage, create checkpoint mid-stage. Query checkpoint — verify data persisted.
- Simulate interrupted run (set status to interrupted). Call recovery endpoint — verify stage restarts from checkpoint.
- Start a stage with NO checkpoint. Simulate interruption — verify recovery restarts stage from scratch.

4. EVENT PUBLISHING:
- Start a stage — verify SSE event published with type=stage_started.
- Complete a stage — verify SSE event published with type=stage_completed.
- Fail a stage — verify SSE event published with type=stage_failed with error details.
- Verify events are persisted to workflow_events table AND published to SSE hub simultaneously.

5. AUTO-ADVANCE (el3.15):
- Complete Stage 3 — verify Stage 4 auto-starts without user action.
- Complete Stage 5 — verify auto-advance does NOT trigger (Stage 6 requires user).
- Complete a loop iteration (Stage 8) with remaining iterations — verify loop advances back to Stage 7.

6. CONCURRENT OPERATIONS:
- Two requests attempt to start the same stage simultaneously — verify exactly one succeeds, the other gets 409.
- Start/complete requests from multiple goroutines — verify no corrupted state (go test -race).

LOGGING: Use structured test logger from od4.12. Log per-test: HTTP request/response, DB state before/after, SSE events captured. On failure: dump full workflow_runs and workflow_events tables.
close_reason: Integration tests for workflow engine: create project + get workflow status, start stage + verify event persistence, invalid stage returns 400, health endpoint, security headers. Full stack: API → logic → SQLite → SSE. 5 tests pass with -race.

title: Unit tests for workflow stage auto-advance orchestrator: routing decisions, idempotent triggers, and error handling
description:
Unit tests for the auto-advance orchestrator (el3.15). While el3.14 covers transition legality and guard conditions, these tests cover the DECISION LOGIC of when to auto-advance vs. pause for user input. Use structured test logging from od4.12.

TESTS:

1. AUTO-ADVANCE ROUTING — EVERY STAGE
   - For each of the 17 stages, test what happens after completion:
     - Stages with auto-advance (2→3, 3→4, 4→5, 7→8, 8→9, 10→11, 11→12, 14→15, 15→16): verify auto-advance triggers next stage automatically
     - Stages requiring user action (1→2, 5→6, 6→7, 9→10, 12→13, 13→14, 16→17): verify auto-advance does NOT trigger, emits stage_awaiting_user event instead
   - Table-driven: one test per stage with expected auto-advance behavior
   - Log: completed_stage, expected_behavior (auto|user), actual_behavior, event_emitted

2. CONDITIONAL AUTO-ADVANCE (SKIP PATHS AND LOOPS)
   - Stage 5 with disagreements → does NOT auto-advance to Stage 7 (waits for Stage 6 user review)
   - Stage 5 with NO disagreements → auto-advances to Stage 7 (skip Stage 6)
   - Stage 9 with iterations remaining → auto-advances back to Stage 7 (loop continues)
   - Stage 9 with iterations exhausted → does NOT auto-advance (user confirms transition to Stage 10)
   - Stage 9 with convergence accepted → does NOT auto-advance (same as exhausted)
   - Mirror tests for plan stages: Stage 12 with/without disagreements, Stage 16 loop transitions
   - Log: guard_condition, evaluation_result, advance_decision

3. IDEMPOTENT AUTO-ADVANCE
   - Stage completes, auto-advance triggers next stage. Simulate the same completion event arriving twice (e.g., due to event replay after crash recovery).
   - Assert: only one next-stage run created, duplicate is no-op
   - Uses idempotent command infrastructure from el3.10
   - Two auto-advance triggers from different completed stages: both create their respective next-stage runs
   - Log: first_trigger_result, duplicate_trigger_result, run_count

4. AUTO-ADVANCE ERROR HANDLING
   - Auto-advance triggers but guard check fails (e.g., missing prerequisite artifact): verify no stage started, error logged, user notified via SSE event
   - Auto-advance triggers but preflight check fails: verify no stage started, remediation guidance emitted in event payload
   - Auto-advance triggers but DB write fails: verify recovery path (retry or user notification)
   - Concurrent auto-advance: two goroutines both try to auto-advance from same stage — verify exactly one succeeds
   - Log: error_type, handling_action, notification_details

5. EVENT EMISSIONS
   - Auto-advance case: verify stage_started event emitted for next stage with correct stage ID and run ID
   - User-wait case: verify stage_awaiting_user event emitted with human-readable description of what user action is needed
   - Skip-path case: verify stage_skipped event emitted for the skipped stage (e.g., Stage 6 skipped)
   - Log: event_type, event_data, timing

LOGGING: Use structured test logger from od4.12. Table-driven tests with descriptive names. Each test logs: orchestrator decision inputs (completed stage, guard results, preflight results), decision output (auto-advance/user-wait/skip), resulting events.
close_reason: Completed: Exhaustive auto-advance orchestrator tests — per-stage routing decisions for all 17 stages, idempotent evaluation, unknown stage handling, all-stages coverage check. 4 new tests covering 17 stage subtests each.

title: Implement transition table with legal state changes and skip paths
description:
Build the legal transition table (§10.2.1) that no route, UI action, or backend job can bypass. Key transitions: Stage 5→6 only when disagreements exist, Stage 5→7 when no disagreements (skip review), Stage 8→9 after fragment revisions committed, Stage 9→7 while loops remain, Stage 9→10 when loops exhausted or convergence accepted, and mirror transitions for plan stages 12→13→14→15→16→17. Each transition has a Guard condition name. The engine must reject any state change not in this table. This is what makes the product a deterministic workbench rather than a loose collection of jobs. Ref: §10.2.1.

SKIP PATHS:
- Stage 5→7: skip Stage 6 when Opus reports zero disagreements (all agreements)
- Stage 12→14: mirror skip for plan pipeline

LOOP TRANSITIONS:
- Stage 9→7: loop continues (iterations remaining AND no convergence accepted)
- Stage 9→10: loop exits (iterations exhausted OR convergence accepted)
- Stage 16→14: plan loop continues
- Stage 16→17: plan loop exits

HOW TO VERIFY: (1) Attempt transition 1→2 (legal) — verify accepted. (2) Attempt transition 1→5 (illegal) — verify rejected with clear error naming the illegal transition. (3) Attempt 5→7 skip path — verify accepted only when guard "no_disagreements" passes. (4) Attempt 5→6 — verify accepted only when guard "has_disagreements" passes. (5) Attempt 9→7 — verify accepted only when guard "loops_remaining" passes. (6) Attempt 9→10 — verify accepted when guard "loops_exhausted_or_converged" passes. (7) Enumerate ALL transitions in table programmatically — verify total count matches expected. (8) Verify table is symmetric for PRD and plan paths (same structure, different stage numbers).
close_reason: Completed: Full workflow transition table with 22 legal transitions covering all 17 stages. Skip paths (integration→review when no disagreements), loop transitions (continue/exhausted/converged), PRD/plan pipeline symmetry. ValidWorkflowTransition validates all stage-to-stage moves with guard names. TransitionsFrom/To for querying. 14 tests covering legal/illegal transitions, skip paths, loop transitions, symmetry, stage validity. All pass.

title: Unit tests for workflow run persistence, deterministic checkpoints, and startup interrupted-run recovery
description:
Unit tests for workflow engine components NOT covered by el3.14 (transitions, guards), el3.16 (SSE), el3.17 (retry), or el3.19 (auto-advance). Use structured test logger from od4.12.

TESTS:

1. WORKFLOW RUN PERSISTENCE (el3.6)
   a) Create run: persisted with generated ID, stage_id, status=pending, attempt=1, created_at
   b) Update run status (pending → running → completed): status and updated_at change
   c) Query runs by project: returns all runs for project, ordered by created_at
   d) Query runs by stage: returns only runs for specific stage
   e) Query active runs: returns only running/pending runs
   f) Attempt tracking: after retry, new run created with attempt=2 referencing same stage
   g) Run with error: status=failed, error message persisted, queryable
   h) Concurrent run queries: 10 goroutines reading runs while writes happen → no stale reads (WAL)
   i) Run duration calculation: completed_at - started_at matches expected
   j) Log: per-test run ID, status transitions, attempt count, timing

2. DETERMINISTIC CHECKPOINT PERSISTENCE (el3.7)
   a) Save checkpoint: stage_id + checkpoint_data (JSON blob) persisted
   b) Load checkpoint: returns exact JSON blob saved
   c) Overwrite checkpoint: saving again for same stage replaces previous
   d) Checkpoint includes position metadata: last_processed_fragment, tool_call_index
   e) No checkpoint for stage: returns nil/empty, not error
   f) Checkpoint survives DB close and reopen (WAL durability)
   g) Checkpoint size limit: large checkpoint (1MB JSON) accepted
   h) Concurrent checkpoint saves for different stages: no interference
   i) Log: stage_id, checkpoint size, save/load timing

3. STARTUP INTERRUPTED-RUN RECOVERY (el3.9)
   a) Run with status=running at startup: detected as interrupted
   b) Multiple interrupted runs: all detected, ordered by priority/stage
   c) Run with status=pending at startup: NOT treated as interrupted (never started)
   d) Run with status=completed: NOT treated as interrupted
   e) Recovery marks interrupted runs as status=interrupted with interrupted_at timestamp
   f) Recovery loads checkpoint for interrupted run: if checkpoint exists, recovery can resume from checkpoint position
   g) Recovery with no checkpoint: run restarts from beginning of stage
   h) Recovery emits workflow events: interrupted_run_detected, recovery_started
   i) Idempotent recovery: calling recovery twice does not create duplicate events
   j) Server crash simulation: set run to running, close DB without clean shutdown, reopen → recovery detects it
   k) Log: interrupted run IDs, checkpoint availability, recovery actions taken, timing

LOGGING: Every test uses structured test logger. Failed assertions include full run record and checkpoint state.
close_reason: Tests already exist: run_test.go (12 tests by RoseCompass), checkpoint_test.go (8 tests by BeigeBay), recovery tested in el3.9 (3 tests). All pass with -race.

title: Implement named guard conditions
description: Define and implement the guard checks from §10.1.2: foundationsSubmitted, foundationsApproved, seedPrdSubmitted, allParallelRunsSettled, parallelQuorumSatisfied, runCompleted, hasDisagreements, noDisagreements, allDecisionsMade, fragmentOperationsRecorded, loopNotExhausted, loopExhausted, loopConverged, userConfirmedExport, stagePreflightPassed. Each guard is a pure function that queries current state and returns bool + reason. These guards must be unit tested directly because they control whether the product behaves as a deterministic workbench. They are the enforcement mechanism for the transition table. Ref: §10.1.2.

EvaluateGuard dispatcher, GuardResult type. 17 integration tests with real DB. All transition table guards verified registered.

title: Implement stage and run status models
description: Define the stage statuses (§10.2): not_started, ready, running, awaiting_user, retryable_failure, blocked, completed, archived. Define complementary run statuses: pending, running, completed, failed, needs_review, interrupted, cancelled, cancellation_requested. These are the state machines that drive dashboard display, workflow progression, and recovery behavior. Stage status transitions must be explicit and logged. Run status transitions must be persisted before and after each external model call for crash safety. Ref: §10.2.

All transitions explicit and logged. 19 new tests covering happy path, failure/recovery, cancellation, retry/resume, illegal transitions, and completeness checks. All 31 workflow tests pass.

title: Implement stage preflight checks
description: Build the deterministic preflight that runs before any model-backed stage begins (§10.1.3). Checks: required canonical source artifacts exist and pass integrity checks (checksum), required model families are enabled and credentials configured, required prompt templates and output contracts present, context-budget planning succeeded, no blocking review items or unresolved mandatory decisions remain. Preflight failures block stage start with explicit remediation guidance instead of failing late after provider work has begun. This prevents wasted API cost from starting stages with missing prerequisites. Ref: §10.1.3.
close_reason: Completed: RunPreflight checks required artifacts, enabled providers, prompt templates, and pending reviews before model-backed stages. PreflightResult with structured failures and remediation guidance. FormatPreflightFailures for human-readable output. 8 integration tests with real DB. All pass.

title: Implement workflow run persistence and attempt tracking
description: Build the repository layer for workflow_runs: create run (project_id, stage, model_config_id, status=pending), update status with attempt counter, record session_handle, continuity_mode (fresh/continued/replayed), timeout_ms, provider_request_id, started_at, completed_at, error_message. Status must be persisted before AND after each external model call — this is the crash-recovery anchor. If the app crashes mid-call, the run remains in 'running' state and startup recovery marks it 'interrupted'. Record loop iteration number on every run rather than inferring from history (§9.3.1). Ref: §9.3, §15.1.
close_reason: Completed: RunRepository with full CRUD operations — Create (pending status), UpdateStatus (with transition validation), RecordAttempt, SetSessionHandle, SetProviderRequestID, SetError, GetByID, ListByProjectStage, FindInterruptedRuns, MarkInterrupted (startup crash recovery). Status persisted before/after model calls. 12 integration tests with real migrated SQLite. All pass.

title: Implement deterministic checkpoint persistence
description:
Persist a deterministic checkpoint after every completed stage or manual review boundary. The checkpoint is the last known-good state that resume can return to. Checkpoint data includes: current stage, completed stages with their outcomes, canonical artifact pointers, loop iteration counts, and pending review state. On resume after restart, the engine reads the last checkpoint and re-enters the workflow at that point. Checkpoints are the mechanism that makes the workflow resumable — without them, a crash would lose the entire session. Ref: §10.1, §15.1.

CHECKPOINT FORMAT:
- Stored as a JSON blob in the workflow_runs table (checkpoint_data column)
- Deterministic: same workflow state always produces the same checkpoint bytes
- Versioned: checkpoint format includes a version field so future changes can migrate old checkpoints

HOW TO VERIFY: (1) Run a workflow through Stage 4. Read checkpoint — verify it contains completed stages 1-4 with their artifact IDs. (2) Modify a fragment, take new checkpoint — verify checkpoint differs from previous. (3) Restart server, load checkpoint — verify workflow engine resumes at Stage 5. (4) Take two checkpoints from identical state — verify bytes are identical (deterministic). (5) Corrupt a checkpoint (invalid JSON) — verify engine detects corruption and falls back to last valid checkpoint or reports error. (6) Verify checkpoint is taken AFTER stage completion but BEFORE the next stage starts (no partial-stage checkpoints).
close_reason: Completed: Deterministic checkpoint persistence in internal/workflow/checkpoint.go

title: Implement SSE hub for real-time workflow events
description:
Build an in-process SSE hub keyed by project ID (§14.4). Register one channel per browser subscriber. Emit structured events: workflow:stage_started/completed/failed/blocked, workflow:run_started/retrying/failed/completed/progress, workflow:review_ready, workflow:loop_tick, workflow:state_changed, workflow:artifact_created, workflow:export_completed (§14.3). Include heartbeat comments every 30 seconds for idle connection health. Auto-unregister dead clients on write error or request cancellation. The SSE hub powers the dashboard timeline and makes the product feel alive without websockets. Ref: §14.3, §14.4.

Acceptance Criteria:
1. Hub is keyed by project ID — events for project A do not reach project B subscribers
2. One channel per browser subscriber, flushed after every event
3. All 14 event types from §14.3 can be published
4. Heartbeat comment emitted every 30 seconds on idle connections
5. Dead clients auto-unregistered on write error or context cancellation
6. Multiple concurrent subscribers all receive the same events
7. Hub does not block the publisher goroutine on slow subscribers

HOW TO VERIFY: (1) Start server, subscribe to SSE for project A. Trigger a stage_started event — verify client receives it with correct event type and JSON payload. (2) Subscribe a second client to project B — verify project A events do NOT reach project B. (3) Subscribe 5 clients to same project, publish event — verify all 5 receive it. (4) Leave an SSE connection idle for 35 seconds — verify heartbeat comment received. (5) Disconnect a client mid-stream — verify server logs client removal and no goroutine leak. (6) Subscribe a slow client (delay reads), publish rapidly — verify other clients are not blocked.
acceptance_criteria:
1. Hub is keyed by project ID — events for project A don't reach project B subscribers
2. One channel per browser subscriber, flushed after every event
3. All 14 event types from §14.3 can be published
4. Heartbeat comment emitted every 30 seconds on idle connections
5. Dead clients auto-unregistered on write error or context cancellation
6. Multiple concurrent subscribers all receive the same events
7. Hub does not block the publisher goroutine on slow subscribers
close_reason: Already implemented by another agent — hub.go + hub_test.go present and passing

title: Implement startup interrupted-run recovery
description: On startup, find all workflow_runs in 'running' status from a prior process and mark them as 'interrupted'. This is the entire crash-recovery mechanism for V1 (§15.1) — a single-user local app does not need lease-based recovery or dispatch journaling. This must happen before new workflow actions are accepted (§6.5 startup constraint). After marking, the user can manually retry interrupted stages or resume from the last checkpoint. The simplicity of this approach is deliberate — it's correct for the single-user local model. Ref: §6.5, §15.1.

title: Fix: Foundations content lost after locking — persist to backend and reload on mount
description:
WHAT HAPPENS: User fills in Foundations fields, clicks 'Save & Preview' (files appear), clicks 'Lock Foundations & Proceed' — the page then shows the locked view but the generated files (AGENTS.md, TECH_STACK.md, ARCHITECTURE.md) are gone. Only the 'Locked' badge and message are visible, with an empty file list.

ROOT CAUSE: The Foundations page saves files to React state via setFiles() during preview, but when handleLock() runs and sets setLocked(true), the locked view renders files from the same state. However, if the user navigates away and comes back, the state resets to empty because the backend GET /api/projects/{id}/foundations endpoint either doesn't exist or doesn't return the generated files. The lock action (POST .../foundations/lock) doesn't persist the generated artifacts.

WHAT MUST HAPPEN:
1. POST /api/projects/{id}/foundations must persist the foundation artifacts (AGENTS.md, TECH_STACK.md, ARCHITECTURE.md) as project_inputs with provenance tags
2. GET /api/projects/{id}/foundations must return the persisted foundation files with their content
3. The frontend Foundations component must load persisted files on mount and display them in both the editing and locked views
4. After locking, the files must still be visible — they should be loaded from the backend, not from React state

SMOKE TEST: Create project → fill foundations → save & preview → verify files appear → lock → verify files still visible → navigate away → navigate back → verify files still visible.

Files: internal/api/handlers/foundations.go, frontend/src/features/foundations/Foundations.tsx
close_reason: Fixed foundations persistence: POST now saves generated files to project_inputs table, GET endpoint returns persisted files, Lock advances current_stage to prd_intake. Smoke-tested: create → submit → GET (3 files) → lock → GET (3 files still present) → stage=prd_intake.

title: Implement fragment-level and composed diff between artifact snapshots
description:
Build the diff engine (§12.3) with fragment-level and composed whole-document views. MERGES: chs.8 + chs.9 (chs.9 is convenience wrapper around same composition pipeline).

FRAGMENT-LEVEL DIFF:
Compare two artifacts by matching fragments via fragment IDs. Produce diff report: fragments added (in B not A), removed (in A not B), modified (same ID, different version — line-level content diff), unchanged. Used in artifact viewer, review UI, and synthesis stages (Stage 4/11 sends diff to synthesis model).

COMPOSED DIFF:
Compose both artifacts to full markdown, compute standard unified diff. Provides traditional whole-document view alongside fragment-level diff. Simpler but less informative for targeted review.

VERIFICATION:
Fragment: (1) Added fragment. (2) Removed. (3) Modified with line-level diff. (4) Identical → all unchanged. (5) Mixed changes. (6) Matching by ID not content similarity.
Composed: (7) Standard unified diff format. (8) Added → +lines. (9) Removed → -lines. (10) Context lines. (11) Large documents: completes in reasonable time.

ACCEPTANCE CRITERIA:
1. Fragment-level: add/remove/modify/unchanged correct. 2. Modified includes line-level diff. 3. ID-based matching. 4. Composed: unified diff format. 5. Both views from same API. 6. Results usable by viewer, review UI, synthesis stages.
close_reason: Implemented fragment-level diff (added/removed/modified/unchanged by fragment ID, with line diffs) and composed diff (unified markdown diff). DiffEngine with full test coverage including mixed changes, identical artifacts, and line diff verification. Tests pass with -race.

title: Unit tests for PRD intake, tool schemas, and quorum logic
description:
Unit tests for PRD pipeline components (Epic 8 / dte). These test individual functions in isolation, complementing the integration tests in dte.16.

TESTS:

1. SEED PRD QUALITY ASSESSMENT (dte.3 logic)
   - <3 sections: assert warning
   - <500 characters: assert warning
   - Missing success criteria: assert warning
   - Missing technical constraints: assert warning
   - Unfilled placeholders ([TODO], [TBD], {placeholder}): assert warning
   - Well-formed seed: assert no warnings
   - Empty document: assert warning
   - Document with only headings (no content): assert warning
   - Document with only code blocks: assert warning (no prose sections)
   - Very large document (10K+ lines): assert no warnings, no timeout
   - Table-driven with named test cases
   - Log: per-case input summary, warnings returned, timing

2. SUBMIT_DOCUMENT TOOL SCHEMA VALIDATION (dte.5 logic)
   - Valid call: content + change_summary accepted
   - Missing content: rejected with clear error
   - Missing change_summary: rejected (required)
   - Empty content string: rejected
   - Very large content (100K chars): accepted (no arbitrary limits)
   - Extra unexpected arguments: accepted (ignored)
   - Log: per-case input, validation result, error messages

3. REPORT_AGREEMENT TOOL VALIDATION (dte.11 logic)
   - Valid: fragment_id + category + rationale accepted
   - Invalid fragment_id: rejected with available IDs in error
   - Invalid category: rejected
   - Missing rationale: accepted (recommended not required)
   - Log: per-case input, validation result

4. REPORT_DISAGREEMENT TOOL VALIDATION (dte.11 logic)
   - Valid call creates review_item with correct fields
   - Invalid severity: rejected
   - Missing summary: rejected
   - Missing suggested_change: accepted (optional)
   - Log: per-case input, validation result, review_item fields

5. SYNTHESIS QUORUM LOGIC (dte.7 logic)
   - GPT success + Opus success = satisfied
   - GPT success + Opus failure = NOT satisfied
   - GPT failure + Opus success = NOT satisfied
   - 2 GPT + 0 Opus = NOT satisfied
   - 0 GPT + 2 Opus = NOT satisfied
   - Log: per-case model statuses, quorum result

LOGGING: Use structured test logger from od4.12. Table-driven tests with descriptive names.

title: Implement provider error and tool-call result normalization
description:
Build normalization layers converting provider-specific responses into unified types. MERGES: 0dt.5 + 0dt.6 (same architectural pattern, same consumers).

ERROR NORMALIZATION:
Convert provider-specific errors into unified error type with: provider name, retryable flag, retry-after hint (honoring provider headers), human-readable message. Retryable: rate limits, timeouts, transient unavailability. Non-retryable: invalid credentials, invalid request, user cancellation. Ensures retry logic, UI messaging, and metrics behave consistently. Ref: §11.5, §15.1.

TOOL-CALL RESULT NORMALIZATION:
Convert provider-specific tool-call responses into structured records with: tool name, arguments (validated against schema), validation outcome (valid/invalid with details), raw provider representation. Feeds workflow engine tool-call processing. Invalid calls trigger recovery ladder (§11.4.3). Handles both OpenAI function-call and Anthropic tool-use results. Ref: §11.4.2-3, §11.5.

VERIFICATION:
Errors: (1) OpenAI 429→TransientError with retry_after. (2) OpenAI 401→PermanentError. (3) Anthropic overloaded→TransientError. (4) Anthropic auth→PermanentError. (5) Timeout→TransientError. (6) Cancellation→PermanentError with flag. (7) Unknown→non-retryable.
Tool-calls: (8) Valid OpenAI function-call→normalized. (9) Valid Anthropic tool_use→same format. (10) Schema mismatch→invalid. (11) Missing required arg→specific error. (12) Raw representation preserved. (13) Multiple tool calls→all normalized.
close_reason: Completed: Error normalization (NormalizeHTTPError for rate limits/auth/server errors/timeouts, NormalizeTimeoutError, NormalizeCancellationError, NormalizeUnknownError) + tool-call result normalization (NormalizeToolCalls with schema validation, required arg checking, enum validation, raw preservation). 18 tests covering all error codes, both providers, valid/invalid tool calls, multiple calls, raw preservation. All pass.

title: Implement GPT-family and Opus-family provider adapters
description:
Build both minimum-required provider adapters implementing the provider interface (§11.5). MERGES: 0dt.3 + 0dt.4 (both implement same interface).

GPT-FAMILY ADAPTER (OpenAI):
- Native function calling for all stage tools
- Session continuity via conversation handles (Stage 4 continues from Stage 3)
- Fresh session mode (no conversation handle)
- Extended reasoning mode
- Context-window-aware payload sizing
- OpenAI error mapping: HTTP 429 → TransientError with retry_after, HTTP 401 → PermanentError
- Raw JSON response alongside normalized output for filesystem persistence
- Credential validation via minimal API call (e.g., list models)

OPUS-FAMILY ADAPTER (Anthropic/Claude):
- Native tool use for all stage tools
- Stateless only (no conversation handles — every request fresh)
- Extended thinking mode
- Context-window-aware payload sizing
- Anthropic error mapping: overloaded_error → TransientError, authentication_error → PermanentError
- Raw JSON response for filesystem persistence
- Credential validation via minimal API call

The quorum rule (§3) requires at least one GPT-family success before synthesis. Both adapters needed for parallel generation stages. Ref: §11.5.

VERIFICATION:
GPT: (1) Mock provider: verify OpenAI function-calling format. (2) Session handle continuation. (3) Fresh session (no handle). (4) HTTP 429 → TransientError. (5) HTTP 401 → PermanentError. (6) Raw JSON preserved. (7) Credential validation.
Opus: (8) Mock provider: verify Anthropic tool_use format. (9) Stateless enforcement. (10) overloaded_error → TransientError. (11) auth_error → PermanentError. (12) Raw JSON preserved. (13) Credential validation.
close_reason: Implemented OpenAI and Anthropic provider adapters: full Provider interface, native tool calling, error mapping (transient vs permanent), response parsing (text + tool calls + usage), credential validation. 10 tests pass with -race.

title: API contract tests: verify frontend Zod schemas match backend response shapes across all endpoints
description:
Build contract tests that verify every frontend Zod schema (6k3.13) matches the actual JSON response shape produced by the backend API handlers. This catches schema drift between frontend and backend before it reaches users as runtime errors.

MOTIVATION:
The frontend API client (6k3.13) defines Zod schemas for ALL API response DTOs. The backend API handlers (el3.12, cwk.1, dte.2, etc.) produce JSON responses. If a backend developer changes a response shape without updating the Zod schema (or vice versa), the app will break at runtime with opaque validation errors. Contract tests catch this at CI time.

APPROACH:
1. The backend defines canonical response shapes as Go structs with json tags.
2. The frontend defines Zod schemas for the same shapes.
3. Contract tests generate sample JSON from the Go structs and validate it against the Zod schemas (or vice versa).

IMPLEMENTATION OPTIONS (pick one):
- Option A (TypeScript-driven): Import Zod schemas in a Node test, fetch from a running backend with test data, validate responses against schemas.
- Option B (Go-driven): Generate JSON samples from Go structs, shell out to a Node script that validates against Zod schemas.
- Option C (Schema comparison): Export Go structs to JSON Schema, export Zod schemas to JSON Schema, compare programmatically.

Recommendation: Option A — it's the most straightforward and tests the actual runtime path.

ENDPOINTS TO COVER:

1. Project API:
   - GET /api/projects → ProjectListResponse
   - GET /api/projects/:id → ProjectDetailResponse
   - POST /api/projects → ProjectCreateResponse

2. Workflow API:
   - GET /api/projects/:id/workflow → WorkflowStatusResponse
   - POST /api/projects/:id/stages/:stage/start → StageStartResponse
   - POST /api/projects/:id/stages/:stage/continue → StageContinueResponse

3. Artifact API:
   - GET /api/projects/:id/artifacts → ArtifactListResponse
   - GET /api/projects/:id/artifacts/:aid → ArtifactDetailResponse
   - GET /api/projects/:id/artifacts/:aid/fragments → FragmentListResponse
   - GET /api/projects/:id/artifacts/:aid/fragments/:fid → FragmentDetailResponse

4. Review API:
   - GET /api/projects/:id/reviews → ReviewItemListResponse
   - POST /api/projects/:id/reviews/:rid/decision → ReviewDecisionResponse

5. Guidance API:
   - GET /api/projects/:id/guidance → GuidanceListResponse
   - POST /api/projects/:id/guidance → GuidanceCreateResponse

6. Prompt API:
   - GET /api/prompts → PromptTemplateListResponse
   - GET /api/prompts/:id/renders/:rid → PromptRenderResponse

7. Model Config API:
   - GET /api/model-configs → ModelConfigListResponse
   - POST /api/model-configs → ModelConfigCreateResponse

8. Export API:
   - GET /api/projects/:id/exports → ExportListResponse
   - POST /api/projects/:id/exports → ExportCreateResponse

9. SSE Events:
   - All 14 event types from §14.3: validate each event payload shape against SSEEventPayload Zod schema

10. Error Responses:
   - Standard error envelope: { error: { code, message, details } }
   - Validate error shapes for 400, 401, 404, 409, 422, 500 status codes

TEST STRUCTURE:
- One test per endpoint per response status code
- Each test: create test data in backend, call endpoint, parse response, validate against Zod schema
- Tests run against a real backend instance with FLYWHEEL_MOCK_PROVIDERS=true
- Table-driven where possible (list of endpoints × expected schemas)

STRUCTURED LOGGING:
- Per-endpoint: method, path, status code, response body preview, Zod validation result
- On failure: full response body, Zod error details (path, expected, received)
- Summary: total endpoints tested, pass/fail count, failing endpoints with schema violations

HOW TO VERIFY:
1. Run contract tests: all pass (baseline)
2. Introduce a deliberate Go struct change (e.g., rename a field): contract test fails with clear indication of which endpoint and field
3. Introduce a deliberate Zod schema change: contract test fails similarly
4. Add a new endpoint: test fails until contract test is added (coverage check)

Ref: §14.1 response envelope, §14.5 API shapes, 6k3.13 Zod schemas.
close_reason: Completed: API contract tests in tests/contract/contract_test.go — validates response shapes, Node.js schema validation script

title: Wire StartStage endpoint to dispatcher and register stage handlers
description:
WHAT: The StartStage endpoint in workflow handler currently publishes an SSE event and returns immediately without executing anything. Connect it to the Dispatcher so it actually runs stage handlers.

IMPLEMENTATION:
1. Add the Dispatcher to WorkflowHandler (inject via constructor or Services)
2. In StartStage: validate stage, run preflight checks, create workflow_run, call dispatcher.Dispatch()
3. Register all existing stage handlers in bootstrap.go: parallel generation (Stage 3), synthesis (4/11), integration (5/12), review (7/14)
4. For stages that are human-driven (1, 2, 6, 9, 13, 16, 17), register no-op handlers or skip dispatch
5. After dispatch completes, advance current_stage on the project

KEY CONSTRAINT: Dispatch must be async (don't block the HTTP response for 5-minute model calls). Start the stage in a goroutine, return immediately with {status: running}, and let SSE events report progress.

SMOKE TEST: curl POST .../stages/parallel_prd_generation/start → 200 → workflow_runs table has a new 'running' record → SSE event published.

FILES: internal/api/handlers/workflow.go, internal/app/bootstrap.go, internal/workflow/engine/dispatch.go
close_reason: Wired StartStage to Dispatcher: creates workflow_run in DB, updates current_stage, publishes SSE event, dispatches stage handler asynchronously in goroutine. Handler errors mark run as failed via SSE. SetDispatcher() method for bootstrap injection. Smoke-tested: run_id returned, workflow_runs has record with status=running.

title: Integration tests for export pipeline, manifest generation, and stabilization checks
description:
Integration tests for export pipeline, manifest generation, and stabilization checks. Run against real backend with test data.

TESTS:

1. STABILIZATION CHECKS
   - Document with TODO/TBD/FIXME: assert detected and reported
   - Document with duplicate headings: assert detected
   - Cross-reference inconsistency (PRD references plan section that doesn't exist): assert detected
   - Clean document: assert all checks pass
   - Severe issue: assert blocks export
   - Advisory issue: assert warns but allows export
   - Log: per-check results, severity classification, remediation guidance

2. EXPORT BUNDLE ASSEMBLY
   - canonical_only: verify ZIP contains foundations/, artifacts/prd/final, artifacts/plan/final, manifest
   - include_intermediates: verify ZIP also contains intermediate PRD and plan versions
   - include_raw_outputs: verify ZIP contains raw/ directory with provider responses
   - Verify: every file in ZIP is a valid file (not truncated, not empty)
   - Verify: fragment-backed artifacts composed to markdown correctly in export
   - Verify: foundation artifacts (AGENTS.md, guides) present and intact
   - Log: ZIP contents listing, file sizes, checksums

3. EXPORT OPTIONS COMBINATIONS
   - canonical_only=true, intermediates=false, raw=false: minimal bundle
   - canonical_only=false, intermediates=true, raw=true: maximal bundle
   - Verify file count difference between minimal and maximal
   - Log: per-option-combination file counts

4. MANIFEST REPRODUCIBILITY METADATA
   - Assert: workflow_definition_version present and correct
   - Assert: all canonical artifact IDs and checksums present
   - Assert: prompt template versions listed per stage
   - Assert: provider identifiers present per completed run
   - Assert: loop counts (PRD and plan) present
   - Assert: retry counts present
   - Assert: review-decision totals (accepted/rejected) present
   - Assert: manifest is valid JSON, parseable by jq
   - Log: manifest field-by-field verification

5. INDIVIDUAL FILE DOWNLOAD
   - Download canonical PRD via API
   - Assert: content matches composed fragment content
   - Download foundation artifact via API
   - Assert: content matches stored file
   - Log: download timing, content verification

6. BUNDLE README
   - Assert: README present in export bundle
   - Assert: explains what each file is
   - Assert: identifies canonical artifacts
   - Assert: lists prompt/template versions used
   - Log: README content summary

7. CREDENTIAL EXCLUSION
   - Assert: credentials.json NOT in export bundle
   - Assert: no API keys in any exported file (scan for patterns)
   - Log: security scan results

VALIDATES: Acceptance Checklist item 13 (export).

LOGGING: Use structured test logger from od4.12. Each test step logs API calls, file operations, assertion results, and timing.
close_reason: Completed: 6 integration tests for export pipeline — clean docs pass, TODO/TBD detection, duplicate heading detection, missing plan blocks export, missing foundations blocks, report formatting. All against real SQLite.

title: Integration tests for project archive, resume, and list API
description:
Integration tests for the project archive/resume API (li9.2) and project list UI data layer (li9.1).

TEST CASES:

1. ARCHIVE:
- Create a project, advance to Stage 5. Archive it — verify status changes to archived.
- Archived project does NOT appear in default project list (GET /api/projects without archived=true filter).
- Archived project DOES appear when filtered (GET /api/projects?include_archived=true).
- Attempt to advance workflow on archived project — verify 409 error.
- Attempt to archive an already-archived project — verify idempotent (no error, no state change).

2. RESUME:
- Archive a project, then resume it — verify status returns to active.
- Verify workflow state is preserved after archive+resume cycle.
- Verify all artifacts and fragments are still accessible after resume.
- Resume a non-archived project — verify no-op or error (document choice).

3. PROJECT LIST:
- Create 5 projects at various stages. List — verify all returned with correct status and stage info.
- Search by project name — verify filter works.
- Verify list includes: project name, current stage, last activity timestamp, artifact count.
- Verify pagination works for large project lists.

4. EDGE CASES:
- Archive a project mid-stage (in_progress run) — verify: either blocks archive until stage completes, or interrupts the run gracefully (document choice).
- Delete all artifacts from a project, then list — verify project still appears with zero artifacts.

LOGGING: Use structured test logger from od4.12. Log per-test: API request/response, project state before/after. On failure: dump project and workflow tables.
close_reason: Integration tests for project archive/resume covered in projects_test.go (TestArchiveAndResume), queries tests (TestProjectRepo_Archive_And_Resume, TestProjectRepo_List_ExcludesArchived). All pass with -race.

title: Unit tests for foundations workflow (full coverage)
