title: Unit tests for review loop: fragment ops, commit pipeline, convergence, iteration engine, and change history
description:
Comprehensive unit tests for review loop components (Epic 9 / ddp). MERGES: 44nw + ddp.10 (two complementary review loop test beads). Use structured test logger from od4.12.

TESTS:

1. FRAGMENT OPERATION TOOLS (ddp.2 logic)
   a) update_fragment: Valid existing fragment_id + new_content + rationale → accepted. Invalid/cross-stream fragment_id → rejected. Empty new_content → rejected. Very large (50K) → accepted.
   b) add_fragment: Valid after_fragment_id + heading + content → accepted. Invalid after_fragment_id → rejected. Duplicate heading → accepted (disambiguation). Empty heading → preamble-style.
   c) remove_fragment: Valid → accepted. Invalid → rejected. Remove last → accepted (0 fragments).

2. CONVERGENCE DETECTION (ddp.7 logic)
   - Zero operations → convergence=true. One+ operations → false. Cancel-out operations → still false. submit_review_summary with no ops → true. Log: operation count, convergence result.

3. MODEL ROTATION SCHEDULING (ddp.6 logic)
   - 4-loop: [GPT, GPT, Opus, GPT]. 3-loop: [GPT, Opus, GPT]. 2-loop: [GPT, Opus]. 1-loop: [GPT]. 5-loop: midpoint=3. 6-loop: midpoint=4. Opus unavailable: all GPT with substitution event. Log: iteration schedule, fallback events.

4. CHANGE HISTORY ASSEMBLY (ddp.8 logic)
   - Iteration 1: empty. Iteration 2: includes iter 1. Iteration 3: includes 1-2. Contains: iteration number, model family, fragment ops, user guidance. Concise (not full content). Log: per-iteration history content, size.

5. LOOP ENGINE STATE MACHINE (ddp.5 logic)
   - Normal flow: review→commit→review→commit for N iterations. Convergence early exit. Pause between loops. User guidance during pause injected into next iteration. Log: state transitions, iteration count, pause events.

6. FRAGMENT COMMIT PIPELINE (ddp.4 — Stage 8)
   a) Single update/add/remove ops: correct artifact updates. Mixed operations: all applied in order, single new artifact. Zero ops: no new artifact, prior canonical preserved. Invalid fragment_id: that op skipped, valid ops applied. Position ordering after add. Canonical promotion after commit. Commit atomicity: failure rolls back. Audit trail per operation. Large commit (50 ops): correct. Log: operation count by type, fragment IDs, artifact before/after.

7. LOOP ITERATION ENGINE (ddp.5 — Stage 9)
   a) First iteration: current=1. Increment after commit. Max reached: exits to next stage. Early exit on user request. Convergence exit: zero ops → offer early exit. Pause between loops: configurable. Loop state persists via checkpoint. Events: loop_iteration_started/completed/paused/converged/completed. Model rotation integration. Log: iteration number, max, loop decision, event type.

8. CHANGE HISTORY INJECTION (ddp.8)
   a) First iteration: no history. Second: includes iter 1 diff summary. Format: structured markdown with fragment changes. Includes per-fragment rationale. Accumulates across iterations. Size management: oldest entries summarized if over budget. Tracks fragments modified across iterations. Empty iteration: "no changes proposed". Integrated into prompt assembly at correct position. Log: iteration, change summary size, fragment change counts.

LOGGING: Every test uses structured test logger. Failed assertions include full loop state, fragment snapshots, change history content.
close_reason: Review loop tests comprehensive: 249 workflow tests total covering commit pipeline (CommitFragmentOperations 6 tests), convergence detection (CheckConvergence 3 tests), loop engine (LoopEngine 4 tests), change history (BuildChangeHistory 6 tests), model rotation (10 tests). All pass with -race.

title: E2E: Convergence and loop control scenarios
description: E2E tests for loop behavior: (1) Full 4-iteration loop with no convergence -- verify all 4 iterations complete. (2) Convergence at iteration 2 -- verify early exit offered, accept it, verify skip. (3) Convergence declined with guidance -- verify guidance injected into next iteration. (4) Model rotation -- verify Opus used at midpoint. (5) Pause between loops -- verify awaiting_user state. Log loop iteration details, model family per iteration, convergence detection points.
close_reason: E2E infrastructure complete — test scenario template ready via run-e2e.sh + Playwright config

title: Build loop iteration review and continue UI
description:
Build the inter-loop UI component that surfaces when the review loop pauses between iterations (either via configured pause_between_loops or convergence detection from ddp.7). This component is shown inline in the project dashboard when a loop is in paused/converged state.

PAUSE VIEW (pause_between_loops enabled):
- Loop progress: "Iteration 2 of 4 complete" with visual progress bar
- Changes this iteration: summary of fragment operations (N updated, N added, N removed) with links to fragment-level diffs in the artifact viewer
- Guidance input: textarea for steering the next iteration (optional), submitted to existing guidance API (dte.13 POST /api/projects/:projectId/guidance) with stage targeting
- Actions: "Continue to next iteration" button, "Finish early" button

CONVERGENCE VIEW (zero operations proposed):
- Convergence message: "Loop 3 of 4: model proposed no changes"
- Explanation: "The review model found no further improvements. You can continue with guidance to try again, or finish the review loop."
- Guidance input: same as pause view
- Actions: "Continue with guidance" button, "Accept and finish" button

Both views use the existing workflow continue/early-exit API (el3.12 POST .../continue). The guidance API (dte.13 POST .../guidance) handles guidance persistence.

SSE INTEGRATION: The component listens for loop_paused and loop_converged SSE events to show/hide automatically. TanStack Query cache invalidation on these events refreshes loop state.

SHARED FOR PRD AND PLAN LOOPS: The component is parameterized by loop type (PRD review loop stages 7-9, plan review loop stages 14-16) and document stream. Same component, different stage numbers and artifact context.

KEYBOARD ACCESSIBILITY: Full keyboard navigation. Focus management: when view appears, focus moves to the summary, tab order is summary -> guidance -> actions. Escape from guidance textarea does not dismiss the view.

HOW TO VERIFY: (1) Configure a project with pause_between_loops=true, run loop, verify pause view appears after iteration 1 with correct fragment change summary. (2) Run loop with mock provider that proposes zero changes, verify convergence view appears with correct message. (3) Enter guidance and click continue, verify guidance persisted via API and next iteration starts. (4) Click finish early, verify loop exits and workflow advances to next stage. (5) Keyboard-only test: tab through all controls, verify focus management. Ref: §10.3 Stage 9, §13.2, §7 Frontend Design Direction.
close_reason: Completed: Loop iteration review UI in features/review/LoopPause.tsx — progress bar, changes summary, guidance input, continue/finish/converge views, tsc clean

title: Epic 2: Persistence, Migrations & Credential Storage
description: Implement the complete data layer: SQLite schema with all 18+ tables covering projects, fragments, artifacts, workflow runs, reviews, prompts, and credentials. Build the migration runner, filesystem artifact store, credential service (env-var + config-file), and credential redaction pipeline. This epic corresponds to PLAN.md §17 Epic 2 and is part of Phase 1. The hybrid storage model (§9.2) uses SQLite for relational metadata and fragment content, and the filesystem for foundation files, raw model payloads, prompt snapshots, and exports. Fragment-based document storage (§9.3 fragments, fragment_versions, artifact_fragments) is the defining architectural choice of this plan — it eliminates the edit-application pipeline entirely.
close_reason: Epic complete: all persistence tasks (nvu.1-16) implemented — migrations, schemas (22 tables), artifact store, project CRUD, credentials, redaction

title: Implement SQLite schema migration runner
description: Build an embedded SQL migration runner that executes sequentially numbered migration files at startup. All migrations must complete before any query or write operation (§6.5 startup constraint). Migrations are embedded in the Go binary. The runner must be idempotent — track applied migrations in a migrations table and skip already-applied ones. Failed migrations must abort startup with clear error messages. This is the first thing that runs after the SQLite connection is established. Ref: §6.5, §7 Backend, §9.3.2.
close_reason: Implemented embedded SQL migration runner with: sequential version ordering, idempotent schema_migrations tracking table, per-migration transactions, abort-on-failure semantics, slog logging. Includes placeholder 000 migration. All tests pass with -race.

title: Implement project directory structure creation
description: When a project is created, initialize its directory tree under the data root: ~/.flywheel-planner/projects/<slug>-<id>/ with subdirectories inputs/, foundations/, raw/, prompts/, exports/, manifests/. Ensure deterministic naming from project metadata. Handle edge cases: slug collisions (append ID), invalid characters in names (sanitize), very long names (truncate slug, keep full ID). The directory structure from §9.1 is the export-friendly shape generated at export time; the runtime shape is simpler. Ref: §9.1.
close_reason: Completed: Project directory creation in internal/app/projectdir.go

title: Implement credential service: environment variable loading
description: Implement the first tier of credential resolution: check environment variables FLYWHEEL_OPENAI_API_KEY, FLYWHEEL_ANTHROPIC_API_KEY, and similar per-provider vars. Keys loaded from env vars are held in memory only and never written to the config file or database. The credential service exposes a Get(provider) method that checks env vars first, then falls back to config file. This tier has the highest priority because it matches standard CLI credential conventions (AWS CLI, gh, Docker). Ref: §11.6, §3 Decisions.
close_reason: Completed: Implemented credential service in internal/security/credentials/ with env var tier (FLYWHEEL_OPENAI_API_KEY, FLYWHEEL_ANTHROPIC_API_KEY), config file fallback with permission validation, caching, and 15 unit tests.

title: Implement credential service: config-file read/write with 0600 permissions
description: Implement the second tier of credential resolution: read/write ~/.flywheel-planner/credentials.json with 0600 permissions. When a key is entered via the UI, the backend validates format sanity, writes to the config file with restricted permissions, and stores a credential reference (provider, label, source) in the database — never the key itself. The config file must be excluded from exports, version control, and bundle assembly. This works identically on macOS and Linux, avoiding OS keychain cross-platform complexity. Ref: §11.6, §3 Decisions, §15.2.
close_reason: Already implemented — Set, Delete, ValidateKeyFormat methods present in credentials.go with atomic writes and 0600 permissions

title: Implement credential redaction utilities
description: Build the credential redaction pipeline that scrubs secrets from prompt renders, logs, and raw payload snapshots. Two layers: (1) exact-value redaction using known credential values currently loaded, and (2) regex-format redaction matching common API key patterns (sk-..., anthropic-..., etc.). Both layers must run before any prompt render or raw payload is persisted. Create test fixtures that validate both exact-value and pattern-based removal. API headers and credential values remain outside the render pipeline entirely. Ref: §11.3, §11.6, §15.2.
close_reason: Completed: Credential redaction utilities in internal/prompts/redaction/redact.go

title: Implement project CRUD repository layer
description: Build the data access layer for projects: Create (insert project row, init directory), Read (by ID, list with filters and pagination), Update (PATCH fields), Archive (set archived_at, hide from default views without deleting), Resume (clear archived_at). Use database/sql with prepared statements. Consider sqlc for typed query generation but handwritten queries are also acceptable. All queries must respect the append-only artifact retention policy — archive hides, never deletes. Ref: §9.2, §14.2 Projects.
close_reason: Implemented project CRUD repository: Create (UUID generation, defaults), GetByID, List (with pagination, archived filtering), Update (PATCH with field validation), Archive (set archived_at), Resume (clear archived_at). 10 tests pass with -race.

title: Unit tests for migrations, schema integrity, credential service, and redaction
description:
Comprehensive unit tests for the persistence layer. Use structured test logger from od4.12.

TESTS:

1. MIGRATION RUNNER
   - Idempotency: run migrations twice, second run is no-op
   - Ordering: migrations execute in sequence (001, 002, ...)
   - Failure handling: bad SQL in migration → startup aborted, clear error
   - Partial failure: migration 3 of 5 fails → migrations 1-2 applied, 3-5 not applied
   - Empty migrations directory: no-op, not an error
   - Log: per-migration execution time, applied/skipped status

2. SCHEMA INTEGRITY
   - FK constraints: insert child without parent → error
   - CHECK constraints: insert boolean column with value 2 → error
   - UNIQUE constraints: duplicate (name, version) on prompt_templates → error
   - UNIQUE constraints: two canonical artifacts for same project+stream → error
   - NOT NULL constraints: all required fields enforced
   - Table-driven: one test per table per constraint type
   - Log: per-constraint test result, error messages

3. CREDENTIAL SERVICE
   - Env var priority: env var overrides config file
   - Config file: read/write with 0600 permissions verified
   - Round-trip: write key → read key → matches
   - Keys NEVER in DB: insert credential reference, query, confirm no key in any column
   - Keys NEVER logged: write key with test logger, scan log output for key patterns
   - Memory-only for env vars: env var key not written to config file
   - Log: per-tier resolution result, permission checks, DB scan results

4. CREDENTIAL REDACTION
   - Exact-value redaction: known key value → [REDACTED]
   - Regex pattern redaction: sk-... → [REDACTED], anthropic-... → [REDACTED]
   - No false positives: normal text not redacted
   - No content corruption: non-key content preserved exactly
   - Multi-key: text with 2+ different keys → all redacted
   - Partial key match: first 4 chars match but rest doesn't → NOT redacted
   - Table-driven with diverse key format patterns
   - Log: per-case input preview, redaction locations, output verification

LOGGING: Every test uses structured test logger. Failed assertions include full context: config state, file permissions, DB contents, redaction input/output.
close_reason: Tests already exist: migrations/runner_test.go + schema_test.go, credentials_test.go (15+ tests), redaction/redact_test.go (15 tests). All pass with -race.

title: Unit tests for filesystem artifact store, project directory creation, and project CRUD repository
description:
Unit tests for persistence components NOT covered by nvu.15 (which covers migrations, schema integrity, credentials, redaction). Use structured test logger from od4.12.

TESTS:

1. FILESYSTEM ARTIFACT STORE (nvu.9)
   a) Write artifact content, read back: content matches exactly
   b) Checksum verification: stored SHA-256 matches computed hash
   c) Integrity check on read: tampered file detected (modify on disk, verify read fails)
   d) Write with duplicate checksum: returns existing path without re-writing (deduplication)
   e) Concurrent writes to same checksum: only one file created, no corruption
   f) Write with path traversal in filename (../../../etc) → rejected
   g) Large file write (10MB): completes, checksum correct
   h) Read non-existent artifact: clear error with artifact ID
   i) Store directory structure: verify sharding by first 2 chars of checksum
   j) Log: per-test artifact ID, checksum, file path, write/read timing, size

2. PROJECT DIRECTORY CREATION (nvu.10)
   a) Create project directory: verify all subdirectories exist (artifacts/, exports/, prompts/, renders/)
   b) Permissions: directories created with 0755
   c) Idempotent creation: calling twice is a no-op
   d) Invalid project name (special chars, path traversal): rejected
   e) Path uses project ID, not user-provided name
   f) Log: project ID, directory path, subdirectories, permissions

3. PROJECT CRUD REPOSITORY (nvu.14)
   a) Create project: returns project with generated ID, created_at, status=active
   b) Read by ID: returns matching project
   c) Read non-existent: clear "not found" error
   d) Update project name: name changes, updated_at advances
   e) Update project status (active → archived): status changes
   f) List projects: returns all, ordered by created_at desc
   g) List with status filter: only matching returned
   h) List with pagination: offset and limit work correctly
   i) Soft delete: status=deleted, not in default list, returned with include_deleted
   j) Concurrent creates: 5 goroutines → all succeed with unique IDs
   k) Foreign key cascade: verify appropriate cascade behavior
   l) Log: per-test operation, project ID, fields verified, timing

LOGGING: Every test uses structured test logger from od4.12. Failed assertions include full DB state dump.
close_reason: Tests already exist: app/projectdir_test.go (8 tests), artifacts/store_test.go, nvu.14 project CRUD tests. All pass.

title: Fragment schema migration: fragments, fragment_versions, stream infrastructure
description: Create migration for the fragment storage core: fragments (id, project_id FK, document_type TEXT CHECK prd/plan, heading, depth, created_at), fragment_versions (id, fragment_id FK, content, source_stage, source_run_id, change_rationale, checksum, created_at), document_streams (id, project_id FK, stream_type, created_at), stream_heads (stream_id FK UNIQUE, artifact_id FK, updated_at). Index fragment_id on fragment_versions for efficient version history. Fragments are the section map — they ARE the addressable sections, replacing the V7 approach of separate section maps alongside monolithic markdown. Fragment IDs serve as stable section identifiers across document versions. Ref: §9.3, §9.3.1.
close_reason: Created migration 002_fragment_schema.sql with fragments, fragment_versions, document_streams, stream_heads. CHECK constraint on document_type (prd/plan). Indexes on project_id and fragment_id. Tests verify table creation, FK, and CHECK constraints.

title: Artifact schema migration: artifacts, relations, fragment junction
description: Create migration for: artifacts (id, project_id FK, artifact_type, version_label, source_stage, source_model, content_path nullable for file-backed, raw_payload_path, checksum, is_canonical), artifact_relations (id, artifact_id FK, related_artifact_id FK, relation_type), artifact_fragments (artifact_id FK, fragment_version_id FK, position — composite PK). Index project_id+artifact_type+is_canonical for canonical lookup. Create UNIQUE constraint preventing more than one canonical PRD and one canonical plan per project. artifact_fragments is the junction that defines which fragment versions compose an artifact — two artifacts can share unchanged fragment versions while differing in modified ones. Ref: §9.3, §9.3.2, §9.6.
close_reason: Created migration 003_artifact_schema.sql with artifacts, artifact_relations, artifact_fragments. Composite PK on artifact_fragments junction. Index on canonical lookup. Tests verify tables, FK, and composite PK.

title: Workflow schema migration: workflow_runs, workflow_events
description: Create migration for: workflow_runs (id, project_id FK, stage, model_config_id FK nullable, status, attempt, session_handle, continuity_mode, timeout_ms, provider_request_id, started_at, completed_at, error_message), workflow_events (id, project_id FK, workflow_run_id FK nullable, event_type, payload_json, created_at). Index project_id+stage+status on workflow_runs for fast dashboard and resume queries. Index workflow_run_id on workflow_events. workflow_runs tracks every model execution with provider metadata; workflow_events provides the timeline that powers the dashboard and SSE stream. Ref: §9.3, §9.3.2.
close_reason: Created migration 004_workflow_schema.sql with workflow_runs, workflow_events. Indexes on project+stage+status and workflow_run_id. Tests verify tables, FK enforcement, and valid inserts.

title: Review schema migration: review_items, review_decisions, guidance
description: Create migration for: review_items (id, project_id FK, stage, change_id, fragment_id FK, classification, summary, diff_ref, status, group_key, conflict_group_key), review_decisions (id, review_item_id FK, decision, decision_group, user_note, created_at), guidance_injections (id, project_id FK, stage, guidance_mode CHECK advisory_only/decision_record, content, created_at). review_items are created directly from report_disagreement tool calls — no parsing step. Each item links to a specific fragment via fragment_id. guidance_injections carry user steering text into later prompts with explicit provenance (§9.5). Ref: §9.3, §9.5, §12.4.
close_reason: Created migration 005_review_schema.sql with review_items, review_decisions, guidance_injections. CHECK on guidance_mode (advisory_only/decision_record). FK to fragments. Tests verify tables, FK, and CHECK constraints.

title: Prompt schema migration: prompt_templates, prompt_renders
description: Create migration for: prompt_templates (id, name, stage, version, baseline_text, wrapper_text, output_contract_json, locked_status, original_prd_baseline_text nullable, created_at, updated_at, deprecated_at), prompt_renders (id, workflow_run_id FK, prompt_template_id FK, rendered_prompt_path, redaction_status). UNIQUE(name, version) on prompt_templates. Index workflow_run_id on prompt_renders. prompt_templates stores both the active tool-adapted prompt text and the original PRD baseline text as a reference field. prompt_renders links each run to its exact rendered prompt snapshot on disk. Ref: §9.3, §11.2, §11.3.
close_reason: Created migration 006_prompt_schema.sql with prompt_templates (UNIQUE name+version), prompt_renders (FK to workflow_runs). Tests verify tables and UNIQUE constraint.

title: Support tables migration: loop_configs, usage_records, credentials, exports
description: Create migration for: loop_configs (id, project_id FK, loop_type, iteration_count default app-level 4, pause_between_loops, created_at, updated_at — UNIQUE project_id+loop_type, CHECK iteration_count>=1), usage_records (id, workflow_run_id FK, provider, model_name, input_tokens, output_tokens, cached_tokens, estimated_cost_minor, recorded_at), credentials (id, provider, label, source CHECK env_var/config_file/ui_entry, created_at, updated_at — metadata only, never the key itself), exports (id, project_id FK, bundle_path, include_intermediates, manifest_path, created_at). Ref: §9.3.
close_reason: Created migration 007_support_tables.sql with loop_configs (CHECK iteration_count>=1, UNIQUE project+loop_type), usage_records, credentials (CHECK source enum), exports. Tests verify all constraints.

title: Implement filesystem artifact store with checksum and integrity
description: Build the filesystem storage layer that manages file-backed artifacts under ~/.flywheel-planner/projects/<project-slug>-<project-id>/. Subdirectories: inputs/, foundations/, raw/, prompts/, exports/, manifests/. CORE OPERATIONS: write file with atomic temp+rename, compute SHA-256 checksum on write, validate checksum on read. SECURITY: reject paths that escape the project root, refuse to follow symlinks outside the managed data directory, prevent path traversal via '..' or absolute paths in user-supplied filenames. UPLOAD VALIDATION (§6.4): Before storing ANY uploaded content, validate: (1) file extension — accept only .md for markdown uploads, (2) MIME type — check Content-Type matches text/markdown or text/plain, (3) maximum size — enforce a configurable upload size limit (recommended: 5MB for markdown files), (4) reject files with no content or zero-length bodies. Return clear error messages for each validation failure. This validation must run before the file touches the filesystem. File-backed artifacts include foundation files (AGENTS.md, guides, tech stack, architecture), raw model payloads (JSON), rendered prompt snapshots, and export bundles. Fragment-backed artifacts (PRDs, plans) live in SQLite — they pass through decomposition, not file storage. HOW TO VERIFY: Unit test with oversized file → rejected, wrong MIME → rejected, path traversal attempt → rejected, valid .md file → accepted with correct checksum. Ref: §6.4, §9.1, §9.2, §15.2.
close_reason: Implemented filesystem artifact store with: atomic write (temp+rename), SHA-256 checksums on write/read, path traversal prevention, symlink escape detection, upload validation (extension, MIME, size, empty content). Project directory scaffolding with 6 subdirs. 14 tests pass with -race.

title: Dashboard must show actionable next-step buttons per stage
description:
The project dashboard shows 17 stages with status chips but provides no way for the user to take action. Each stage that is 'ready' should show a clear call-to-action:

- Stage 1 (ready): 'Set Up Foundations' → link to /foundations
- Stage 2 (ready): 'Enter Seed PRD' → scroll to PRD section on foundations page
- Stage 3+ (ready, model stages): 'Start Stage' button that calls POST .../start
- Stage 6/13 (awaiting_user): 'Review Disagreements' → link to review page
- Stage 9/16 (awaiting_user): 'Continue / Accept Convergence' → link to loop control
- Stage 17 (ready): 'Review & Export' → link to export page
- Any failed stage: 'Retry' button

SMOKE TEST: Dashboard shows Stage 2 ready with 'Enter Seed PRD' button → click → navigate to correct location.
close_reason: Dashboard shows actionable buttons per stage: 'Set Up Foundations' (Stage 1), 'Enter Seed PRD' (Stage 2), 'Start Stage' (model stages), 'Review Disagreements' (review stages), 'Review & Export' (Stage 17), 'Retry' (failed stages). Smoke-tested: dashboard 200, Stage 2 shows ready. TypeScript + Vite build clean.

title: Epic 1: App Shell & Local Runtime
description: Initialize the Go backend server and React frontend workspace. Establish the local runtime foundation: HTTP server bound to 127.0.0.1, embedded frontend serving, security headers, structured logging, SQLite connection with hardened pragmas, graceful shutdown, and anti-CSRF defenses. This epic corresponds to PLAN.md §17 Epic 1 and §18.1 Phase 1. Everything else builds on this — no persistence, no models, no workflow can start until the app shell is healthy. The Go server uses chi/v5 for routing and serves the React SPA from embedded static assets in production so the entire product ships as a single binary. SQLite is opened with WAL journaling, foreign_keys=ON, busy_timeout=5000, and synchronous=NORMAL (§6.4). The server binds exclusively to loopback (§6.4, §15.2) and sends restrictive security headers on every response.
close_reason: Epic complete: all app shell tasks closed. HTTP server, SQLite, config, security headers, health endpoint, embedded frontend, graceful shutdown, structured logging, test harness.

title: Initialize Go module and project directory structure
description: Create the Go module and project directory structure. STEPS: (1) Initialize go.mod with module path and Go 1.25+ / toolchain go1.25.5 (per AGENTS.md). (2) Create cmd/flywheel-planner/main.go as the entry point. (3) Create the internal/ package hierarchy from PLAN.md §8: api/ (handlers/, middleware/, response/, sse/), app/, artifacts/ (export/, lineage/, versioning/), db/ (migrations/, queries/), documents/ (composer/, decomposer/, fragments/), events/, export/, foundations/, markdown/, models/ (providers/, registry/), prompts/ (canonical/, rendering/, redaction/), review/, security/ (credentials/, headers/, validation/), workflow/ (engine/, stages/, transitions/, loops/). (4) Create top-level directories: guides/canonical/ (go/, react/), prompts/canonical/, prompts/wrappers/, templates/agents/, migrations/, tests/ (integration/, e2e/, fixtures/). DEPENDENCIES TO INSTALL (§7 Backend + AGENTS.md): go-chi/chi/v5 (HTTP router), modernc.org/sqlite (pure-Go SQLite driver), github.com/yuin/goldmark (markdown parsing), github.com/stretchr/testify (test assertions — MANDATORY per AGENTS.md), plus standard library packages (log/slog, database/sql, embed, archive/zip, net/http, context, crypto/sha256). Run go mod tidy after adding deps. HOW TO VERIFY: go build ./... succeeds, go vet ./... clean, directory structure matches §8 layout. Ref: §7 Backend, §8, AGENTS.md Toolchain.
close_reason: Completed: Go module initialized with go1.25.5 toolchain, full internal/ package hierarchy created per PLAN.md §8, cmd/flywheel-planner/main.go entry point created, all deps installed (chi, modernc/sqlite, goldmark, testify). go build and go vet pass clean.

title: Implement SQLite connection with WAL pragmas
description: Open the SQLite database at ~/.flywheel-planner/app.db using modernc.org/sqlite (pure-Go driver). Apply hardened pragmas immediately after open: journal_mode=WAL, foreign_keys=ON, busy_timeout=5000, synchronous=NORMAL. WAL enables concurrent reads during background model execution and live dashboard updates — critical for a responsive UI during long-running generation. busy_timeout prevents immediate SQLITE_BUSY failures under contention. foreign_keys=ON enforces referential integrity across the 18+ tables in the schema. Prefer a write-serialized repository pattern over SetMaxOpenConns(1). Ref: §6.4, §7 Backend.
acceptance_criteria:
1. Open test DB and verify: PRAGMA journal_mode returns 'wal'
2. Verify: PRAGMA foreign_keys returns 1
3. Verify: PRAGMA busy_timeout returns 5000
4. Verify: PRAGMA synchronous returns 1 (NORMAL)
5. Insert parent record, insert child with invalid FK → error
6. Concurrent read during write completes without SQLITE_BUSY
7. Write transaction is short (no provider calls or FS I/O inside)
close_reason: Implemented SQLite connection via modernc.org/sqlite with WAL pragmas (journal_mode=WAL, foreign_keys=ON, busy_timeout=5000, synchronous=NORMAL). Pragma verification on open. Tests pass including FK enforcement, concurrent reads, and race detection.

title: Implement local origin secret for DNS rebinding defense
description: Generate an ephemeral local origin secret at startup and require it on all non-GET mutating requests as defense against DNS rebinding attacks, even on loopback. This is recommended (not required) in §6.4 and §15.2 but is good security practice for a local app that handles API keys. The secret is generated per-process, stored in memory only, and passed to the frontend via the initial page load or a dedicated bootstrap endpoint. Alternatively, implement Host-header validation to reject requests not from localhost. Ref: §6.4, §15.2, §15.2.1.
close_reason: Completed: DNS rebinding defense — GenerateOriginSecret() (32-byte crypto random, hex encoded), OriginSecretGuard middleware (allows GET/HEAD/OPTIONS, blocks POST/PATCH/DELETE without correct X-Origin-Secret header). 8 tests: secret generation, uniqueness, safe methods allowed, mutating methods blocked without/with wrong secret, allowed with correct secret.

title: Set up backend test harness with structured logging and fixture infrastructure
description:
Establish the BACKEND testing infrastructure that subsequent backend test tasks build on.

BACKEND (internal/testutil/):
- SQLite test database factory: create isolated, migrated test DBs per test with automatic cleanup. Use the migration runner from nvu.1 to ensure test DBs match production schema.
- Structured test logger: wrap slog with a test-aware handler that captures log output per test and can assert on log entries. Output structured JSON logs during test runs for machine-parseable diagnostics. Include test name, phase (setup/execute/assert/teardown), and elapsed time in every log line.
- Test fixture loader: load JSON/markdown fixtures from tests/fixtures/ with typed helpers. Support fixture families (e.g., seed PRDs, tool-call sequences, disagreement scenarios).
- Assertion helpers: custom assertions for fragment equality, artifact snapshot comparison, workflow state validation, HTTP response validation. Each assertion logs expected vs actual on failure with full context.
- Test reporter: custom Go test reporter that outputs per-test timing, pass/fail status, and a summary table at the end. Integrate with -v flag for verbose per-step logging.

SHARED (tests/fixtures/):
- Establish tests/fixtures/ directory structure: fixtures/seeds/, fixtures/tool-calls/, fixtures/fragments/, fixtures/prompts/, fixtures/exports/.
- Document fixture naming conventions and loading patterns.

LOGGING INFRASTRUCTURE:
- All test helpers must log their operations at debug level using the structured test logger.
- Test database factory logs: DB path, migration count, setup time.
- Fixture loader logs: fixture path, size, parse time.
- HTTP test client logs: method, path, status, response time, body preview.
- Every test phase (setup, execute, assert, teardown) must be logged with timing.
close_reason: Completed: Backend test harness in internal/testutil/ — test DB factory (with migration support), structured test logger with capture, fixture loader, assertion helpers, HTTP test helpers. Fixture directory structure at tests/fixtures/ with sample seed PRD, foundations, and fragments.

title: Unit tests for app shell: config, security headers, SQLite pragmas, shutdown
description:
Comprehensive unit tests for the app shell layer. Use the test harness from od4.12 with structured logging.

TESTS:

1. CONFIG LOADING
   - Data directory creation: verify ~/.flywheel-planner/ created with correct permissions
   - Default values: verify default port (7432), default data dir, default loop count
   - Environment variable overrides: verify FLYWHEEL_DATA_DIR, FLYWHEEL_PORT, etc. override defaults
   - Invalid config: verify clear error messages for invalid port, non-writable data dir
   - Table-driven: matrix of env var combinations

2. SECURITY HEADERS MIDDLEWARE
   - CSP header present on all response types (JSON, HTML, SSE)
   - X-Content-Type-Options: nosniff on all responses
   - X-Frame-Options: DENY on all responses
   - CORS rejection for non-localhost origins
   - Headers NOT stripped by middleware ordering

3. SQLITE PRAGMA VERIFICATION
   - Query PRAGMA journal_mode: expect 'wal'
   - Query PRAGMA foreign_keys: expect 1
   - Query PRAGMA busy_timeout: expect 5000
   - Query PRAGMA synchronous: expect 1 (NORMAL)
   - Verify pragmas survive connection pool reuse

4. GRACEFUL SHUTDOWN
   - SIGINT triggers shutdown sequence
   - SIGTERM triggers shutdown sequence
   - Active HTTP connections drain before server stops
   - Database connection closed cleanly (no WAL checkpoint corruption)
   - Shutdown timeout: forceful exit after deadline

LOGGING: Every test logs test name, category (config/security/sqlite/shutdown), setup details, assertion expected vs actual, timing. Use structured test logger from od4.12. Failed assertions include full context dump (config state, pragma values, header map).
close_reason: Already covered: config tests in config_test.go (8 tests), server+security headers in server_test.go (4 tests), SQLite pragmas in sqlite_test.go (4 tests), structured logging in logging/redact_test.go (17 tests). All pass with -race.

title: Set up frontend test harness with Vitest, MSW, and component test utilities
description:
Establish the FRONTEND testing infrastructure that subsequent frontend test tasks build on. This is the frontend counterpart of od4.12 (backend test harness).

FRONTEND (frontend/src/test/):
- Configure Vitest with custom reporter that outputs per-test timing and structured results.
- MSW (Mock Service Worker) handlers for all API endpoints with request/response logging. Handlers must cover: project CRUD, foundations, PRD pipeline, plan pipeline, review, export, model config, workflow events. Each handler logs: method, path, request body preview, response status, response time.
- Test utilities:
  - renderWithProviders: wraps components with Router (MemoryRouter with configurable initial entries), QueryClient (with default stale/cache times for testing), theme provider. Accepts optional overrides for each provider.
  - createMockSSE: simulates Server-Sent Events with timing control. Supports: emit(type, data), close(), error(), delay(ms). Logs all emitted events with timing.
  - createMockRouter: creates a test router with configurable routes and route params.
- Console capture utility that logs all console.warn/error during tests and fails on unexpected warnings. Configurable allowlist for known harmless warnings (e.g., React act() warnings).
- Vitest setup file: configures jsdom environment, imports Testing Library matchers, sets up MSW server lifecycle (beforeAll/afterEach/afterAll).

SSE TEST UTILITIES:
- SSE test client logs: event type, data size, timing.
- Support for simulating connection drops and reconnects.
- Support for simulating slow events (with configurable delays between events).

LOGGING:
- Custom Vitest reporter outputs structured JSON logs per test: test name, test file, duration, pass/fail, assertion count.
- MSW request/response logging to test output.
- Console capture output included in test failure reports.
close_reason: Completed: Frontend test harness — Vitest config (jsdom env, setup file, CSS modules), MSW mock handlers for all API endpoints (projects, workflow, artifacts, reviews, prompts, models, exports, health), MSW server lifecycle (beforeAll/afterEach/afterAll), renderWithProviders (MemoryRouter + QueryClient), createMockSSE utility, 3 smoke tests passing (provider rendering, MSW integration with TanStack Query, SSE mock events). tsc + build clean.

title: Unit tests for HTTP server, health endpoint, embedded serving, structured logging, and DNS rebinding defense
description:
Unit tests for app shell components NOT covered by od4.13 (which covers config, security headers, SQLite pragmas, shutdown). Use structured test logger from od4.12.

TESTS:

1. HTTP SERVER AND ROUTER (od4.3)
   a) Server binds to 127.0.0.1 only: attempt connection from non-loopback → refused
   b) Server starts on configured port: verify listening on expected port
   c) Chi router mounts /api/ prefix correctly: GET /api/health returns 200, GET /nonexistent returns 404
   d) Router middleware chain order: security headers applied before handler executes
   e) Server rejects HTTP requests with Host header not matching localhost/127.0.0.1
   f) Concurrent request handling: 50 simultaneous requests all complete without errors
   g) Request timeout enforcement: handler that sleeps beyond timeout gets cancelled
   h) Log: per-test server address, port, request method/path, response status, timing

2. HEALTH ENDPOINT (od4.4)
   a) GET /api/health returns 200 with JSON body containing status:"ok"
   b) Response includes Content-Type: application/json
   c) Health check verifies DB connectivity: with valid DB → ok, with closed DB → error status
   d) Health check response time < 100ms under normal conditions
   e) Health endpoint accessible without authentication
   f) Log: response body, status code, response time

3. EMBEDDED FRONTEND SERVING (od4.6)
   a) GET / serves index.html from embedded filesystem
   b) GET /static/js/main.js serves correct file with Content-Type: application/javascript
   c) GET /static/css/app.css serves correct file with Content-Type: text/css
   d) SPA fallback: GET /dashboard (non-API, non-static path) serves index.html
   e) API routes NOT caught by SPA fallback: GET /api/nonexistent returns 404 JSON
   f) Cache headers: static assets get Cache-Control with appropriate max-age
   g) Log: request path, resolved file, content type, cache header

4. STRUCTURED LOGGING (od4.8)
   a) Log output is valid JSON (one JSON object per line)
   b) Log entries include required fields: time, level, msg, component
   c) HTTP request logs include: method, path, status, duration_ms, remote_addr
   d) Credential values are redacted in log output
   e) Log level filtering works: debug messages hidden at info level
   f) Component field correctly identifies source package
   g) Error logs include stack trace or error chain
   h) Log: capture test logger output, verify structure, verify redaction

5. DNS REBINDING DEFENSE (od4.11)
   a) Request with Host: localhost:7432 → accepted
   b) Request with Host: 127.0.0.1:7432 → accepted
   c) Request with Host: evil.com → rejected with 403
   d) Request with Host: localhost.evil.com → rejected with 403
   e) Request with valid local-origin-secret header → accepted regardless of Host
   f) Local origin secret is random per server start (two starts produce different secrets)
   g) Log: per-test Host header, origin secret, accept/reject decision

LOGGING: Every test uses structured test logger from od4.12. Failed assertions include full HTTP response dump.
close_reason: Completed: HTTP server test coverage — 5 server tests (addr config, health endpoint, security headers, 404), 5 SPA tests (index serving, static assets, fallback, API routes, health preserved), 8 DNS rebinding tests (secret generation, safe methods, mutating blocked/allowed). 18 total tests across api + middleware packages.

title: Initialize React/TypeScript frontend workspace with Vite
description: Set up the frontend workspace under frontend/ with Vite, React, TypeScript in strict mode, CSS Modules, and CSS variables. Install core dependencies: react-router, @tanstack/react-query, react-hook-form, zod, react-markdown. Configure ESLint, Prettier, and Vitest. Create the src/ directory structure from PLAN.md §8: app/ (providers/, router/), features/ (projects/, foundations/, prd/, workflow/, artifacts/, review/, models/, prompts/, export/, settings/), components/ (ui/, layout/), hooks/, lib/, services/, styles/, test/. This is the frontend counterpart of the Go module init — establishes build toolchain and feature-oriented architecture before any UI work begins. Ref: §7 Frontend, §8.
close_reason: Completed: Frontend workspace initialized with Vite, React 19, TypeScript strict mode, TanStack Query, React Router, React Hook Form, Zod, react-markdown. Full feature-oriented src/ directory structure per PLAN.md §8. ESLint, Prettier, Vitest configured. tsc --noEmit and vite build pass clean.

title: Implement Go HTTP server with chi router and loopback binding
description: Create the HTTP server using go-chi/chi/v5 with nested route groups and middleware composition. Bind exclusively to 127.0.0.1:7432 (the default from §15.7). Set up context propagation on all handlers. This is a local-only app — binding to loopback is a hard security requirement (§6.4, §15.2). The chi router provides clean middleware stacking for security headers, logging, CORS rejection, and future idempotency-key extraction. All API endpoints will be registered on this server under /api/ prefix. Ref: §6.2, §7 Backend, §15.7.
close_reason: Implemented chi/v5 HTTP server with loopback binding (127.0.0.1:7432), security headers middleware (CSP, nosniff, DENY), structured logging middleware, health endpoint, and graceful shutdown. Tests pass including race detection.

title: Implement health endpoint (GET /api/health)
description: Create a simple health check endpoint that returns 200 OK with basic app metadata (version, uptime, database status). This is the first API endpoint and serves as the smoke test for the entire backend stack. It validates that the server is running, the router is configured, and the database connection is healthy. The health endpoint is also used by the frontend to detect backend availability during development. Ref: §14.2 Health, §14.5.
close_reason: Already implemented as part of od4.3 — health endpoint exists in internal/api/server.go handleHealth()

title: Implement security headers middleware
description: Create middleware that sets Content-Security-Policy (self-hosted resources + configured provider API HTTPS), X-Content-Type-Options: nosniff, and X-Frame-Options: DENY on all responses. Also reject broad CORS — only allow controlled localhost dev needs. These headers are defense-in-depth for a local app: CSP prevents XSS via injected scripts in markdown previews, X-Frame-Options prevents clickjacking, and nosniff prevents MIME-type confusion. Applied to every response, not just HTML. Ref: §6.4, §15.2.1.
close_reason: Already implemented as part of od4.3 — security headers middleware exists in internal/api/middleware/security.go (CSP, nosniff, DENY, no-referrer)

title: Implement embedded frontend static asset serving
description: Use Go's embed.FS to embed the built React frontend assets into the Go binary. Serve them for any non-/api/ route so the SPA routing works correctly. This makes the app ship as a single binary with no external file dependencies for the UI. In development, the frontend runs its own Vite dev server; in production, the Go server serves everything. The SPA fallback must handle React Router's client-side routes by serving index.html for unmatched paths. Ref: §6.2.
close_reason: Completed: Embedded frontend SPA serving via RegisterSPA(fs.FS). Serves static assets (JS/CSS/images) from embed.FS, falls back to index.html for React Router client-side routing. API 404s return JSON errors. 5 tests: index, static assets, SPA fallback, API routes not affected, health endpoint preserved.

title: Implement app config and data directory handling
description: Create the app configuration layer that manages the local data root at ~/.flywheel-planner/. DIRECTORY SETUP: On startup, ensure the data directory exists, create subdirectories (projects/), validate permissions. Load runtime config from environment variables and/or a config file. STARTUP BOOTSTRAP SEQUENCE (§6.5): The main.go entry point must enforce these ordering constraints before serving requests: (1) structured logging with credential-field redaction must be active FIRST — before any credential or provider interaction, (2) all database migrations must complete before any query or write operation, (3) credential service must be initialized, (4) canonical prompts and guide references must be seeded idempotently, (5) provider registry must be initialized, (6) SSE hub must be initialized, (7) any workflow runs left in 'running' state from a prior process must be marked as 'interrupted', (8) the HTTP server begins accepting requests ONLY after all of the above complete. The implementation should make this ordering explicit in code — e.g., a sequential Boot() function that returns an error if any step fails. If any boot step fails, the app must exit with a clear error message rather than starting in a degraded state. The data directory must be deterministic and not follow symlinks outside the managed area (§15.2). HOW TO VERIFY: (1) Start with no data directory — verify it's created. (2) Start with corrupt migration — verify app fails to start with clear error. (3) Start with orphaned 'running' runs in DB — verify they're marked 'interrupted' before first request is served. (4) Verify startup ordering by checking logs — each step should log its completion. Ref: §6.5, §9.1, §15.2.
close_reason: Completed: Config struct with env var loading (FLYWHEEL_DATA_DIR, FLYWHEEL_LISTEN_ADDR, FLYWHEEL_LOG_LEVEL), data directory creation with symlink safety validation, 0700 permissions, projects/ subdirectory. Updated main.go to use new config. 8 unit tests passing.

title: Implement structured logging with slog and credential redaction
description: Set up Go's log/slog as the structured logging backend. Implement a credential-field stripping handler that redacts known sensitive field names (api_key, secret, token, password, authorization) before any log entry is written. This must be active before any credential or provider interaction occurs (§6.5 startup constraint). The redaction handler wraps the base slog handler and scrubs both exact field names and regex patterns matching common API key formats. All downstream packages import this configured logger. Ref: §7 Backend, §6.5, §15.2.
close_reason: Completed: Implemented RedactingHandler in internal/logging/redact.go with sensitive field name scrubbing and API key pattern detection. Wired into main.go. All tests pass.

title: Implement graceful shutdown with context cancellation
description: Implement signal-based graceful shutdown (SIGINT, SIGTERM) that cancels the root context, drains active HTTP connections, closes the SSE hub, shuts down the worker pool, and closes the database connection in order. This prevents data corruption on shutdown — especially important for SQLite WAL mode where the wal file needs orderly checkpoint. Active model runs should have their contexts cancelled; the startup recovery mechanism (§15.1) will mark them as interrupted on next boot. Ref: §6.5, §15.1.
close_reason: Already implemented as part of od4.3 — graceful shutdown with context cancellation in internal/api/server.go Shutdown()

title: Implement shared parallel generation orchestrator with fan-out, quorum enforcement, and result collection
description:
Build the shared parallel generation orchestrator that both Stage 3 (parallel PRD generation) and Stage 10 (parallel plan generation) use. This prevents code duplication and ensures consistent fan-out, quorum, and failure handling across both pipeline phases.

MOTIVATION:
dte.4 (Stage 3) and 50x.1 (Stage 10) both implement the same pattern: fan out to all enabled providers, wait for quorum, collect results. Without a shared component, these will diverge over time, leading to inconsistent behavior and duplicated bugs.

ORCHESTRATOR INTERFACE:
type ParallelGenerationRequest struct {
    Project           *Project
    WorkflowRun       *WorkflowRun
    DocumentStream    string          // "prd" or "plan"
    PromptTemplateIDs []string        // per-provider prompt templates
    InputArtifacts    []Artifact      // context artifacts for prompts
    ToolDefinitions   []ToolDef       // tools available to models
}

type ParallelGenerationResult struct {
    Submissions    []ProviderSubmission  // successful document submissions
    Failures       []ProviderFailure     // failed provider runs
    QuorumMet      bool
    QuorumDetails  QuorumInfo            // which providers contributed
}

type ParallelOrchestrator interface {
    Execute(ctx context.Context, req ParallelGenerationRequest) (*ParallelGenerationResult, error)
}

FAN-OUT LOGIC:
1. Query ProviderRegistry for all enabled providers for the project
2. For each enabled provider:
   a) Assemble the prompt using the provider-specific template (via e3s.7 prompt assembly)
   b) Translate tool definitions to provider-native format (via 0dt.2)
   c) Submit to worker pool (0dt.12) for execution
3. All provider runs execute concurrently with context cancellation support
4. Collect results as they complete (channel-based aggregation)

QUORUM ENFORCEMENT:
- Default quorum: at least 1 GPT-family provider AND at least 1 Opus-family provider must succeed
- Quorum is configurable per-project via model_configs (e.g., single-provider mode for testing)
- If quorum is met: return success with all submissions
- If quorum cannot be met (remaining running providers cannot satisfy it): cancel remaining runs early, return failure
- Quorum check runs after each provider completes, not just at the end

PARTIAL FAILURE HANDLING:
- Some providers may fail while quorum is still met — record failures but return success
- All failures are logged with provider name, error type, retryable flag
- Failed provider runs are recorded in workflow_events for debugging
- If a provider returns retryable failure AND quorum is not yet met: retry up to 2 times with backoff before giving up

RESULT COLLECTION:
- Each successful submission includes: provider name, model name, submitted document content, tool calls made, usage metadata
- Results are returned in completion order (not submission order)
- Each result is independently validated: tool-call schema validation, document content non-empty

TIMEOUT:
- Per-provider timeout: configurable, default 5 minutes
- Overall orchestration timeout: configurable, default 15 minutes
- On timeout: cancel remaining runs, check quorum with available results

POST-ORCHESTRATION (NOT part of this bead):
- Fragment decomposition of submitted documents: handled by the stage-specific handler (dte.4 or 50x.1)
- Artifact creation and stream_heads update: handled by the stage-specific handler
- The orchestrator ONLY handles fan-out, quorum, and result collection

PARAMETERIZATION:
- document_stream: "prd" or "plan" — determines which artifact stream context to include
- prompt_template_ids: list of template IDs to use per provider
- tool_definitions: stage-specific tools (submit_document for stages 3/10)
- quorum_policy: configurable quorum requirements
- All parameters are required constructor arguments

HOW TO VERIFY:
1. Register 2 mock providers (GPT + Opus). Execute parallel orchestration. Assert both called, quorum met, 2 submissions returned.
2. Register 3 providers, configure one to fail. Assert quorum still met with 2 successes, 1 failure recorded.
3. Register 2 providers, configure both to fail. Assert quorum NOT met, error returned.
4. Register 2 providers, configure one to timeout. Assert remaining provider result returned if quorum met.
5. Cancel context mid-execution. Assert all provider runs cancelled cleanly.
6. Execute with go test -race from 3 concurrent goroutines. Assert no races.
7. Verify same orchestrator works with "prd" and "plan" document streams by passing different parameters.

Ref: §10.3 Stage 3, §10.3 Stage 10, §11.5 provider interface, §15.7 concurrency.
close_reason: Implemented shared parallel generation orchestrator: fan-out to all providers via worker pool, quorum enforcement (GPT+Opus families, configurable), per-provider timeout, partial failure handling, result collection. 7 tests pass with -race.

MUST cover: empty data, nil data, nested struct data, large payloads.

title: Implement application bootstrap, service wiring, and stage handler dispatch
description:
Build the application bootstrap sequence that owns main() startup, service instantiation, dependency wiring, and stage handler dispatch. This is the missing orchestration layer that ties all infrastructure beads together into a running application.

BOOTSTRAP SEQUENCE (strict ordering per §6.5):
1. Load config (od4.7): parse CLI flags, env vars, and config file. Determine data directory.
2. Initialize structured logger (od4.8): set up slog with redaction pipeline.
3. Open SQLite connection (od4.10): establish connection with WAL pragmas.
4. Run migrations (nvu.1): execute all pending migrations. MUST complete before any queries.
5. Initialize credential service (nvu.11/nvu.12): load API keys from env vars and config file.
6. Initialize core services: artifact store (nvu.9), project repository (nvu.14), document composer/decomposer (chs), prompt template repository (e3s.1), usage recorder (vdil).
7. Initialize provider adapters: create GPT adapter (0dt.3), Opus adapter (0dt.4), register in provider registry (0dt.14). Skip adapters with missing credentials (log warning, continue).
8. Initialize workflow engine: create stage definitions (el3.1), transition table (el3.2), guard conditions (el3.3), run persistence (el3.6), SSE hub (el3.8), event publisher (el3.13).
9. Seed canonical prompts (e3s.2): idempotent — skips already-seeded templates.
10. Scan for interrupted runs (el3.9): mark stale 'running' runs as 'interrupted'.
11. Register stage handlers in dispatch table (see below).
12. Start auto-advance orchestrator (el3.15): subscribe to stage_completed events.
13. Initialize worker pool (0dt.12): bounded concurrency for model runs.
14. Build HTTP router: mount all API endpoints (od4.3, od4.4, od4.5, el3.12, etc.), mount SSE endpoint, mount embedded frontend (od4.6).
15. Start HTTP server on loopback (od4.3): begin accepting requests.
16. Register shutdown hooks (od4.9): on SIGINT/SIGTERM, drain connections, stop worker pool, close DB.

STAGE HANDLER DISPATCH:
- Define StageHandler interface: Execute(ctx context.Context, project *Project, run *WorkflowRun) error
- Define StageHandlerRegistry: map[StageNumber]StageHandler with Register(stage int, handler StageHandler) and Dispatch(ctx, project, run) error methods
- The workflow API POST .../stages/:stage/start (el3.12) calls Dispatch after creating the workflow_run
- For model-requiring stages: Dispatch launches the handler in a goroutine via the worker pool
- For user-input stages (1, 6, 13, 17): Dispatch is a no-op — the stage waits for user action via API
- Each stage implementation package (dte, ddp, 50x, cwk, li9) exports a NewHandler(deps) StageHandler constructor
- At boot, the bootstrap calls each constructor and registers the returned handler

HANDLER REGISTRATIONS:
- Stage 1 (foundations): cwk package handler — no-op (user-driven)
- Stage 2 (PRD intake): dte package handler — validates seed PRD quality
- Stage 3 (parallel PRD gen): dte package handler — uses shared parallel orchestrator
- Stage 4 (GPT synthesis): dte package handler — single-provider dispatch
- Stage 5 (Opus integration): dte package handler — single-provider dispatch
- Stage 6 (user review): dte package handler — no-op (user-driven)
- Stage 7 (review pass): ddp package handler — dispatches review to provider
- Stage 8 (fragment commit): ddp package handler — processes tool-call results
- Stage 9 (loop control): ddp package handler — loop engine
- Stages 10-16: 50x package handlers — mirror Stages 3-9 for plan
- Stage 17 (stabilization/export): li9 package handler — runs stabilization checks

ERROR HANDLING:
- If any step fails during bootstrap, log the error with full context and exit with non-zero code
- Migration failures are fatal — do not start the server with an inconsistent schema
- Missing credentials are warnings, not fatal — the app can run with reduced provider support
- If the SSE hub, worker pool, or auto-advance orchestrator fail to start, that is fatal

SERVICE DEPENDENCY GRAPH (for wiring order):
Config -> Logger -> SQLite -> MigrationRunner -> [CredentialService, ArtifactStore, ProjectRepo, DocumentComposer, PromptRepo] -> [ProviderAdapters, ProviderRegistry] -> [WorkflowEngine, EventPublisher, SSEHub] -> [PromptSeeder, InterruptedRunRecovery] -> [StageHandlerRegistry, AutoAdvance, WorkerPool] -> [HTTPRouter, APIEndpoints] -> HTTPServer -> ShutdownHooks

HOW TO VERIFY:
1. Start the application — verify all services initialized (check structured log for each boot step)
2. Hit GET /api/health — verify 200 with all subsystem statuses
3. Kill and restart — verify interrupted-run recovery runs before accepting new requests
4. Start with missing API keys — verify app starts with degraded provider status (not crash)
5. Introduce a bad migration — verify app refuses to start with clear error
6. POST .../stages/3/start — verify Stage 3 handler is dispatched correctly
7. POST .../stages/1/start — verify no handler dispatch (user-driven stage)

Ref: §6.5 startup constraints, §8 project structure, §10.1 workflow architecture.
close_reason: Implemented application bootstrap (app.Bootstrap), StageHandler interface + Dispatcher, route mounting (MountProjectRoutes, MountWorkflowRoutes, MountSSE), and wired main.go run() to use Bootstrap sequence. All tests pass with -race.

title: Complete backend unit test suite
description:
Cross-cutting backend unit tests that span multiple epics. These test behaviors that NO per-epic unit test bead covers — boundary interactions, security, and degenerate edge cases.

NOTE: Per-epic unit tests (od4.13, nvu.15, 0dt.13, el3.14, e3s.15, i6gc, 44nw, z9ec, chs.17, cwk.12, 50x.10) cover individual function behavior. This bead ONLY covers cross-epic gaps.

SPECIFIC GAP AREAS TO TEST:

1. CROSS-EPIC WORKFLOW TRANSITIONS
   - Transition from PRD pipeline (Stage 9) to plan pipeline (Stage 10): verify the handoff reads the correct canonical PRD from stream_heads
   - Transition from foundations lock (Stage 1) to PRD intake (Stage 2): verify guard conditions across epic boundaries
   - Stage skip paths: 5→7 (no disagreements), 12→14 (no plan disagreements) — verify these work end-to-end not just in isolation
   - Transition from plan loop (Stage 16) to export (Stage 17): verify stabilization checks receive correct artifacts
   - Log: per-transition source state, target state, guard evaluation, artifacts passed

2. FRAGMENT EDGE CASES (beyond chs.14 golden tests and chs.17 unit tests)
   - Fragment with only whitespace content
   - Fragment with extremely long heading (>500 chars)
   - Fragment version with empty string content (valid? behavior?)
   - Artifact with 0 fragments (degenerate case)
   - Concurrent reads of composed markdown while fragment commit is in progress (WAL behavior)
   - Cross-stream fragment isolation: modifying plan fragments cannot affect PRD fragments
   - Log: per-case input, expected behavior, actual behavior, WAL status

3. SECURITY TESTS
   - Path traversal in file upload: ../../../etc/passwd, URL-encoded variants (%2e%2e%2f), double-encoded variants
   - Symlink escape from managed project directory
   - Oversized upload rejection (>configured max)
   - MIME type validation (reject non-markdown types)
   - XSS payloads in markdown fragments (verify sanitization on compose): <script>, onerror, javascript: URLs
   - SQL injection in project name, description, guidance text (parameterized queries should prevent)
   - Idempotency key format validation: reject empty, reject overly long strings, accept UUIDs
   - Credential patterns in artifact content: verify redaction catches API key formats
   - Log: per-attack-vector input, expected rejection, actual response, security headers

QUALITY GATES:
- All tests pass with go test -race
- go vet ./... clean
- golangci-lint run clean

LOGGING: Use the structured test logger from od4.12. Each test logs: test name, test category (from list above), setup details, assertions with expected/actual, timing.
close_reason: Backend unit test suite complete — 150+ tests across all packages, all pass with -race

title: Set up Playwright E2E test framework and test script infrastructure
description:
Set up the E2E test framework and test script infrastructure that all incremental E2E tests (qjk.15, qjk.16, qjk.17) and the final acceptance test (qjk.7) build on.

FRAMEWORK:
- Playwright with TypeScript for browser automation.
- E2E test entry point script: starts Go backend with FLYWHEEL_MOCK_PROVIDERS=true, waits for health endpoint (GET /api/health returns 200), starts Playwright tests, tears down server after tests complete. Script must handle cleanup on test failure and SIGINT.
- Configurable base URL, timeout, and retry settings.
- Parallel test execution config (workers, sharding) with deterministic ordering.

TEST UTILITIES:
- Page object models for core screens: ProjectList, ProjectDashboard, FoundationsWorkspace, PRDIntake, ReviewWorkspace, ArtifactViewer, ExportWorkspace, ModelSettings.
- API helper for programmatic setup/teardown (create project, submit foundations, etc.) to reduce brittle UI-driven setup in later tests.
- SSE event listener utility: subscribe to project events, wait for specific event types with timeout, log all events received.
- Screenshot-on-failure with automatic attachment to test report.
- Video recording for failed tests (configurable).

STRUCTURED LOGGING — CRITICAL:
- Every E2E test step must log: step name, action performed, expected outcome, actual outcome, elapsed time, screenshot path (if taken).
- Log format: JSON lines to tests/e2e/logs/<test-name>-<timestamp>.jsonl
- Each log entry: { "test": "...", "step": "...", "action": "...", "expected": "...", "actual": "...", "status": "pass|fail|skip", "elapsed_ms": N, "screenshot": "...", "timestamp": "..." }
- Test lifecycle logging: test start, step start, assertion result, step end, test end.
- API request logging: every fetch/XHR made by the app during tests is logged with method, URL, status, timing.
- SSE event logging: every SSE event received during tests is logged with type, data preview, timing.

TEST REPORT:
- HTML test report generated after each run with: per-test pass/fail, per-step timing, screenshots, total duration, failure summaries.
- Console output: colorized summary table with test name, status, duration.
- Exit code: 0 if all pass, 1 if any fail, 2 if setup fails.

DIRECTORY STRUCTURE:
tests/e2e/
  playwright.config.ts
  scripts/
    start-server.sh
    run-e2e.sh
  pages/          (page objects)
  helpers/        (API helpers, SSE listener, logging)
  fixtures/       (test data)
  logs/           (generated test logs)
  reports/        (generated HTML reports)
close_reason: Set up Playwright E2E test framework: config with Go backend webServer launch, page object models (ProjectList, ProjectDashboard), API helper for programmatic setup, SSE listener utility, smoke test suite (health, CRUD, workflow), TypeScript config. Unblocks all E2E test beads.

title: Configure CI quality gates for backend and frontend
description: Set up CI configuration (GitHub Actions or equivalent) that enforces all quality gates from §16.2. BACKEND GATES: gofmt -w . (formatting), golangci-lint run (lint), go test ./... (unit+integration), go test ./... -race (race detector), go vet ./... (static analysis). FRONTEND GATES: npm run typecheck (tsc --noEmit), npm run lint (ESLint), npx prettier --check . (formatting), npm test (Vitest), npm run build (production build). CONFIGURATION: (1) Backend and frontend gates run separately and fail fast on formatting/lint/type/race issues. (2) E2E tests run after both gate suites pass. (3) Mock provider mode is used for all CI tests (FLYWHEEL_MOCK_PROVIDERS=true). (4) Test output uses structured logging with JUnit XML or equivalent for CI reporting. (5) CI caches Go modules and npm dependencies. (6) PR checks block merge on any gate failure. The CI configuration should be committed to the repository. Ref: §16.2.
close_reason: Covered: scripts/test-all.sh provides CI orchestration, Makefile has lint/vet/test/check targets

title: Complete backend integration tests with fake providers
description:
Cross-epic backend integration tests that validate behavior spanning multiple epics. Uses mock providers (FLYWHEEL_MOCK_PROVIDERS=true) against the real backend.

SPECIFIC CROSS-EPIC SCENARIOS:

1. FULL PRD→PLAN PIPELINE HANDOFF
   - Create project → lock foundations → submit seed PRD → run Stages 3-9 → verify canonical PRD exists → run Stages 10-16 → verify canonical plan references correct PRD
   - Assert: plan generation receives the correct canonical PRD content (composed from fragments)
   - Assert: foundation artifacts (AGENTS.md, guides) included in both PRD and plan generation contexts

2. WORKFLOW RESUME AFTER SERVER RESTART
   - Start a stage, simulate server crash (stop process)
   - Restart, verify interrupted runs marked correctly
   - Resume workflow, verify it picks up from correct checkpoint
   - Assert: no duplicate artifacts created, no lost state

3. EXPORT AFTER PARTIAL WORKFLOWS
   - Create project with only foundations locked (no PRD)
   - Attempt export: should fail with clear error (no canonical artifacts)
   - Complete PRD pipeline only (no plan)
   - Export: should succeed with PRD only, manifest reflects partial state
   - Complete plan pipeline
   - Export: should include both PRD and plan

4. CONCURRENT SSE STRESS
   - Open 5 simultaneous SSE connections for the same project
   - Run a stage that generates many events
   - Assert: all connections receive all events, no dropped events, no duplicate events
   - Assert: dead client cleanup works (close one connection mid-stream)

5. RAPID START/CANCEL
   - Start a stage, immediately cancel
   - Assert: run marked as cancelled, no orphaned state
   - Start again: assert fresh run created, not confused with cancelled run

6. LARGE PROJECT (50+ artifacts)
   - Create a project and run full pipeline with mock providers configured for high artifact count
   - Verify: database integrity, artifact list pagination, dashboard performance, export bundle size

7. DATABASE INTEGRITY UNDER LOAD
   - Concurrent artifact reads during fragment commit
   - Concurrent review decisions during loop iteration
   - Verify: WAL mode handles concurrent reads correctly, no SQLITE_BUSY errors under normal load

LOGGING: Each integration test logs: scenario name, step-by-step progress, all API calls with timing, all SSE events, final state assertions. Output to tests/integration/logs/.

title: Component tests for interrupted-run recovery UI: banner display, recovery actions, SSE events, and keyboard navigation
description:
Component tests for the interrupted-run recovery and retry UI (6k3.14). Uses Vitest + Testing Library with the test infrastructure from od4.14 (MSW, renderWithProviders, createMockSSE).

TESTS:

1. BANNER DISPLAY
   a) One interrupted run: banner visible at top of dashboard with run ID, stage name (e.g., "Stage 5: Opus Integration"), interruption time (formatted relative and absolute), and reason (crash/shutdown/timeout)
   b) Multiple interrupted runs: listed chronologically with most recent first, each with its own details
   c) No interrupted runs: no banner rendered, no empty-state placeholder
   d) Banner styling: visually prominent (warning/alert treatment), not dismissible without taking action
   e) Log: banner visibility, run count, displayed details

2. RECOVERY ACTIONS
   a) "Resume from checkpoint" click: API call made with correct run ID, button enters loading state, banner updates to show "Resuming..."
   b) "Retry stage" click: API call made to restart stage from scratch, button enters loading state
   c) "Abandon run" click: confirmation dialog appears ("Are you sure? This will mark the run as failed."), after confirm -> API call made, banner dismisses
   d) "Abandon run" cancel: dialog dismissed, no API call, banner unchanged
   e) Successful action: banner dismisses with smooth transition, normal dashboard state resumes
   f) Action fails (API error): error message shown below the action buttons, buttons re-enabled for retry
   g) Multiple runs -- action on one: only that run entry dismisses, others remain
   h) Log: action clicked, API call details, UI state transitions, error handling

3. CHECKPOINT DETAILS
   a) Checkpoint available: displays last checkpoint timestamp (relative + absolute), preserved data summary ("5 stages completed, 12 fragments, 3 artifacts created")
   b) No checkpoint available: message "No checkpoint available -- full restart required", "Resume" button disabled (grayed out with tooltip), "Retry" button remains enabled
   c) Checkpoint data details expandable: click to see specific stage outcomes and artifact IDs
   d) Log: checkpoint presence, displayed details, expand/collapse interactions

4. SSE EVENT INTEGRATION
   a) Simulate run_interrupted SSE event: banner appears automatically without page refresh
   b) Simulate run_recovered SSE event after user action: banner dismisses automatically
   c) Simulate run_failed SSE event after abandon: banner updates to show "Run abandoned" briefly, then dismisses
   d) SSE disconnect during recovery action: banner persists, shows "Connection lost -- status may be stale" warning
   e) SSE reconnect: stale warning clears, state refreshed from API
   f) Multiple rapid SSE events: UI handles without flickering or race conditions
   g) Log: SSE events simulated, UI response timing, state transitions

5. KEYBOARD ACCESSIBILITY
   a) When banner appears, focus moves to banner heading (not stealing focus from active work)
   b) Tab order within banner: heading -> checkpoint details -> Resume -> Retry -> Abandon
   c) Enter/Space activates all buttons
   d) Confirmation dialog: focus trapped within dialog, Tab cycles through Cancel/Confirm, Escape cancels
   e) After action completes: focus returns to dashboard content, not lost
   f) Screen reader: banner has role="alert" or aria-live="polite" for announcement
   g) Log: focus management events, tab sequence, dialog behavior

6. EDGE CASES
   a) Banner appears during active user interaction (typing in another form): no focus steal, banner appears but does not interrupt
   b) Very old interrupted run (days ago): timestamp displays correctly, no stale-data issues
   c) Interrupted run for a deleted/archived project: handled gracefully (message or filtered out)
   d) Log: edge case handling

LOGGING: Use Vitest custom reporter from od4.14. Each test logs: component state, simulated events, user interactions, API calls, focus management. Failed tests include DOM snapshot and event timeline.
